**Occupy Movement Protests Continue Across the Country**

The Occupy movement, a nationwide protest against economic inequality and the excesses of the US financial system, continues to unfold with protests in Philadelphia, Los Angeles, and other cities. Despite a 5 p.m. Sunday deadline set by Philadelphia officials for Occupy protesters to move from a similar encampment, many tents and other structures stayed put, and police sources said authorities were hoping the rest of the protesters would relocate voluntarily and that no major actions were expected before Tuesday.

In Los Angeles, police in riot gear closed in before dawn on Monday on anti-Wall Street activists who defied a midnight deadline to vacate a camp outside City Hall, but stopped short of clearing the encampment. Four demonstrators were arrested during a brief confrontation, accused of being present at an unlawful assembly. Later, attorneys for Occupy LA asked a federal judge for an injunction barring police from evicting the camp, arguing that Mayor Antonio Villaraigosa and police chief Charlie Beck had violated their civil rights by ordering it dismantled.

The Los Angeles encampment, which officials had tolerated for weeks even as other cities moved in to clear out similar camps, is among the largest on the West Coast aligned with a 2-month-old national Occupy Wall Street movement. The camp had grown to roughly 400 tents and 700 to 800 people, with at least a third of campers believed to be homeless. By Sunday night, the size of the crowd outside City Hall swelled further as supporters from organized labor, clergy, civil rights, and other groups streamed into the area, answering a call for an 11th-hour show of support.

In Philadelphia, about 100 Occupy Philly protesters sat down on the cold concrete of Dilworth Plaza at 5 p.m. Sunday and waited to be rousted for violating a deadline Mayor Nutter had set for the group to leave. However, the expected police eviction had not happened by late Sunday evening, and city officials continued to avoid saying when, or whether, they would throw the Occupiers and their tents off City Hall's so-called front lawn. Police had no plans to evict anyone, Chief Inspector Joseph Sullivan said about 6:30 p.m., and cautioned that protesters would be prevented from setting up another camp elsewhere in the city unless they got a permit.

The city has said it needs to erect fencing this week for work at Dilworth Plaza, including renovation of the SEPTA tunnels and the addition of grass, a cafe, stage, and winter ice r
details.ground_truth: The Occupy LA encampment got a stay of execution this morning—though a few arrests have been reported beyond the boundaries of City Hall Park. Two hours after protesters defied city hall's order to vacate the park by 12:01am, lawyer and Occupy supporter Jim Lafferty told Reuters he expected police to give the camp a two-day reprieve. At around 5am, police told protesters over a loudspeaker that they didn't intend to clear the park, the LA Times reports. Police do, however, intend to clear protesters out of the street, and several people who refused to move out of an intersection have already been arrested. "We definitely need to get morning traffic in," a police commander explained. Everything also seems calm at Occupy Philadelphia, where protesters defied a 5pm Sunday deadline to disperse. But as of last night, a police inspector said they didn't intend to forcibly evict anyone. "We look forward to working with Occupy Philadelphia and a resolution of the problem," he told the Philadelphia Inquirer. "Confrontation is never good."

**Mikhail Kalashnikov's Legacy and Spiritual Crisis**

Mikhail Kalashnikov, the inventor of the legendary AK-47 assault rifle, left behind a complex and intriguing legacy before his death in December 2023 (Note: The year of his death is not mentioned in the passage, but it is known to be 2013). The 94-year-old designer expressed his fears of being morally responsible for the people killed by his weapon in a lengthy emotional letter to the head of the Russian Orthodox Church, Patriarch Kirill.

The letter, published by the pro-Kremlin newspaper Izvestia, reveals Kalashnikov's spiritual crisis and his struggle to come to terms with the devastating impact of his invention. He wrote, "My spiritual pain is unbearable. I keep having the same unsolved question: if my rifle took away people's lives, then can it be that I... am guilty for people's deaths, even if they were enemies?" The letter is typed on Kalashnikov's personal writing paper and is signed with a wavering hand by the man who describes himself as "a slave of God, the designer Mikhail Kalashnikov."

Kalashnikov's design of the AK-47 was born out of his experience of the Red Army's dire lack of weapons during World War II. The rifle's comparative simplicity made it cheap to manufacture, reliable, and easy to maintain, leading to its widespread adoption and manufacture around the world. However, its use by terrorists, child soldiers, and other criminal elements has brought shame and sadness to Kalashnikov.

The Russian Orthodox Church has taken a nuanced stance on Kalashnikov's legacy, with the Patriarch's press secretary, Alexander Volkov, stating that the Church supports both the creators and the soldiers who use the weapon when it serves to protect the Fatherland. Kalashnikov's funeral was attended by President Vladimir Putin, and he received many Russian state honors, including the Order of Lenin and the Hero of Socialist Labour.

Kalashnikov's daughter, Yelena, has also spoken out about her father's spiritual crisis, stating that he did not live strictly according to the commandments, but rather came to terms with his faith later in life, first going into a church at the age of 91 and being later baptised.

In conclusion, Mikhail Kalashnikov's legacy is a complex and multifaceted one, marked by both innovation and devastation. His spiritual crisis and expression of guilt in his letter to the Russian
details.ground_truth: In our own obituary of AK-47 inventor Mikhail Kalashnikov last month, we included this quote: The rifle was invented "for the protection of the Motherland," he said. "I have no regrets and bear no responsibility for how politicians have used it." It turns out "no regrets" might not have exactly been true. The AFP picks up a report from Russia's Izvestia that Kalashnikov penned a letter to the head of the Russian Orthodox Church nine months before his December death in which he wrote of "unbearable ... spiritual pain" and asked about his culpability. It contains this line: "I keep having the same unsolved question: if my rifle took away people's lives, then can it be that I ... am guilty for people's deaths, even if they were enemies?" AFP notes that Izvestia ran a copy of the letter, which is typed on Kalashnikov's personal stationery and signed "with a wavering hand." A rep for Patriarch Kirill confirms that the letter was sent, and says the church leader wrote a reply—one that very may well have calmed his fears. Says the rep, "The Church has a very definite position: when weapons serve to protect the Fatherland, the Church supports both its creators and the soldiers who use it." But the BBC notes that it's not clear whether every word came from Kalashnikov: Izvestia reports that his daughter believes a priest helped him write the letter.

**Deadly LAX Shooting: Investigation and Aftermath**

On November 1, 2013, a gunman opened fire at Los Angeles International Airport (LAX), killing Transportation Security Administration (TSA) officer Gerardo Hernandez and wounding three others. The suspect, 23-year-old Paul Ciancia, was shot by airport police officers four times and taken into custody.

**Investigation and Coordination Issues**

An investigation into the shooting is ongoing, with officials examining the conversations between police and fire commanders to determine when it was safe enough to enter the terminal and provide medical attention to Hernandez. The investigation has raised the possibility that a lack of coordination between police and fire officials prevented speedy treatment for Hernandez and other victims. TSA workers at LAX have expressed similar concerns, citing a lack of coordination between entities at the airport.

**Medical Response and Treatment**

Hernandez was left bleeding for 33 minutes before being wheeled out by police to an ambulance. Trauma surgeon David Plurad said Hernandez had no signs of life when he arrived at Harbor-UCLA Medical Center, but doctors worked for an hour to revive him despite significant blood loss. Dr. Lawrence E. Heiskell, an emergency physician and reserve police officer, emphasized the importance of implementing lifesaving skills immediately in the event of a shooting.

**Changes in Response Protocols**

In the aftermath of the 1999 Columbine school massacre, police response protocols have changed to prioritize stopping the shooter as quickly as possible. Officers are trained to step over the wounded and stop the gunman first, then tend to victims. However, a recent audit by the Los Angeles Police Commission found that the LAPD had a zero percent compliance rate in providing first aid and CPR training to officers.

**Update on Suspect's Condition**

On Tuesday, Ronald Reagan UCLA Medical Center announced that Paul Ciancia's condition had been upgraded from critical to fair. Ciancia is charged with killing Hernandez and wounding three others, carrying a potential death sentence.

**Memorial Service for Hernandez**

A public memorial service was held on the same day as the update on Ciancia's condition, honoring Gerardo Hernandez, the first TSA officer killed in the line of duty.
details.ground_truth: Unnamed officials say TSA officer Gerardo Hernandez lay bleeding for 33 minutes at LAX before being taken to an ambulance. For 28 of those minutes, alleged shooter Paul Ciancia was in police custody, though officers hadn't yet declared the area safe to enter. Officers checked on Hernandez, who was just 20 feet from an exit, then moved on while paramedics waited 150 yards away, the AP reports, noting it's not clear if immediate attention might have saved Hernandez's life. Now under review: the conversations took place between police and fire commanders to determine when it was safe enough to enter, and whether paramedics could have gone into the terminal earlier. While an officer who checked on Hernandez about 5 minutes after he was shot told numerous officers he was dead, the AP reports that it's not known whether that officer had the training needed to make that determination; no first-aid was administered. "I basically think there's a lack of coordination between entities at this airport. That lack of coordination may have led to something that shouldn't have happened," an LAX employee says. "We may be talking about Officer Hernandez as a survivor." The results of the investigation could be months away, the AP adds. Ciancia was on Tuesday upgraded from critical to fair condition at UCLA Medical Center.

**Egypt's Ousted President Morsi Meets with EU Diplomat, Amid Ongoing Crisis**

Cairo, Egypt - In a significant development, Egyptian ousted President Mohamed Morsi met with European Union foreign policy chief Catherine Ashton on Monday night, marking his first contact with an independent official since his ouster nearly a month ago. The two-hour meeting, which took place at an undisclosed location, was described as a "friendly and open and very frank discussion" by Ashton.

According to Ashton, Morsi is in good condition and has access to newspapers and television. The meeting was seen as a signal that Egypt's military may be willing to work with Morsi towards a political solution to the country's ongoing crisis, which has spawned repeated violence between security forces and the ousted president's supporters.

The crisis has claimed the lives of at least 80 pro-Morsi demonstrators and a police officer, according to the Health Ministry. Ashton's visit to Egypt appeared to have calmed the tense capital, but Morsi's supporters rallied in Cairo on Tuesday night and marched towards the military intelligence headquarters, although no violence had been reported by early Wednesday.

Ashton met with a wide range of Egyptian political figures, including Gen. Abdel Fatah al-Sissi, the commander of the armed forces, and representatives of the Muslim Brotherhood. She emphasized that the EU is there to help and not impose, and that the people of Egypt will determine their own future.

The Muslim Brotherhood's Freedom and Justice Party, from which Morsi hails, told Ashton that any political solution for Egypt must be based on "the return of the president," and that the demonstrations in support of Morsi will not stop until "constitutional legitimacy" is restored.

Meanwhile, U.S. Defense Secretary Chuck Hagel spoke by telephone with Sissi, urging "restraint" in dealing with protesters and calling for "an inclusive reconciliation process."

The situation in Egypt remains fluid and uncertain, with the country's interim government and the Brotherhood-backed leaders locked in a bitter struggle for power.
details.ground_truth: Under circumstances that sound straight out of a Le Carre novel, Mohamed Morsi has met with an outsider for the first time since being dumped by Egypt's military, holding a midnight meeting with the European Union's top diplomat at an "undisclosed location" last night, the Wall Street Journal. Though many have feared for Morsi's well-being after almost a month in captivity, Catherine Ashton, the EU's foreign affairs and security policy chief, reports that he is well and has access to TV and newspapers, Reuters reports. The pair had a "very long and in-depth conversation," Ashton says. Ashton, who has met with several other Egyptian political figures while in the country, says she wasn't there to help Morsi break out, but rather to facilitate discussions toward a peaceful end to the country's political unrest, the Washington Post reports. "First of all, we are here to help. We are not here to impose," she says. "The people of Egypt will determine their own future." But will Morsi play ball? "He's a very proud man," says a source close to the former leader, per the Journal. "I think the idea of doing anything on (the) army's terms will not work with him."

**Global Crisis Escalates: Syria Tensions and Military Readiness**

The world is on high alert as the crisis in Syria deepens, with the US Navy's regional commander postponing a scheduled port call in Naples, Italy, to keep a destroyer with Tomahawk cruise missiles in striking distance of Syria. This move suggests that the US is preparing for a potential military strike against the Syrian government, which has been accused of using chemical weapons against its own citizens.

The Russian government, however, has called on President Bashar al-Assad to allow United Nations investigators to access the site of the alleged chemical attack, but this move is seen as a ploy to deflect blame and shift the focus to the Syrian opposition. Russia's foreign ministry has accused the rebels of being behind the attack, citing reports of criticism against government troops posted on the internet hours before the attack.

In a bizarre twist, Russia's foreign ministry spokesman, Aleksandr K. Lukashevich, may have been confused by YouTube's time-stamping practice, which led him to suggest that the attack was a previously planned action by the rebels. Lukashevich has also praised the Assad government for welcoming a UN commission member who suggested that the rebels had used chemical weapons, and accused the Syrian opposition of not cooperating with the investigation.

Meanwhile, CBS News has reported that American intelligence agencies detected activity at locations known to be chemical weapons sites before the attack, which may have been preparations for the assault. The Syrian government has not commented on the allegations.

The situation in Syria is rapidly escalating, with the US and Russia taking opposing sides in the conflict. The world is holding its breath as the situation unfolds, and the possibility of a military strike against the Syrian government looms large.
details.ground_truth: A government backed by Russia is accused of killing its own civilians. That applies not only to the current situation in Syria but to the 1999 situation in Kosovo. And as the New York Times reports, top US officials are keeping Kosovo very much in mind as they weigh their options. President Obama would likely never get the approval of the UN Security Council for a military response because of Russia's veto power. When Bill Clinton was in the same boat in 1999, he turned to NATO and got its blessing for 78 days of airstrikes. “It’s a step too far to say we’re drawing up legal justifications for an action, given that the president hasn’t made a decision,” a government official tells the Times. “But Kosovo, of course, is a precedent of something that is perhaps similar.” Obama was meeting today with national security aides to determine next steps if the US concludes that Bashar al-Assad used chemical weapons. (Assad in turn accused the rebels of doing the same today.) Meanwhile, the US Navy is keeping two destroyers positioned in the Mediterranean in case the green light is given for a cruise-missile attack, reports the Wall Street Journal.

**Kleiner Perkins Seeks to Recover Costs from Ellen Pao in High-Profile Gender Discrimination Case**

Kleiner Perkins Caufield & Byers, a prominent Silicon Valley venture capital firm, is seeking to recover approximately $973,000 in costs from Ellen Pao, a former partner who lost a high-profile sex-discrimination case against the firm. The costs include $865,000 in expert witness fees and other expenses related to the trial.

The case, which captivated Silicon Valley and sparked a wide discussion about gender diversity in the tech industry, was decided in March when a jury cleared Kleiner Perkins of claims that it short-circuited Pao's career because she is a woman. Pao's lawyers argued that her career deteriorated after she ended a brief affair with a partner and that the firm retaliated against her in a climate that was unfriendly toward women.

Kleiner Perkins has offered to waive its costs request if Pao chooses not to appeal, citing that women in technology would be best served by focusing on making progress on gender diversity issues outside of continued litigation. Pao's legal team is considering the proposal and will respond in the next two weeks.

The case has drawn attention to the issue of gender diversity in the tech industry, with some experts suggesting that Kleiner Perkins' offer to waive its costs request is a common practice when defendants prevail in employment lawsuits. However, others have criticized the firm for sounding punitive and not taking a more conciliatory approach.

In related news, Kleiner Perkins has also filed a new deal with Pao, offering to forgive and forget if she agrees not to pursue the case any further or pays the firm $1 million. The offer was made in papers filed in California Superior Court and includes a statement from a Kleiner spokeswoman that women in technology would be best served by focusing on making progress on gender diversity issues outside of continued litigation.

Overall, the case has highlighted the challenges and biases faced by women in the tech industry and the need for greater diversity and inclusion in the sector.
details.ground_truth: Even though Ellen Pao lost her gender-discrimination suit against a former employer, she's still widely hailed as a hero for bringing the boys' club atmosphere of Silicon Valley under a microscope. But now she's got a tough choice on her hands: The venture-capital firm she sued, Kleiner Perkins Caufield & Byers, says she owes almost $1 million in legal fees—but it will drop its pursuit of the money if she doesn't appeal last month's ruling, reports the Wall Street Journal. Pao's attorneys say they will have a decision in a few weeks. Kleiner Perkins says it offered Pao about $1 million as a settlement before the trial began, but received no response from her legal team. Because of that offer, the company can now go after Pao for expensive witness fees, explains the New York Times. One factor that will surely weigh on the decision: A review of previous cases suggests that Pao has only a slim chance of winning on appeal, reports Reuters.

**Cold Case Conviction Overturned: 76-Year-Old Man Released from Prison**

In a stunning turn of events, a 76-year-old man from Washington state, Jack McCullough, has been released from prison after a judge ordered a new trial in the 1957 abduction and killing of 7-year-old Maria Ridulph in Sycamore, Illinois. McCullough, who was serving a life sentence, was convicted in 2012 in one of the oldest unsolved U.S. cases ever to go to trial.

The decision to release McCullough was made by Judge William P. Brady, who pointed to several factors, including newly discovered phone records that support McCullough's long-held contention that he was about 40 miles away from Sycamore when Maria was abducted. The records show that McCullough made a collect call to his parents at 6:57 p.m. from a phone booth in downtown Rockford, which is 40 miles northwest of where Maria was abducted between 6:45 p.m. and 6:55 p.m.

DeKalb County State's Attorney Richard Schmack, who conducted a six-month review of the evidence, concluded that McCullough could not have killed Maria. Schmack's report, which was filed with the court last month, appeared to pick the case apart, point-by-point, and found that there was no physical evidence tying McCullough to the crime.

McCullough's alibi was that he was in Rockford, attempting to enlist with the U.S. Air Force at a military recruiting station, on the night Maria disappeared. Schmack's review of police reports and hundreds of other documents, including from the Air Force recruitment office, which had been improperly barred at trial, found that they contained "a wealth of information pointing to McCullough's innocence, and absolutely nothing showing guilt."

The case has been a source of pain and trauma for Maria's family, who remain convinced that McCullough is guilty and have sought the appointment of a special prosecutor to keep him behind bars. Maria's brother, Charles Ridulph, 70, has said in recent weeks that his family feels let down by the state prosecutor's office about-face.

The release of McCullough has reopened old wounds and raised questions about the justice system and the handling of cold cases.
details.ground_truth: A man convicted four years ago in the oldest cold case ever tried was freed Friday after an Illinois judge vacated his conviction and subsequent life sentence, CNN reports. According to CBS News, 76-year-old Jack McCullough was convicted of kidnapping a 7-year-old girl, choking her, and stabbing her to death in 1957. The case was reopened in 2008, and McCullough was arrested in 2011 and convicted the following year. After an appeal by McCullough, Illinois state's attorney Richard Schmack launched a six-month investigation that found what he calls "clear and convincing evidence" that McCullough is innocent. While the judge Friday vacated McCullough's conviction, he stopped short of declaring him innocent, and a new trial will be held, the Chicago Tribune reports. McCullough, who lived in the same neighborhood as the kidnapped girl, says he was 40 miles away at an Air Force recruiting center at the time of the kidnapping. It's an alibi that passed a polygraph test in 1957 and made it impossible for him to be the culprit based on the FBI's original timeline for the kidnapping. That timeline was later changed by police, and Schmack says documents—including phone and Air Force records—supporting McCullough's alibi were wrongly not allowed at his trial. A friend of the kidnapped girl picked McCullough out of a photo lineup when the case was reopened. But his was the only non-yearbook photo in the lineup, and she picked a different man out of a photo lineup 50 years earlier. That info wasn't allowed at McCullough's trial either. No physical evidence was ever found to support McCullough's conviction.

**Rush Limbaugh Advertiser Exodus Continues: Saturday Night Live Parodies the Fallout**

In the wake of his highly publicized and misogynistic attacks on Georgetown student Sandra Fluke, Rush Limbaugh's radio show has continued to lose advertisers. According to a memo obtained by Radio-Info.com, 98 advertisers have requested that their commercials not be aired on Limbaugh's show or other programs deemed to be "offensive or controversial." The list of advertisers includes major carmakers (Ford, GM, Toyota), insurance companies (Allstate, Geico, Prudential, State Farm), and restaurants (McDonald's, Subway).

The memo specifically asks radio stations to schedule commercials in dayparts or programs free of content that may stir negative sentiment from a small percentage of the listening public, naming Limbaugh, Mark Levin, Tom Leykis, Michael Savage, Glenn Beck, and Sean Hannity as examples of such content.

As a result of the advertiser exodus, the online feed at WABC, Limbaugh's flagship station, has increasingly turned to free public service announcements during his show. The fallout has also been parodied on Saturday Night Live, with Taran Killam playing the cranky radio host in a sketch that pokes fun at the bizarre sponsors of Limbaugh's show, including "Depends For Racists." While the sketch was amusing, it ultimately felt like a repeat of the same joke, making it feel longer than Limbaugh's actual show.

The situation has highlighted the growing power of social media and the public's ability to hold advertisers accountable for their support of controversial content. As the advertiser exodus continues, it remains to be seen whether Limbaugh's show will be able to recover from the damage.
details.ground_truth: Rush Limbaugh may be bleeding sponsors—one count now has the number fleeing his show up to 98—because of his "slut" comment, but Saturday Night Live had some suggestions last night about who might fill the advertising void. A few of the highlights, as per Mediaite: Misaki Dolphin Poppers: "Start your day off right, with bits of dolphin." Depends for Racists: "If you pee a little every time you see a Mexican, you need Depends for Racists." Schoder's fake rape whistles: "Help is not on the way." Syria Tourism Board: Because, "Ah! No! There's nowhere to hide!"

**Summary of Recent News Headlines**

**Montana Congressman-Elect Greg Gianforte Pleads Guilty to Misdemeanor Assault**

In a recent development, Montana congressman-elect Greg Gianforte pleaded guilty to misdemeanor assault after being accused of "body slamming" a reporter during a campaign event last year. Gianforte was sentenced to a 180-day deferred sentence, 40 hours of community service, 20 hours of anger management, and a $300 fine along with an $85 court fee. The incident was caught on audiotape and sparked widespread outrage.

**President Trump's Comments on Gianforte's Assault Spark Controversy**

President Trump sparked controversy when he made light of Rep. Greg Gianforte's physical assault on a reporter at a Montana rally. Trump said that Gianforte was a "tough cookie" and praised him for his actions, saying "Any guy that can do a body slam, he is my type!" The White House Correspondents' Association condemned the president's comments, saying that they amount to the celebration of a crime and an attack on the First Amendment.

**Montana Senate Race Heats Up as President Trump Visits the State**

The Montana Senate race is heating up as President Trump visits the state to campaign for Republican candidate Matt Rosendale. Trump has made it his mission to unseat Democratic Senator Jon Tester, who he blames for the failed nomination of White House physician Adm. Ronny Jackson. Tester has touted a bipartisan record in the Senate and is only a few points ahead of Rosendale in recent polls.

**Twitter's Location Feature: A New Way to Share Your Location**

Twitter has introduced a new feature that allows users to add location information to their tweets, such as their city or precise location. Users can also delete their tweet location history at any time. This feature is designed to make it easier for users to share their location and connect with others in their area.

**Note:** There are three duplicate passages (Passage 2, Passage 3, and Passage 5) which are not included in the summary.
details.ground_truth: "Any guy that can do a body-slam, he's my guy," President Trump shouted to a cheering Montana audience Thursday night as he simulated slamming someone to the ground, then noting, "I shouldn't say that." Trump was discussing Rep. Greg Gianforte, who assaulted Guardian reporter Ben Jacobs last year during the special election for the House seat vacated by Ryan Zinke, a misdemeanor assault to which he pleaded guilty and completed community service and an anger management class. Trump said after the incident, he feared Gianforte had blown his chance at the congressional seat. "I said, 'Oh, this is terrible, he's gonna lose the election,'" Trump recounted. "Then I said, 'Well, wait a minute—I know Montana pretty well. I think it might help him.' And it did." Trump was in Big Sky Country stumping for Matt Rosendale in his race for Democrat Jon Tester's Senate seat. Reaction came quickly: John Mulholland, US editor of the Guardian, put out a statement after the rally blasting Trump's words as "an attack on the First Amendment," asking for an apology and adding, "In the aftermath of the murder of Jamal Khashoggi, it runs the risk of inviting other assaults on journalists both here and across the world." ABC News reports on what the Toronto Star's Daniel Dale calls the "most significant" and "truly horrible" moment in the rally: "The president is gleefully applauding violence against a journalist." Trump also praised Gianforte as "smart" and then advised the crowd to "never wrestle him." (Trump seems to acknowledge Khashoggi is dead.)

**Cigarette Smoke Fuels the Rise of Superbugs: A Growing Concern for Public Health**

Recent research has shed light on a disturbing connection between cigarette smoke and the virulent rampage of superbugs, specifically MRSA (Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus). Studies conducted by researchers at the University of California, San Diego School of Medicine have found that cigarette smoke exposure stresses MRSA, causing it to alter its cell walls to repel toxic substances and enhance its survival against immune responses.

According to the studies, MRSA exposed to cigarette smoke extract becomes four times more resistant to macrophage killing than non-exposed MRSA. Additionally, the increased resistance continues for 24 hours after exposure, suggesting it could be inherited. This is a concerning finding, as MRSA is a type of bacteria that causes life-threatening infections to the skin, bloodstream, or a surgical site, and sometimes causes pneumonia.

The studies also found that cigarette smoke-exposed MRSA becomes more resistant to antimicrobial peptides, which pierce bacterial cell walls. Furthermore, the smoke-exposed MRSA becomes better at invading human cells in lab cultures, and when put into mice, there was a significant difference in mortality compared to MRSA that had not been smoke-exposed.

These findings suggest that cigarette smoke not only harms smokers but also assists pathogens, making them more resistant to immune responses. This is a growing concern for public health, as the rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria is a major public health issue. The studies' findings provide new evidence that cigarette smoke-induced resistance in MRSA may be an additional contributing factor to the rise of superbugs.

The researchers hope that their findings will add yet another reason to why individuals should refrain from smoking. The study was funded in part by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs and involved a team of researchers from the University of California, San Diego, and the Veterans Affairs San Diego Healthcare System.

**Key Takeaways:**

* Cigarette smoke exposure stresses MRSA, causing it to alter its cell walls to repel toxic substances and enhance its survival against immune responses.
* MRSA exposed to cigarette smoke extract becomes four times more resistant to macrophage killing than non-exposed MRSA.
* The increased resistance continues for 24 hours after exposure, suggesting it could be inherited.
* Cigarette smoke-exposed MRSA becomes more resistant to antimicrobial peptides and becomes better at invading human cells in lab cultures.
* The findings suggest that cigarette smoke not only harms smokers but also assists pathogens, making them more
details.ground_truth: Superbugs such as MRSA are already bad enough. But it turns out that something can make them even nastier: cigarette smoke. Researchers found that antibiotic-resistant bacteria exposed to the smoke became more aggressive and far more difficult to kill, they explain at Eureka Alert. It's almost as if the smoke sets off an alarm that causes the bacteria to strengthen its defenses, explains the Union-Tribune of San Diego. Scientists made the discovery by exposing immune cells to two different batches of MRSA, one normal and one treated with cigarette smoke extract. The bigger the dose of smoke, the bigger the effect, notes Medical Daily. "We already know that smoking cigarettes harms human respiratory and immune cells, and now we've shown that, on the flipside, smoke can also stress out invasive bacteria and make them more aggressive," says lead author Laura Crotty Alexander of UC San Diego. It's a double-whammy: Smokers already have weakened immune systems that make them more susceptible to infectious diseases, and the smoke they inhale seems to make invading pathogens even more dangerous. In this study, the smoke helped MRSA survive longer and kill more mice with pneumonia. (The latest antibiotic-resistant bug to make headlines is a nasty stomach virus.)

**Renowned Hairdresser Fabio Sementilli Found Dead in Woodland Hills Home**

On January 23, 2017, internationally known hairdresser and beauty company executive Fabio Sementilli was found dead in his Los Angeles home. The 49-year-old Canadian-born Sementilli was discovered by his wife and daughter on the patio of a gated house in the upscale Woodland Hills neighborhood. He had suffered multiple stab wounds to his neck and upper torso and was bleeding profusely at the scene.

**Investigation Underway**

Homicide detectives are investigating the death of Sementilli, who was Vice President of Education for Wella, a major German hair-care company. The police are trying to determine if the attack was random or targeted, and they are reviewing security footage from the neighborhood to identify any potential suspects.

**Tributes Pour in for Sementilli**

Sementilli was remembered as a mentor, a positive role model, and a true leader in the hair styling industry. His colleagues and friends, including Eden Sassoon, the daughter of Vidal Sassoon, paid tribute to his kind and generous spirit. Sementilli's favorite quote, "Being a leader is not about age; instead, it's all about attitude toward others," reflects his commitment to mentoring and giving back to the industry.

**Missing Porsche and No Motive**

Sementilli's black 2008 Porsche 911 Carrera with paper plates was missing from the home, and detectives have not yet identified a motive for the attack. The police are urging anyone with information about the case to contact the LAPD's Homicide Bureau at (818) 374-1925.

**A Legacy of Giving Back**

Sementilli was the heart behind Wella's Hairdressers at Heart program and was known for his hands-on approach to mentoring and educating others. His legacy of giving back to the industry will be remembered and celebrated by his colleagues and friends.

In summary, the death of Fabio Sementilli is a tragic loss for the hair styling industry, and his colleagues and friends are paying tribute to his kind and generous spirit. The investigation into his death is ongoing, and the police are reviewing security footage to identify any potential suspects.
details.ground_truth: One of the most renowned hairstylists in Los Angeles was murdered on Monday by intruders who made their getaway in his Porsche, police say. Fabio Sementilli was stabbed several times and was found on his home's patio by his wife and daughter, CBS Los Angeles reports. Paramedics were called but the 49-year-old was bleeding profusely and died at the scene. Police say the Canadian-born hairstylist's 2008 Porsche 911 Carrera was missing from the scene and they believe it was stolen by two men who broke into the upscale Woodland Hills home and attacked Sementilli. Police aren't sure whether the murder was random or a targeted attack, the AP reports. Sementilli gained international recognition during his 30 years as a hairstylist and also served as an exec at companies including Wella, the Los Angeles Daily News reports. He was "recognized as an exceptional stylist, educator, and visionary," Wella said in a statement that describes him as "an icon in the hairdressing industry." A post at Modern Salon says he was a VP of education for cosmetics company Coty, adding that he "mentored tens of thousands of hairdressers with a hands-on approach either on a one-to-one basis or on a grander scale." (This man was murdered just two months after President Obama commuted his sentence.)

**The Most Influential People in the World: A Study of Wikipedia and Beyond**

A recent study by the University of Toulouse's Young-Ho Eom has shed light on the most influential people in the world, using a unique approach that combines Google's PageRank algorithm with a new method called CheiRank. The study analyzed 24 major language editions of Wikipedia, looking for individuals with the most important articles linking to them. The results were surprising, with Frank Sinatra taking the top spot, followed by Michael Jackson and Pope Pius XII.

However, when the study took into account the number of outgoing links from an article, using the CheiRank method, the results changed. Carl Linnaeus, the 18th-century Swedish naturalist, emerged as the most influential person in the world, with his system of naming and classifying organisms leading to a vast network of links on Wikipedia.

But Linnaeus' influence is not limited to Wikipedia. A new study by the Internet Archive, a non-profit organization that aims to preserve the web, highlights the importance of archiving and preserving links on the web. The organization's crawlers are working to archive pages as they are created and the pages they refer to, in an effort to fix all broken links on the web.

The concept of influence and intellectual success has been a topic of discussion for centuries, with Sir Isaac Newton famously saying that "If I have seen further, it is by standing upon the shoulders of giants." A new study by the University of Toulouse's Young-Ho Eom has taken this concept to a new level, using a unique approach to calculate the most influential thinker in the world. The result? Carl Linnaeus, the Father of Taxonomy, who has a vast network of links on Wikipedia due to his system of naming and classifying organisms.

In contrast, a previous study by Jasleen Kaur, Filippo Radicchi, and Filippo Menczer found that Karl Marx was history's most influential scholar, based on how often academics referenced each other in their work. The two studies highlight the complexity and nuance of measuring influence and intellectual success, and the importance of considering different approaches and perspectives.

**Key Takeaways:**

* Frank Sinatra takes the top spot in a study of Wikipedia's most influential people, followed by Michael Jackson and Pope Pius XII.
* Carl Linnaeus emerges as the most influential person in the world, using a unique approach that combines PageRank and CheiRank.
* The Internet Archive is working to archive
details.ground_truth: Using methods borrowed from Google, a group of researchers has analyzed all Wikipedia pages and determined that, at least on the English language version of the site, Frank Sinatra is the world's most important person. Second place goes to Michael Jackson, and third to Pope Pius XII. When factoring in all 24 language editions of the online encyclopedia, the team found that Adolf Hitler ranked the most important person, while Michael Jackson was again second and Madonna third, reports the Guardian. "Our analysis shows that most important historical figures across Wikipedia language editions are born in western countries after the 17th century, and are male," the authors write. The researchers combined two algorithms to reach these conclusions. First, Google's PageRank algorithm, which determines a page's importance based on how many other pages link to it. In using PageRank on Wikipedia, the most important person in the world was 18th-century Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus, likely because the pages of organism after organism link back to the "father of taxonomy." So the researchers added an algorithm called CheiRank, which determines importance based on the number of outgoing links, the thought being that an important person would himself be connected to other important people, things, and events. Using the two together—an approach they dubbed 2DRank—no one beat out Frank Sinatra on English pages or Adolf Hitler across all language versions. Last year, in a separate study analyzing academic references, one team deemed Karl Marx to be the world's most important scholar, according to the Smithsonian. (Click to read about one of Sinatra's quirks.)

details.ground_truth: Arnold Schwarzenegger seems to think Donald Trump needs a lesson in making friends after the president-elect chose to criticize his Celebrity Apprentice successor. **Smart Home Devices and Privacy Concerns: A Growing Trend**

In recent times, the trend of internet-enabled devices in the home, also known as the "Internet of Things" (IoT), has been gaining momentum. However, this trend has also raised concerns about the collection and use of personal data by these devices. Two recent news stories from Samsung and Microsoft's Xbox division highlight the extent to which these devices can collect and use our personal data.

**Samsung's SmartTV and Voice Recognition**

Samsung's new privacy policy for its smart TVs allows the company and its partners to listen in on everything users say. The policy has drawn comparisons to George Orwell's dystopian fiction 1984, where the government's telescreens can monitor and record citizens' conversations. While Samsung's voice recognition software can be turned off, the company will still collect data on how users use the TV. The data collected includes information about content watched, purchased, downloaded, or streamed, as well as device information, such as IP address and device identifiers.

**Microsoft's Xbox and Always-Listening Kinect**

Microsoft's new Xbox console comes with an always-listening Kinect, which can recognize and process audio personalized to specific individuals. The Kinect can even read a user's heartbeat while exercising and recognize individual voices. While Microsoft claims that the Kinect is not recording, but rather listening, the always-listening mode has raised concerns about privacy implications, especially if the system is compromised remotely by a malicious actor.

**Common Themes and Concerns**

Both Samsung and Microsoft's devices highlight the growing trend of IoT in the home and the concerns about the collection and use of personal data. The devices can collect a wide range of data, including device information, usage patterns, and even biometric data. The always-listening mode of the Kinect and the voice recognition software of the Samsung TV raise concerns about the extent to which these devices can monitor and record our conversations.

**Conclusion**

As the trend of IoT in the home continues to grow, it is essential to be aware of the extent to which these devices can collect and use our personal data. While Samsung and Microsoft's devices highlight the benefits of these devices, such as convenience and personalized recommendations, they also raise concerns about the potential risks to our privacy and security. It is crucial for consumers to be informed and to take steps to protect their data, such as disabling the always-listening mode of the Kinect and turning off the voice recognition software of the Samsung TV.
details.ground_truth: Own a Samsung smart TV? Then take note: Your television is not only listening, it's sending your data to a third party, the Independent reports. The TV's voice-recognition software lets you give it orders, but the product's privacy policy warns that "personal or other sensitive information ... will be among the data captured and transmitted to a third party through your use of Voice Recognition." Samsung tells the BBC that the third party is Nuance, the company that does Samsung's speech-to-text conversion. But intellectual property lawyer Corynne McSherry says it would be nice to see that clarified in Samsung's policy, the Daily Beast reports. "And I’d definitely like to know whether my words were being transmitted in a secure form," she says. An activist tweets that Samsung's policy sounds a lot like the description of "telescreens" in George Orwell's dystopian novel, 1984—which reads in part, "Any sound that Winston made, above the level of a very low whisper, would be picked up by [the screen]." Unnervingly, it wasn't clear how often "the Thought Police plugged in on any individual wire was guesswork." Samsung, however, says it doesn't keep voice data, and customers can opt to turn off voice-recognition or even unplug the TV from Wi-Fi. And Samsung isn't alone: the Verge reports that XBoxes already use voice recognition while being connected to the Internet. "Samsung's privacy policy is not unique; its rhetoric just happens to be similar to that of a well-known sci-fi novel," the Verge says.

**NFL Cheerleaders' Fight for Fair Pay: A Summary of Recent Developments**

The National Football League (NFL) has been under scrutiny in recent years for its treatment of cheerleaders, with many teams facing lawsuits over allegations of low pay and poor working conditions. In this summary, we will review the latest developments in the fight for fair pay and treatment of NFL cheerleaders.

**New York Jets Cheerleaders Settle for $325,000**

In a New Jersey court, the New York Jets cheerleaders, known as the Flight Crew, reached a settlement with the team for $325,000. The deal will give each of the 52 cheerleaders $2,500 per season they worked, with an additional $400 payment for each photo shoot. The settlement covers the 2012-13 and 2013-14 NFL seasons and was initially reached in August 2015.

In a similar case, the Buffalo Bills cheerleaders, known as the Jills, have been granted class-action status in a wage suit against the team. details.ground_truth: A New Jersey court on Wednesday gave the Flight Crew—aka the New York Jets cheerleaders—something to cheer about: a $325,000 settlement of the class-action lawsuit filed in 2014 by a cheerleader identified as Krystal C., CNNMoney reports. That amounts to each of the 52 cheerleaders getting $2,500 per season worked and $400 per photo shoot. The settlement covers the 2012-13 and 2013-14 seasons. New York State Sen. Diane Savino, per the New York Post, cheered the settlement, calling on the NFL to "develop uniform rules … to ensure that all cheerleaders in every state received the employee pay and protections they deserve." NFL teams have sought to justify low pay for cheerleaders by saying they're independent contractors, CNN notes. In the case of the Flight Crew, Krystal C.'s suit claimed that cheerleaders were compensated $150 per game and $100 for special appearances. But, when you factor in practices and rehearsals, that pans out to $3.77 per hour. Throw in hair, makeup, and transportation expenses and "the hourly rate goes below $1.50 an hour," says the cheerleaders' attorney. The Flight Crew isn't alone: Cheerleaders have taken to the courts to seek higher pay from the Cincinnati Bengals (tentative agreement reached), Tampa Bay Buccaneers (settled for $825,000), Oakland Raiders (settled for $1.25 million), and the Buffalo Bills (class-action suit on the horizon).

**Mysterious Polio-like Illness, Acute Flaccid Myelitis (AFM), on the Rise in the US**

A mysterious polio-like illness, known as Acute Flaccid Myelitis (AFM), has been reported in 50 cases across 24 states in the US, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). This is more than double the number of cases reported in 2015, when 21 cases were confirmed for the whole year.

**What is AFM?**

AFM affects the nervous system, specifically the spinal cord, and can cause a range of symptoms, including:

* Limb weakness
* Facial droop
* Difficulty breathing or swallowing, or slurred speech
* These symptoms can occur after a respiratory or febrile illness, and can be severe enough to require urgent ventilator support.

**Causes of AFM**

The exact cause of AFM is still unknown, but it has been linked to viral infections, including enteroviruses, which are common and typically cause milder illnesses in children, such as respiratory infections. However, when enteroviruses get into the central nervous system, they can cause more serious illnesses like inflammation of the brain.

**Enterovirus D68 (EV-D68)**

EV-D68 is a type of enterovirus that has been linked to AFM. In 2014, a national outbreak of severe respiratory disease among children caused by EV-D68 occurred, and a cluster of cases of acute limb weakness among children in Colorado was also reported. However, the CDC has not detected traces of EV-D68 in the AFM specimens collected in 2014 and 2015.

**Prevention and Treatment**

The best prevention is to wash your hands, stay up-to-date on vaccinations, and protect yourself from mosquitoes, since the mosquito-borne West Nile virus is linked to AFM. There is no specific treatment for AFM, but neurologists may prescribe therapy on a case-by-case basis. Some doctors have reported that treatment with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) might help, but more research is needed.

**Personal Stories**

Several families have shared their personal stories of dealing with AFM. Sara Carson, whose daughter Laura developed AFM in 2014, said that it was a "nightmare you never wake up from." Laura spent 25 days in the hospital and had to use a wheelchair, but with months of rehabilitation and therapy, she is now able to run
details.ground_truth: At first, it seemed like 4-year-old Laura Carson was suffering from a simple headache. Then came rapid shallow breathing, a tremor, double vision. Within days in August 2014, she was "a limp rag doll," reports Today—but it took doctors some time to diagnose her with acute flaccid myelitis, a rare disease the CDC warns is becoming increasingly more common. Not much is known about the polio-like disease that plagues mostly children, including what causes it. What is known is that AFM affects the spinal cord and causes weak limbs or paralysis, drooping in the face, and slurred speech or difficulty breathing. About 200 kids have been diagnosed since 2014, and this year's figures are especially troubling. The CDC reports 121 cases of AFM were confirmed in 2014, but that was followed by just 21 cases in 2015, reports Fox News. From January to August of this year, however, there were 50 confirmed cases in 24 states. Among that first spike of cases in 2014, the Washington Post reports that 85% of kids recovered partially, but only three recovered fully. A CDC study found 68% of patients had a fever and 81% had a respiratory illness before AFM symptoms appeared. Some had been diagnosed with the West Nile virus, and others with an enterovirus. "August to October is typically when enteroviruses circulate" and "we see more acute flaccid myelitis during that season," a doctor tells NBC News. Washing your hands and covering your mouth when coughing or sneezing are among the ways suggested to help. (Four siblings suffer from a mysterious disease.)

**The Shroud of Turin: A Forensic Investigation Raises Questions About its Authenticity**

The Shroud of Turin, a piece of cloth bearing the image of a crucified man, has long been considered by some to be the burial cloth of Jesus Christ. However, a recent forensic investigation has raised questions about its authenticity, suggesting that it may be a medieval fake.

**Bloodstain Pattern Analysis**

The investigation, conducted by forensic scientists Matteo Borrini and Luigi Garlaschelli, used a technique called Bloodstain Pattern Analysis to examine the bloodstains on the shroud. They created a model of a human body and pumped blood onto it at the points where the stains were located on the shroud. By comparing the angle of the bloodstains on the model with the direction of the stains on the shroud, they found that the bloodstains were inconsistent with any one pose, suggesting that a standing model was used to imprint the patterns at different angles for the hands, chest, and back.

**Inconsistent Bloodstain Patterns**

The researchers found that the bloodstains on the shroud were inconsistent with each other, with some hand bloodstains tracing angles at odds with each other by as much as 10 degrees. They also found that a simulated spear wound in the chest ran in completely different patterns than the one on the shroud, whether standing or prone. Borrini said, "This is just not what happens to a person on a cross."

**Radiocarbon Dating and Other Evidence**

While the forensic investigation raises questions about the shroud's authenticity, it is not the only evidence that suggests it may be a fake. Radiocarbon dating has determined its origin to be between 1260 and 1390, which is consistent with the Middle Ages. However, the researchers also found that the weave and the fabric of the shroud are consistent with the Jesus of Nazareth time frame, as well as the Middle Ages.

**Conclusion**

The Shroud of Turin remains a fascinating and enigmatic mystery, and the forensic investigation raises more questions than it answers. While it is possible that the shroud is a genuine relic, the evidence that suggests it may be a medieval fake is compelling. Ultimately, the question of the shroud's authenticity will remain a topic of debate and study for generations to come.
details.ground_truth: The Shroud of Turin is supposedly the burial cloth that was wrapped around Jesus after his crucifixion; bloodstains on the linen shroud, which are said to have been transferred to it during the three days Jesus was in the tomb, form the image of a crucified man. But a new study reported in the Journal of Forensic Sciences finds the bloodstain image was likely faked. Researchers looking at the blood spatter found that the stains appeared to come from someone standing up, rather than someone who was flat on the fabric, Science Alert reports. As the researchers put it, the stains are "totally unrealistic" when compared to what they should look like. The shroud, which is held in the Cathedral of St. John the Baptist in Italy, is considered an icon, as opposed to a genuine religious relic, by the Vatican; Fox News notes "the church has never weighed in on its authenticity." "This is the kind of forensic work done all the time in police investigations," the forensic scientist who conducted the analysis tells BuzzFeed News. "Even a crucified or hanging person should leave a distinct blood pattern on the cloth, which would be fascinating information to have." The study found inconsistent staining, with researchers concluding multiple poses were used to create the bloodstains—a standing model was likely used to imprint patterns on the cloth at various angles for various body parts. Another bloodstain pattern expert notes that more research could be done to see whether cleaning a body or preparing it for burial might account for the inconsistent staining, though he notes that the stains do appear to have come from flowing blood, meaning a heart that was beating at the time the stains were made. (See previous stories in the real-or-fake debate here.)

**Global News Update: Singapore Summit and Beyond**

In a historic and unprecedented move, President Donald Trump and North Korean leader Kim Jong Un met in Singapore for a summit aimed at denuclearizing the Korean Peninsula. The meeting, which concluded on Tuesday, saw both leaders engage in a series of high-stakes talks and symbolic gestures.

**Denuclearization and Economic Incentives**

According to Trump, North Korea has agreed to relinquish its nuclear weapons and is prepared to push for a lasting peace and complete denuclearization of the Korean Peninsula. The U.S. delegation, led by Trump, used economic development as an incentive to encourage North Korea to enter denuclearization talks. Trump showcased a video simulation of possible projects that could take place in North Korea, including the construction of the "best hotel in the world" on its coastline.

**Symbolic Gestures and a Glimpse into the Presidential Limousine**

In a lighthearted moment, Trump offered Kim a rare glimpse inside the presidential limousine known as "The Beast." The two leaders were spotted chatting and laughing as Kim peeked inside the car. This symbolic gesture was seen as a way for Trump to showcase his power and status as the leader of the United States.

**The Internet Archive's Effort to Fix Broken Links**

In a separate development, the Internet Archive is working to archive and preserve web pages as they are created, with the goal of fixing all broken links on the web. The organization is using crawls of supported "No More 404" sites to achieve this goal, and hopes that references to archived pages will be used in place of broken links.

Overall, the Singapore summit and its aftermath have marked a significant shift in the global landscape, with both leaders engaging in a series of high-stakes talks and symbolic gestures. As the world watches, it remains to be seen what the future holds for North Korea and the Korean Peninsula.
details.ground_truth: You can temporarily take Donald Trump out of real estate, but you can't take the real estate out of Donald Trump. That much was clear at a Singapore press conference Tuesday, which took place after the president's meeting with Kim Jong Un and in which he touched upon the potential of North Korea's coastline. "They have great beaches," Trump noted, per the Washington Examiner. "You see that whenever they're exploding their cannons into the ocean." And that spectacular view got him to thinking: "Wouldn't that make a great condo? Instead of doing that you could have the best hotels in the world right there. Think of it from a real estate perspective, you have South Korea, you have China, and they own the land in the middle, how bad is that, right? It's great." The AP also reports on another post-summit activity, which it deems a "classic alpha male move": Trump showing off "The Beast," the presidential limousine, to the North Korean leader. CNN shows a video clip of the two men approaching the limo, a Secret Service agent opening the door, and Kim peering in to take a look. "We know that these men were trying to find ways to bond, in just like a personal, human way, and what's more natural for guys [than] to want to check out one another's car," Chris Cuomo notes. (Here are seven complimentary things Trump said about Kim after the summit.)

**Historic Moment: Congressional Gold Medal Awarded to Nisei Veterans**

In a significant step towards acknowledging the contributions and sacrifices of a marginalized community, President Barack Obama has signed a bill authorizing the Congressional Gold Medal to be awarded to the Nisei veterans who served in the 442nd Regimental Combat Team and the 100th Infantry Battalion during World War II. The Nisei, or Japanese Americans born to immigrant parents, were subjected to internment camps while their families fought for the United States overseas.

The 442nd Regimental Combat Team, composed of Americans of Japanese ancestry who volunteered to fight, is the most decorated Army unit of its size and length of service in the history of the United States. The Military Intelligence Service, which provided valuable language and cultural knowledge, also played a crucial role in the U.S. victory in the Pacific.

The Congressional Gold Medal is one of the highest civilian honors presented to people who serve the security and national interests of the United States. Past honorees include the Wright Brothers, Rosa Parks, Navajo Code Talkers, the Tuskegee Airmen, and the Dalai Lama.

The awarding of the Congressional Gold Medal to the Nisei veterans is a testament to their bravery, sacrifice, and contributions to the United States. It is also a significant step towards acknowledging the historical injustices faced by the Japanese American community and promoting a more tolerant and just nation.

**Key Statistics:**

- Over 6,000 Japanese-Americans born of immigrant parents will be recognized for their service.
- The 442nd Regimental Combat Team is the most decorated Army unit of its size and length of service in the history of the United States.
- The Military Intelligence Service played a crucial role in the U.S. victory in the Pacific.

**Key Quotes:**

- "While some Japanese-Americans were being wrongly interned due only to their ethnicity, these brave men stepped forward to defend our nation," said Sen. Daniel K. Akaka (D-Hawaii).
- "Their bravery helped to not only win the war, it paved the way towards a more tolerant and just nation," said Sen. Daniel K. Akaka (D-Hawaii).
- "I felt it was my duty. I was born and raised in America," said Art Iwasaki, a veteran of the 442nd Regimental Combat Team.
details.ground_truth: President Obama today signed legislation to honor Japanese-American soldiers who fought on behalf of the Allies during World War II—even as some of their families were kept in confinement in the US. This long-awaited bill will open the door for more than 6,000 soldiers known as Nisei veterans to collectively receive the prestigious Congressional Gold Medal. For more on the vets, see the Digital Journal or Oregon Public Broadcasting.

details.ground_truth: Earlier this year, President Trump noted "I just don't want a poor person" in charge of economic posts in his administration, then touted Commerce Secretary Wilbur Ross as "a very rich person" who was up to the task. But just how rich Ross really is is now up in the air, Forbes reports, noting it's yanked Ross off its billionaires list after a one-month probe revealed that $2 billion or so or Ross' alleged fortune "never existed," and that Ross had engaged in an "apparent sequence of fibs, exaggerations, omissions, fabrications, and whoppers" with the magazine going back 13 years. The discovery came when Ross, 79, contacted Forbes in October to refute his ranking: Last year he was listed with a net worth of $2.9 billion on the Forbes 400; he claimed he was worth closer to $3.7 billion. Then Forbes talked with 10 of Ross' ex-employees at his private equity firm, who noted a "penchant for misleading" that affected fellow workers and investors and spurred big fines, suits, and refunds to backers. "Wilbur doesn't have an issue with bending the truth," one longtime colleague says, while another is more blunt: "He's lied to a lot of people." The magazine delves into Ross' back story, including how, when he first made the Forbes billionaires list in 2004 with a net worth of $1 billion, "everyone that I knew that worked with Wilbur knew it wasn't true," per a former colleague. When Forbes told Ross he was being removed from its billionaires club, Ross retorted the magazine wasn't counting family trusts that he wasn't obligated to inform the feds about—in the amount of "more than $2 billion," assets Ross said he put into the trusts sometime "between the election and [my] nomination." When Forbes asked to see a paper trail proving that, Ross cited "privacy issues"—and the magazine lays out other problems with Ross' story on the supposed $2 billion transfer. **Multimillion-Dollar Court Battle Over Kurt Cobain's Guitar**

A highly publicized and contentious dispute is brewing over the ownership of Kurt Cobain's most prized guitar, a Martin D-18E, which he played during Nirvana's iconic 1993 "MTV Unplugged" concert. The guitar is believed to be the last one Cobain played before his death in 1994.

At the center of the dispute is Frances Bean Cobain, Kurt's daughter with Courtney Love, and her estranged husband, Isaiah Silva, a musician in the band the Eeries. Silva claims that Frances Bean gifted him the guitar as a wedding present in 2014, when they secretly married. However, Frances Bean denies this, stating that the guitar is a treasured family heirloom and not Silva's to take.

The dispute has escalated to a multimillion-dollar court battle, with experts estimating that the guitar is now worth several times its original insured value of $1 million. Love has publicly stated that the guitar is not Silva's and has even allegedly sent threatening messages to Silva's ex-girlfriend in an attempt to get him to hand over the instrument.

**Spousal Support Dispute**

In a separate development, Isaiah Silva has requested spousal support from Frances Bean, citing that he left a steady job to marry her and that she promised to cover his monthly expenses, including private school and housing bills for his child from a previous relationship. Silva is seeking a minimum of $25,000 per month in support.

Frances Bean has made it clear in her divorce documents that the Kurt Cobain money is hers alone and not Silva's. However, she is open to paying some spousal support. The dispute has added to the tension in the already contentious divorce proceedings.

**Next Steps**

The court battle over the guitar is set to go to a judge, with Frances Bean's side accusing Silva of taking "extreme and unsupportable positions" on the disagreement. The outcome of the dispute is far from certain, and it remains to be seen how the court will rule on the ownership of the valuable and historic instrument.
details.ground_truth: A guitar that once belonged to Kurt Cobain, which is likely worth millions, is now in the hands of Eeries frontman Isaiah Silva, and Cobain's daughter is fighting to get it back in the family. Frances Bean Cobain was married to Silva for 21 months, and she's been trying to get the Martin D-18E (which had the bridge flipped so Cobain, a leftie, could play it comfortably) back ever since they split in March. Silva claims she gave it to him as a wedding gift and it's rightfully his. Now, the battle is headed to court, Page Six reports. "It’s not [Silva’s]. It’s a treasured heirloom of the family. It’s not his to take," Frances Cobain has said. But sources tell Page Six that Silva isn't giving in, and "is forcing the matter to litigation." The guitar, the one Cobain played during Nirvana's 1993 MTV Unplugged show and also thought to be the last one he played before taking his life, was once insured for $1 million but, per experts, is probably worth several times that now. Back in August, Silva requested $25,000 a month in spousal support and half of any money Frances made during the marriage, and said the couple had no prenup, TMZ reported. A judge has since ordered Frances Cobain to pay Silva $15,000 a month as part of an interim settlement.

The recent months have seen a significant shift in the way Hollywood and the world at large address and discuss issues of sexual harassment and assault. The #MeToo movement, sparked by actress Alyssa Milano, has encouraged women to share their stories of abuse and harassment, leading to a wave of high-profile accusations and firings.

Matt Damon, a prominent actor and producer, has been at the center of the controversy, with his comments on the "spectrum of behavior" in an interview with ABC News sparking widespread outrage. Actress Minnie Driver, a co-star and former romantic partner of Damon's, called him "tone deaf" and argued that men should not be granted the power to interpret abuse inflicted on women without the risk of redoubling an injustice they can scarcely understand.

Damon has since defended his comments, saying that he was trying to make a distinction between different types of behavior, but many have argued that this approach minimizes the severity of the abuse and harassment that women face. Damon has also been criticized for his defense of comedian Louis CK, who was accused of misconduct, and his comments on Harvey Weinstein, who has been accused of rape and assault.

Weinstein's case has highlighted the issue of confidentiality agreements and the way in which they can be used to silence victims of abuse and harassment. Damon argued that the day of the confidentiality agreements is over, and that victims should be listened to and heard.

The controversy surrounding Damon and his comments has also sparked a wider discussion about the role of men in the #MeToo movement and the way in which they can support and amplify women's voices. Actress Minnie Driver argued that men should not be granted the power to interpret abuse inflicted on women, and that they should instead focus on listening and supporting.

The #MeToo movement has also highlighted the issue of the patriarchy and the way in which it is intertwined with normalized, accepted, and even welcomed misogyny. A victim of sexual assault wrote a powerful piece, stating that each component of the sexual assault spectrum hurts and are all connected to a patriarchy.

Overall, the recent months have seen a significant shift in the way we discuss and address issues of sexual harassment and assault, and the #MeToo movement has encouraged women to share their stories and speak out against abuse and harassment.
details.ground_truth: Matt Damon opined this week that the wave of sex harassment and assault claims sweeping Hollywood and society at large include "a spectrum of behavior" and that "none of us came here perfect," and the woman he once dumped on Oprah wasted no time in shutting him down. "Gosh it’s so interesting (profoundly unsurprising) how men with all these opinions about women’s differentiation between sexual misconduct, assault and rape reveal themselves to be utterly tone deaf and as a result, systemically part of the problem," Minnie Driver tweeted. She further unloaded to the Guardian: "I’ve realized that most men, good men, the men that I love, there is a cut-off. They simply cannot understand what abuse is like on a daily level. I honestly think that until we get on the same page, you can’t tell a woman about their abuse. ... It is so individual and so personal, it’s galling when a powerful man steps up and starts dictating the terms, whether he intends it or not.” Damon was also getting zero sympathy from Alyssa Milano, reports USA Today, who tweeted that, "as a victim of each component of the sexual assault spectrum of which you speak," "they all hurt." "We are not outraged because someone grabbed our a---s in a picture. We are outraged because we were made to feel this was normal. We are outraged because we have been gaslighted. We are outraged because we were silenced for so long." (Damon has said he had no idea that Harvey Weinstein routinely sexually harassed and assaulted women; his full interview this week with ABC News is here.)

Microsoft Unveils Kinect: A Revolutionary Motion-Control System for Xbox 360

Microsoft has finally given its motion-control system a real name, formally unveiling Kinect during a special Cirque du Soleil performance in Los Angeles. Kinect is the company's answer to Nintendo's Wii, which popularized game play, and it doesn't require a controller at all. The system can recognize a wider range of motion and even differentiate voices.

The event, which kicked off the E3 Expo videogame industry tradeshow, was a cross between a gospel revival and a Las Vegas show, featuring Cirque acrobats, a 9-foot tall elephant puppet, and ponchos with illuminating shoulder pads for audience members. The details about pricing and availability are expected to be unveiled at Microsoft's Monday press conference.

Kinect is a motion-sensing controller that allows gamers to use their body as a controller. A number of games were unveiled at the event, including:

* A Yoga/Tai Chi Game (official name to be announced)
* A Soccer Game (official name to be announced)
* Joyride: A racing game where players can steer using their hands
* Kinect Sports: A collection of sports games that lets players jump hurdles, run sprints, play volleyball, kick a soccer ball, and bowl
* Kinectimals: A game where players can play with 20 different kitties like a lion, cheetah, and tiger
* Kinect Adventures: A game where players can go on a river rafting adventure with multiple players moving their bodies in the raft
* Dance Central: A dance-em-up game made by MTV Games

In addition to these games, Kinect will also have dashboard apps that show motion controls used to interact with Netflix, scroll through photos, start live video chats with friends, and share photos.

Microsoft thinks it has a game-changer with Kinect, and it's clear that the company has a system on its hands that it plans to incorporate widely across its video gaming and digital living room environments. However, it remains to be seen how Kinect will be used and what software is being developed for it, which will be revealed at Microsoft's Monday press conference.
details.ground_truth: Microsoft unveiled its much-hyped Project Natal motion control system—now renamed “Kinect”—at E3 last night, in a performance long on grandiosity and short on actual revelations. After walking through a set of fake living rooms, the white-poncho-wearing audience was treated to a performance by Cirque du Soleil acrobats dressed as cavemen, as a narrator declared that “history is about to rewritten” and a boy, raising his arms, used his movements usher in the new motion-based way to play games, according to the Wall Street Journal. And what was this grand historical event all about? Well, mostly mimicking the Wii, according to CNET. Microsoft’s camera-based motion controller may have potential, but the games demoed last night mostly looked like analogues for successful Wii efforts, including a yoga game, a racing game, and a Wii Sports-esque game titled—wait for it—Kinect Sports. IGN has a full list of launch titles. Microsoft is expected to reveal more details, including pricing, at its press conference today.

**Headline: Unique Items and Charitable Causes: A Roundup of Recent News**

In a world where people are constantly seeking the next big thing, it's refreshing to see individuals and celebrities using their platforms for good. This week, we're highlighting a few stories that showcase unique items, charitable causes, and the human side behind the headlines.

**The Unabomber's Memorabilia: A Tragic Event with Historical Value**

The U.S. Marshal's office is currently holding an online auction of the personal effects of Ted Kaczynski, also known as the Unabomber. Between 1978 and 1995, Kaczynski engaged in a mail bombing spree, killing three people and injuring 23 others. The auction, which ends on June 2, aims to raise money to pay off a $15 million restitution order to his victims and their families. While some items, such as his 35,000-word handwritten manifesto and iconic hoodie, are attracting serious bids, others, like a hand-bowed wood saw, are selling for much more than their actual worth due to hype and nostalgia.

**Kellan Lutz's Charitable Luggage: A Celebrity's Heart of Gold**

Twilight heartthrob Kellan Lutz is teaming up with JetBlue and DoSomething.org to auction off his luggage for a good cause. The celebrity baggage auction, which is now in its second round, aims to raise funds for youth groups across the country. Lutz, who says he's happy to take part in the charity auction, hopes his bag will help raise some serious funds to support DoSomething.org.

**A Rare Film Reel: A Piece of History and a Six-Figure Sum**

Collector Morace Park bought a film reel initially because he liked the look of the tin, but it's now set to command a six-figure sum. The film, Charlie Chaplin in Zepped, is the only known surviving copy and shows the famous British actor bringing down a German Zeppelin during a London raid. Made as a morale-boosting film for British troops during World War One, the seven-minute long reel contains some of the earliest known animation.

These stories remind us that even in the midst of tragedy and chaos, there is often value to be found and a chance to make a difference. Whether it's a rare film reel or a celebrity's charitable luggage, these unique items and causes are a testament to the human spirit and our desire to connect with the world around us.
details.ground_truth: Get out your checkbooks, kids: There is a slew of famous and infamous items up for auction. The US Marshal's office's online auction of the Unabomber's personal effects ends tomorrow, and Aol's Weird News astutely observes that Ted Kaczynski's 35,000-word handwritten manifesto ($17,525) is currently going for less than the gray hoodie ($20,025) that figured so prominently into his FBI Wanted poster. Bonus: sunglasses included. Looking for more of a bargain? A bow and arrows in a Sears box is currently a steal at $743. And in other bizarre auction news: Balloon boy's dad is trying to auction off the infamous "flying saucer" balloon for $1 million. But don't worry, reports KTLA: Richard Heene has vowed to give all the money to charity (specifically, Japan). A bunch of celebrities are hoping JetBlue loses their luggage ... right into the hands of eager bidders. The Celebrity Baggage Auction will benefit DoSomething.org. Buy a signed Jessica Simpson-brand bag or a leather Coach bag personally used by Rosario Dawson, and get two roundtrip JetBlue tickets, too. Click for more. Maybe eBay is the place to buy stuff: Morace Park bought a film reel for $5.25 that turned out to be the only known copy of the Charlie Chaplin film Zepped. The Telegraph reports that it's expected to fetch six figures in a June 29 auction.
