Kevin Spacey, a renowned Academy Award-winning actor, has been embroiled in a scandal of alleged sexual misconduct and harassment spanning decades. The allegations, which have surfaced since October 29, involve more than a dozen men, including some who were minors at the time of the incidents.

According to reports, the first accusation of sexual misconduct against Spacey was made by actor Anthony Rapp, who alleged that Spacey made unwanted sexual advances towards him in 1986, when Rapp was 14. Spacey apologized for his behavior and came out as gay in a statement, which was met with criticism from the LGBTQ+ community.

Since then, more allegations have emerged, including from Roberto Cavazos, a Mexican actor who claimed that Spacey fondled or tried to fondle him against his will while he was at the Old Vic theater in London. Other accusers include Daniel Beal, who said that Spacey exposed himself to him in a West Sussex hotel in 2010, and Justin Dawes, who claimed that Spacey gave him and his friend cocktails and played pornography on the TV in his apartment in 1988.

In addition to these allegations, several anonymous accusers have come forward, including a bartender who said that Spacey grabbed his crotch in a London bar in 2007, and a production assistant who claimed that Spacey put his hand down his pants while driving him to a Baltimore-area set during one of the show's early seasons.

The latest development in the scandal is that Spacey will be completely cut from his upcoming film, All the Money in the World, and replaced by Christopher Plummer. The decision was made by director Ridley Scott and supported by Sony, and the film is still set to be released on December 22.

Heather Unruh, a former TV news anchor in Boston, has also come forward with a new allegation against Spacey. She claims that her 18-year-old son was sexually assaulted by Spacey in a bar in Nantucket, Mass., in July 2016. Unruh said that her son was embarrassed and fearful of reporting the incident at the time, but is now committed to doing what he can to stop Spacey from victimizing anyone else.

Spacey's representatives have not commented on the latest allegations, but the actor has previously issued a statement apologizing for his behavior and coming out as gay. The scandal has raised questions about the treatment of sex addiction and criminal behavior in the entertainment industry, and has sparked a wider conversation about the need for greater accountability
details.ground_truth: A former Boston TV news anchor says that last year, when her son was 18, he was at a bar in Natucket, Mass., when he met Kevin Spacey. The actor "bought him drink after drink after drink and when my son was drunk, Spacey made his move and sexually assaulted him," said Heather Unruh at a press conference Wednesday. She says Spacey stuck his hand down her son's pants and onto his genitals, and tried to convince him to go to a party where they could continue to drink, USA Today reports. A bystander asked the teen if he was OK and the teen said no, at which point the woman urged the teen to flee and he took her advice, Unruh said. Unruh, who appeared with an attorney, says her son filed a police report last week and handed over evidence; she says a criminal investigation has been opened but the Nantucket Police Department has not confirmed that. The attorney with Unruh noted that her son's accusations are "well within" both the civil and criminal statutes of limitations. USA Today has a list of the 14 people so far accusing Spacey of sexual assault or harassment. Meanwhile Jon Bernthal, who worked with Spacey on Baby Driver, told a SiriusXM radio show Tuesday that he lost respect for Spacey while working on the film. "I thought he was a bit of a bully," Bernthal said, per People. "I didn’t really care for the way he was behaving toward some of the other people on set."

**Mali Crisis: A Threat to West Africa and the World**

A recent coup in Mali has sparked a crisis that threatens not only the stability of the West African region but also the world. The coup, led by a group of disgruntled soldiers, overthrew the democratically elected government of President Amadou Toumani Touré on March 22. The power vacuum created by the coup has allowed the Tuareg rebellion, a separatist movement that seeks to establish an independent Tuareg state called Azawad, to gain control of the northern part of the country.

The Tuareg rebels, who are divided into two groups - the National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad (MNLA) and Ansar Dine, a group linked to al-Qaeda - have taken advantage of the situation and made rapid advances in the past few days. They now control a third of the country, including the key northern towns of Gao, Kidal, and Timbuktu.

**Threat to the Stability of West Africa**

The crisis in Mali has sparked concerns among leaders across the region, who fear that a similar situation could occur in their own countries. The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), representing 15 nations in the region, has been harsh in its condemnation of the coup and has imposed sanctions on Mali, including a ban on the importation of fuel and a freeze on the country's account at the regional central bank.

The sanctions have had a significant impact on the country, with long lines forming at petrol stations and the country's electricity grid expected to falter in the coming weeks. The landlocked country imports all its fuel, which is trucked in overland from neighboring Ivory Coast and Senegal.

**Threat to the World Heritage Site of Timbuktu**

The crisis in Mali has also raised concerns about the safety of the World Heritage Site of Timbuktu, a city located on the edge of the Sahara desert. The city is home to a rich literary and scientific heritage, including over 700,000 ancient manuscripts, and is considered a repository of knowledge from the 15th century.

The Tuareg rebels have taken control of the city, and residents have reported that Islamic law is being imposed. The city's mayor, Halle Cisse, has expressed concerns about the safety of the city and its heritage, and the UN Security Council is due to hold an emergency meeting on the crisis in Mali later on Tuesday.

**Humanitarian Crisis**

The crisis in Mali
details.ground_truth: Since we last checked in, the situation in Mali following a soldiers' coup has spiraled, with rising concerns over food and gas shortages, violence, and indeed the country's "very existence," according to Le Monde. An update: The coup has driven more than 200,000 people from their homes to other parts of the region, the AP reports. The UN worries that major food shortages could be imminent, and "mayhem in these towns and cities is increasing," says a rep. Neighboring countries have established an embargo against the rebels who ousted the president; they've closed off their borders and frozen the country's regional bank account. That has prompted residents of Mali, which imports all its fuel, to rush to gas stations to collect fuel. The country's electricity grid may also be headed for failure. UNESCO is citing a threat to Timbuktu, a World Heritage Site full of "architectural wonders," notes the BBC. Mosques there are "essential to the preservation of the identity of the people of Mali," says the group. Yet "nothing seems to be able to stop" the Tuareg coup, Le Monde reports. West African leaders need to help Mali's military fight back before the instability spreads beyond the country's borders.

**Global Efforts to Eradicate Child Institutionalization by 2050**

A recent awareness campaign launched by author and philanthropist JK Rowling highlights the issue of child institutionalization, a practice that affects an estimated 8 million children worldwide. According to Rowling, the problem is often overlooked due to the ease with which children, especially those with disabilities, can be silenced and hidden from the public eye.

Lumos, a charity founded by Rowling in 2005, aims to close down child institutions and promote family-based care. The organization has seen significant success in several countries, including Moldova, where a 70% reduction in the number of children in institutions has been achieved since 2007. In the Czech Republic, Lumos's demonstration area has seen a 75% fall in admissions, while in Bulgaria, the number of children in institutions has reduced by 54% since the organization began working there.

The solution to child institutionalization, according to Rowling, is not simply to improve the conditions within institutions, but to eradicate them altogether. This can be achieved by investing in inclusive education and health, providing support to vulnerable families, and promoting a culture that prioritizes keeping families together.

International donors play a crucial role in this effort, and their choices on which issues to fund and which principles to promote can greatly influence the availability of support to children and families. Ending the practice of child institutionalization is not only a moral imperative, but also makes excellent economic sense, as it is far more cost-effective to support a child in a family than in an institution.

The #LetstalkLumos campaign, launched last month, aims to create awareness and change the future for these children. With a critical mass of expertise and evidence now available, and a growing commitment from the EU, the US, and the Global Alliance for Children, the goal of eradicating child institutionalization by 2050 seems within reach.
details.ground_truth: JK Rowling makes the case today that millions of kids around the world are living in appalling conditions—the stuff of Grimms' fairytales—in institutions that are supposed to be caring for them. Rowling wants to change that, not by improving these orphanages with "pretty murals" or teddy bears, but by eliminating them altogether, she writes in the Guardian. The big challenge is providing support for often-poor families who think they have no choice but to place their child in such an institution. If a child's biological family isn't up to the task, then foster families can step in. But Rowling argues that institutions are absolutely the wrong answer. Rowling has started a charity called Lumos (yes, after one of her Harry Potter spells), and writes that it has made genuine progress in reducing new placements in countries such as Bulgaria, Moldova, and the Czech Republic. She wants to raise awareness and donations with campaigns like this one. "I recently committed to becoming president of Lumos for life," writes Rowling. "It is my dream that, within my lifetime, the very concept of taking a child away from its family and locking it away will seem to belong to a cruel, fictional world." Click for her full column.

**US and Pakistan Agree to Cooperate on Future Operations Against "High-Value Targets"**

In a bid to ease tensions and improve their strained relationship, the United States and Pakistan have agreed to work together on future operations against "high-value targets" in Pakistan. The announcement was made in a joint joint statement, following a visit by US Senator John Kerry to Pakistan.

**Key Developments:**

1. **Return of US Helicopter Tail:**** Pakistan has agreed to return the tail of a US military helicopter that was damaged during the raid that killed Osama bin Laden. The handover is part of a series of steps aimed at reducing tensions between Islamabad and Washington.
2. **Increased Intelligence Sharing and Joint Operations:**** The two countries have committed to increase their intelligence sharing and joint operations, which had come to a near standstill in recent months.
3. **Cooperation on "High-Value Targets":**** The US and Pakistan have agreed to cooperate on operations against "high-value targets" in Pakistan, which could help to ease concerns about Pakistani support for militant groups.
4. **US Economic Assistance Package:**** US Senator John Kerry, who chairs the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, has conveyed to Pakistani leaders that they must demonstrate a commitment to fighting Islamist militancy to address the concerns of members of Congress who have called for an end to US aid.

**Challenges Ahead:**

1. **Anti-US Sentiment:**** Anti-US sentiment is widespread in Pakistan, and the country's leaders are under pressure to respond to the outrage over the bin Laden raid.
2. **Drone Strikes:**** The US has continued to carry out drone strikes in Pakistan, which are widely unpopular and have been condemned by the Pakistani Parliament.
3. **Nuclear Arsenal:**** The US has assured Pakistan that it has "no designs" on its nuclear arsenal, but the issue remains a source of concern for Pakistani leaders.

**Conclusion:**

The US and Pakistan have taken a significant step forward in improving their relationship, but the challenges ahead are significant. The two countries must work together to address the threat of militant groups and to ease the concerns of members of Congress who have called for an end to US aid.
details.ground_truth: The US and Pakistan have agreed to work together in the future against "high value targets" in Pakistan, the countries announced in a joint statement today. The news comes after Sen. John Kerry arrived in Pakistan last night, intending to send a strong message following the death of Osama bin Laden: Cooperate more when it comes to rooting out terrorists, or face "profound" changes in the US-Pakistan relationship—which could include kissing billions in aid goodbye. Kerry is the most senior US official to go to Pakistan since bin Laden's death, but since issuing the joint pledge, he said Secretary of State Hillary Clinton will soon announce her own plans to visit, the AP reports. Kerry's meetings with Pakistani leaders began last night, the Washington Post reports. Many in Washington think Pakistan is harboring Islamist militants, and some members of Congress are calling for the US to sever the billions of dollars in aid it provides to Pakistan. Kerry is in Pakistan to lay out the new stakes following bin Laden's death, and question officials about how bin Laden was able to hide in Pakistan for years. In Kabul, Kerry said there is "some evidence" that the Pakistan government has knowledge of insurgent activities, calling it "very disturbing."

**Deadly VX Nerve Agent Used in Assassination of Kim Jong-nam, North Korea's Exiled Half-brother**

In a shocking and deadly turn of events, the banned chemical weapon VX nerve agent was used to kill Kim Jong-nam, the North Korean ruler's outcast half-brother, at the Kuala Lumpur airport in Malaysia. The announcement by Malaysia's police chief, Khalid Abu Bakar, raised serious questions about public safety in the airport, which authorities went 11 days without decontaminating.

According to the police, two women, one Indonesian and one Vietnamese, coated their hands with chemicals and wiped them on Kim's face on February 13 as he waited for a flight home to Macau. Kim sought help from airport staff but fell into convulsions and died on the way to the hospital within two hours of the attack.

The use of VX nerve agent, which has the consistency of motor oil and can take days or even weeks to evaporate, has sparked a diplomatic crisis between Malaysia and North Korea. North Korea has denounced Malaysia's investigation as full of "holes and contradictions" and accused the authorities of being in cahoots with Pyongyang's enemies.

The case has also raised questions about how the two women could have walked away unscathed after handling a powerful poison, even if they were instructed to wash their hands right after the attack. Dr. Bruce Goldberger, a leading toxicologist, said that even a tiny amount of VX nerve agent is capable of killing and that it is possible that both of these women were given the antidote.

North Korea is believed to have the capacity to produce up to 4,500 metric tons of chemical weapons during a typical year, which it could increase to 12,000 tons per year during a period of crisis. Its current inventory has been estimated at 2,500 to 5,000 tons, and it is suspected of being particularly focused on mustard, phosgene, sarin, and V-type chemical agents, including VX.

The use of VX nerve agent in the assassination of Kim Jong-nam has sparked concerns about the potential use of chemical weapons in other parts of the world, including the Korean Peninsula. Joseph Bermudez, a well-known North Korea analyst, wrote an article in 2013 that said the North is capable of employing "significant quantities and varieties of chemical weapons" across the Korean Peninsula and using those weapons worldwide "using unconventional methods of delivery."

Malaysia has three people in custody in connection with Kim Jong-n
details.ground_truth: Kim Jong Nam's assassins killed him with a banned chemical weapon, Malaysian police revealed Friday. The country's police chief said toxicology reports on swabs from the face and eyes of the exiled North Korean found VX nerve agent, which the BBC notes is classed as a weapon of mass destruction by the United Nations. He said that one of two women believed to have rubbed the extremely toxic substance on Kim's face with their hands suffered from vomiting after the attack. The New York Times reports that VX agent can be created by mixing two compounds—and police suspect the two women put the substances on Kim's face, one after the other, to create a deadly dose. The police chief said the airport where Kim was attacked is now being decontaminated. North Korea—which is widely suspected to have been behind the killing of leader Kim Jong Un's half brother—never signed the Chemical Weapons Convention that banned VX, the AP notes. Pyongyang denies involvement and says Malaysia's investigation is full of "holes and contradictions." The father of one of the two women being held, Vietnamese citizen Doan Thi Huong, tells the Times his daughter trained as a pharmacist and he has seen little of her in recent years. (Police say that after Malaysia refused to give Kim's body to North Korean diplomats, somebody tried to break into the morgue.)

**RICHMOND HIGH SCHOOL RAPE AND BEATING CASE: A SUMMARIZED ACCOUNT**

On October 24, 2009, a 15-year-old sophomore girl was brutally gang-raped and beaten on the grounds of Richmond High School in Richmond, California, after attending the school's homecoming dance. The incident sent shockwaves throughout the community and raised questions about campus security and the bystander effect.

**The Attack**

According to police, the girl had left the dance and was walking to meet her father for a ride home when a classmate invited her to join a group drinking in the courtyard. She had consumed a large amount of alcohol by the time the assault began, which lasted for over two hours. As many as two dozen people may have witnessed the rapes or been aware of what was happening, but did not report it to the authorities.

**Arrests and Suspects**

Two suspects were initially arrested: Manuel Ortega, a 19-year-old former student who was arrested as he ran from the scene, and a 15-year-old boy who knew the victim and was arrested on Monday. However, police later revealed that they believe as many as 10 assailants, ranging in age from 15 to their early 20s, were involved in the attack. A 21-year-old Richmond man was also arrested on Tuesday night on suspicion of sexual assault and rape.

**Campus Security and the Bystander Effect**

The attack has raised questions about campus security and the bystander effect. Richmond High has surveillance cameras, but none were working at the time of the attack. A new system is expected to be in place in the next few months. Four police officers and several school security staffers were on hand at the homecoming dance, but two of the staffers left early due to a prior commitment.

**Community Reaction**

The attack has sent shockwaves throughout the community, with many students and parents expressing their outrage and fear. Some students have already left the school in response to the attack, with one student saying, "It's not safe there at all. I'm not going back."

**Investigation and Reward**

The police are still investigating the incident and are offering a $20,000 reward for information leading to the conviction of any of the assailants. Detective Ken Greco called the incident "disgusting" and "a sad state of affairs," and said that the men involved laughed and took photos as they took turns assaulting the girl.

Overall, the Richmond High School rape and beating case is a
details.ground_truth: San Francisco-area cops have arrested three more young men in the savage gang rape, beating, and robbery of a 15-year-old girl outside her homecoming dance while dozens more watched. Five youths are being held, ranging from a 15-year-old boy who knew the victim to a 21-year-old. The girl was drinking outside the dance Saturday night, reports the San Francisco Chronicle, and the group turned on her when she became drunk. Charges are expected today. "This was a barbaric act. I still cannot get my head around the fact that numerous people either watched, walked away or participated in her assault," a Richmond cop tells the AP. "It's one of the most disturbing crimes in my 15 years as a police officer." Some students began transferring out of the school. "It's not safe there at all," said a 16-year-old girl. "I'm not going back."

**Summary of Recent News News**

**FBI and DOJ Under Scrutiny for Alleged Surveillance Abuses**

The House Intelligence Committee has released a memo, known as the Nunes memo, alleging that the FBI and the Justice Department abused their surveillance authority when they sought a secret court order to monitor a former Trump campaign adviser, Carter Page. The memo claims that the government failed to disclose important information about the source of the information used to obtain the warrant, Christopher Steele, a former British intelligence officer who was hired by the Democratic National Committee and the Hillary Clinton campaign to compile a dossier on Trump.

**Steele's Bias and Lack of Reliability**

The memo also raises concerns about Steele's bias and lack of reliability as a source. Steele had a history of unauthorized disclosures to the media and had a clear bias against Trump, which was not disclosed in the FISA application. Additionally, Steele's wife was employed by Fusion GPS, the company that hired Steele, and his relationship with Steele and Fusion GPS was concealed from the FISA court.

**FBI's Handling of the Investigation**

The memo also criticizes the FBI's handling of the investigation, including the decision to brief President-elect Trump on a summary of the Steele dossier, despite its lack of corroboration. Deputy Director McCabe testified that no surveillance warrant would have been sought from the FISA court without the Steele dossier information.

**Twitter Location Location Feature**

In a separate development, Twitter has announced a new feature that allows users to add location information to their tweets, such as their city or precise location. Users can also delete their tweet location history at any time. This feature is designed to provide more transparency and accountability for users who share their location on the platform.

**Overall Implications**

The release of the Nunes memo and the Twitter location location feature have significant implications for the way we communicate and share information online. The memo highlights the need for transparency and accountability in the use of surveillance powers, while the Twitter feature provides a new way for users to share their location and connect with others in their community.

Trump, who has called the investigation a "witch hunt," has supported the release of the memo in the apparent hopes that it could help undermine the probe being led by special counsel Robert Mueller. The president, dogged by the unrelenting investigation into his campaign's ties to Russia, lashed out anew Friday at the FBI and Justice Department as politically biased against Republicans. "The top Leadership and Investigators of the FBI and the Justice Department have politicized the sacred investigative process in favor of Democrats and against Republicans - something which would have been unthinkable just a short time ago. Rank & File are great people!" Trump tweeted.

**Gerry Adams Released After Four Days of Questioning in Connection with 1972 IRA Murder**

Sinn Fein leader Gerry Adams was released on Sunday evening after four days of questioning by police in Northern Ireland in connection with the 1972 IRA murder of Jean McConville, a 37-year-old widow and mother of 10. Adams, who has consistently denied any involvement in the murder, was arrested under the terrorism act and taken to Antrim police station for questioning.

**Background to the Case**

Jean McConville was abducted from her home in west Belfast in December 1972 and was shot and secretly buried. Her body was not found until 2003, when it was discovered on a beach in County Louth. The case is one of the many "Disappeared" cases in Northern Ireland, where people were abducted, murdered, and buried in secret by republicans during the Troubles.

**Adams' Response to the Arrest**

Adams has criticized the timing of his arrest, which came in the middle of an election campaign. He claimed that the police could have used discretion and not arrested him, and that they had used "pernicious, coercive legislation" to deal with a legacy issue. Adams also said that he had contacted the police two months ago about the McConville case and had gone voluntarily to the police station for questioning.

**Reaction from Other Politicians**

Other politicians have rejected claims that the timing of the arrest was political. The Democratic Unionist Party's Jeffrey Donaldson said that there was nothing in the law that said the police had to suspend the investigation of murder during an election campaign. The Ulster Unionist Party's Mike Nesbitt said that the McConville family's 42-year wait for truth and justice made Adams' complaint over the delay seem churlish.

**Next Steps**

The decision to release Adams means that prosecution lawyers will now decide if charges will be brought. The police will send a file to the Public Prosecution Service detailing the evidence gathered, and the lawyers will decide if there is enough evidence to bring a conviction and if the prosecution is required in the public interest. It could take some time for the police to prepare the file and for the prosecutors to assess the evidence.

**Boston College Tapes**

The case against Adams is based on an interview he allegedly gave to researchers at Boston College in the US. The Boston College tapes are a series of candid, confessional interviews with former loyalist and republican paramilitaries, designed to be an oral history of

**Two Daycare Workers Arrested for Injuring Five Toddlers with Hot Glue**

In a disturbing and alarming incident, two daycare workers, Lizandra Cosme, 32, and Susana Gonzalez, 27, have been arrested and charged for allegedly injuring five 2-year-old toddlers with a hot glue gun at the Children's Learning Place in Chicago, Illinois. The incident occurred on December 1, and the extent of the children's injuries is still unclear.

According to surveillance footage, Cosme, who was in charge of 16 children at the time, applied the hot glue gun directly to the hands and arms of the five toddlers, causing them to grimace in pain and whine. Gonzalez, an assistant at the daycare center, was seen laughing and watching the incident unfold.

Cosme's defense attorney claimed that his client "screwed up" but did not act out of malice, citing her 12 years of experience working with young children. However, prosecutors argued that Cosme's actions were reckless and negligent, and she was denied bail at a court appearance.

Gonzalez was charged with five misdemeanor counts of causing the circumstances of child endangerment. Both women are scheduled to return to court on December 26.

The Children's Learning Place, a licensed child-care facility with four locations in Chicago, immediately terminated the two employees after learning of the alleged incident. The state Department of Children and Family Services is investigating the allegations of abuse, and the daycare center is working with investigators to determine the circumstances surrounding the incident.

This incident highlights the importance of ensuring the safety and well-being of children in childcare settings, and the need for strict background checks and monitoring of employees to prevent such incidents from occurring in the future.
details.ground_truth: Two workers at a Chicago day care were fired and arrested after a disturbing incident that left five toddlers injured. Police say surveillance video captured one of the workers burning the 2-year-olds with a hot glue gun while the other woman watched and laughed, People reports. Lizandra Cosme, 32, has been charged with five counts of aggravated battery of a child causing great bodily harm, the Chicago Sun-Times reports. Susana Gonzalez, 27, faces five misdemeanor charges of causing the circumstances of child endangerment over the Dec. 1 incident at the Children's Place day care. It's not clear how seriously the children were injured. Prosecutors say Cosme, who brought the glue gun for a Christmas project, was captured on video applying the hot glue directly to the hands and arms of the three girls and two boys. "Each of the child victims winced and some whined at the hot glue gun application," a prosecutor said during a Monday court appearance. Prosecutors said Cosme tried to cover up the incident, asking a father whether his child had been burned at home, ABC7 reports. They said her actions were discovered when one child's mother, an ER physician, saw the burns and demanded to see surveillance footage. Cosme's lawyer said his client "screwed up" but did not mean to hurt the children.

**Texas Gubernatorial Candidate Wendy Davis Discloses Abortion History in Memoir**

Texas Democratic gubernatorial candidate Wendy Davis has revealed in her upcoming memoir, "Forgetting to Be Afraid," that she had two abortions, one in 1994 and another in 1996, due to severe fetal abnormalities and a medically necessary ectopic pregnancy.

According to the book, Davis's first abortion was in 1994, when she was a mother of two young girls. She writes that she considered considering the pregnancy during her 2013 filibuster on abortion restrictions, but decided against it to avoid overshadowing the event.

The second abortion, in 1996, was due to a severe brain abnormality in the fetus. Davis writes that she could feel the fetus's body trembling violently and knew that it was suffering, leading her to make the difficult decision to end the pregnancy.

Davis's memoir also delves into her personal life, including her struggles as a single mother and her journey to becoming a Harvard Law School graduate and a state senator. She credits her second husband, Jeff, as her partner and mentor, while maintaining that she would have gone through law school even without him.

The revelation of Davis's abortion history has sparked a mixed reaction from both sides of the abortion debate. Anti-abortion groups, such as Texans Right to Life, expressed sympathy for Davis's difficult decision, but emphasized the value of life. Planned Parenthood's president, Cecile Richards, praised Davis's "unwavering courage" for sharing her story and shedding light on the stigma surrounding abortion.

The memoir, which goes on sale Tuesday, is a key part of Davis's campaign for governor against Republican Attorney General Greg Abbott, who is heavily favored to replace Republican Gov. Rick Perry next year.
details.ground_truth: Texas gubernatorial candidate Wendy Davis' new memoir is dedicated in part to daughters Amber, Dru, and Tate, and those familiar with Davis' life story might be puzzled by the last name on that list. That's because Davis is revealing for the first time that Tate is the unborn child that she and her husband aborted in the second trimester, after doctors discovered a severe brain abnormality. The San Antonio Express-News and the AP obtained advanced copies, and the news is generating headlines given that Davis shot to national fame while filibustering against an abortion bill in Texas. Davis writes that she decided to abort in 1996 after doctors said the baby would be blind, deaf, and in a vegetative state if she survived the pregnancy. Davis also felt the fetus “tremble violently" in the womb, "as if someone were applying an electric shock to her, and I knew then what I needed to do. She was suffering." Her doctor "quieted" the unborn child's heart and delivered her by C-section. Davis and her husband had Tate baptized. After the abortion, "an indescribable blackness followed," writes Davis in Forgetting to Be Afraid, "and when I finally did come through it, I emerged a different person." Davis also writes of a previously disclosed procedure to terminate an earlier ectopic pregnancy in which the embryo was implanted outside the uterus. She says she opted not to talk about either case during her famous filibuster because she feared it would "overshadow the events of the day."

**Human Trafficking and Prostitution Ring Bust in Massachusetts**

In a recent investigation, two men, Joseph E. Van Wert, 65, and Randy W. Lambach, 45, were arrested and charged with human trafficking and related crimes for allegedly running a citywide prostitution ring in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. The investigation, which began in the spring of 2017, was sparked by reports of increased prostitution activity in the city.

According to police, Lambach, who had been running the prostitution ring for several years, would seek out women with severe drug addictions and advertise their services on social media. He would set up meetings and rates with prospective clients, and would take a significant cut of the women's money or pay them in heroin, cocaine, or prescription drugs in lieu of cash. Some of the women he recruited were formerly his foster children.

The investigation revealed that Lambach would transport the women to and from specific locations, including his third-floor apartment at a senior living facility, where some of the meetings would take place in exchange for drugs and cash. He also crossed state lines on at least one occasion, with one of the prostitutes saying that he drove her to Springfield, where she would prostitute herself from a strip club.

Lambach allegedly threatened to turn the women in if they stopped working for him, and warned them that the police would not believe them because they were "drug addicts and whores." Witnesses also reported that Lambach may have been involved in prostitution and trafficking for about 10 years.

Van Wert, who allowed some of the meetings to take place in his apartment, was charged with conspiracy to commit human trafficking, deriving support from prostitution, maintaining a house of prostitution, and sexual conduct for a fee. Lambach pleaded not guilty to four counts of human trafficking.

The investigation remains ongoing, and additional charges may be coming. The Pittsfield Police Department detective bureau, anti-crime unit, drug unit, and members of the state police detective unit assigned to the District Attorney's Office are assisting with the investigation.

**Contact Information:**

If you have any information regarding this case, please contact Pittsfield Police Officer Brenna Dorr at 413-448-9700, ext 384.

**Resources:**

For those affected by human trafficking and prostitution, there are resources available to help. The National Human Trafficking Hotline can be reached at 1-888-373-7888, and the Polaris BeFree Textline can be reached at 233733.
details.ground_truth: Two men have been charged with human trafficking after police arrested them for running a prostitution ring out of a senior living facility in Pittsfield, Mass. Police say Joseph Van Wert, 65, and Randy Lambach, 45, will face a hearing later this month, the New York Daily News reports. Lambach would allegedly seek out women with drug addictions and post their pictures online, then arrange meetings with clients. Some of these meetings took place in Van Wert's apartment in a senior facility. Van Wert offered his apartment in return for cash or drugs. The Berkshire Eagle reports that the police investigation began last spring in response to citizen complaints about increased prostitution across the city. Lambach would allegedly keep up to 90% of the money the women made, sometimes refusing them cash altogether and paying them in heroin or crack cocaine instead. At least one woman said Lambach drove her to an "appointment" across state lines. Police estimated one of the victims was 15 years old. They were also told that some of the women were Lambach's former foster children. Both men have pleaded not guilty, Lambach to four counts of human trafficking and Van Wert to conspiracy to commit human trafficking, deriving support from prostitution, maintaining a house of prostitution, and sexual conduct for a fee.

**Archaeological Discoveries Unveil the Secrets of Britain's Ancient Past**

In recent weeks, archaeologists have made two significant discoveries in the Vale of Pewsey, Wiltshire, shedding new light on the lives of people in the areas surrounding Stonehenge. The findings, which include a 4,000-year-old Bronze Age skeleton and a remarkably well-preserved Neolithic house, are set to revolutionize our understanding of Britain's ancient past.

**The Bronze Age Skeleton**

A team of archaeologists from the University of Reading, led by Dr. Jim Leary, discovered the skeleton of a child believed to be around 4,000 years old. The body was found lying in a foetal position, wearing an amber necklace. Scientific analysis will provide information on the child's gender, diet, pathologies, and date of burial, as well as shed light on where the young individual had lived.

**The Neolithic House**

The team also uncovered a remarkably well-preserved Neolithic house, believed to be one of the best-preserved from the period ever found in the UK. The house, which dates back over 4,300 years, was discovered on the edge of Marden Henge, a giant ditch and bank enclosure that has almost vanished from view. The house was found to have been used for great gatherings and feasts, with pig bones and scorch marks from a charcoal firepit suggesting that it was not a permanent residence.

**Marden Henge**

Marden Henge, which is believed to have been one of the biggest and most impressive monuments in Britain, is a key part of the archaeological discovery. The site, which is vast and takes 40 minutes to walk from one end to the other, has already produced a range of finds, including beautifully worked flint arrowheads and blades, decorated pottery, shale and copper bracelets, and a Roman brooch. The purpose of the monument is believed to have been status, showing off wealth and power in the ability to mobilise a massive workforce.

**The Vale of Pewsey Dig**

The discoveries are part of a three-year dig in the Vale of Pewsey, a partnership between the University of Reading, Historic England, the Arts and Humanities Research Council, and Wiltshire Museum. The aim of the dig is to gain a better understanding of the people who lived in the areas surrounding Stonehenge. The findings to date include a range of artifacts, including flint arrowheads and blades, decorated pottery, shale and copper bracelets, and a Roman
details.ground_truth: You think Stonehenge is impressive? Archaeologists in Britain are excavating a monument ten times larger than the iconic structure, though it appears to the naked eye to be little more than farmland. A henge is a circular earthwork, and the one in question is Marden Henge, which sits a few miles north of Stonehenge in Wiltshire. National Geographic reports that roughly 4,500 years ago it featured 10-foot-tall earthen berms that encompassed some 40 acres. The Guardian writes that the henge was "far larger than the Avebury or Stonehenge circles, and too large for any imaginable practical use." But over the ages Marden Henge's berms have "slumped" and farmers have worked the land, and archaeologists have gravitated to the more breathtaking Stonehenge. Until now. Jim Leary of the University of Reading has just kicked off a three-year study of the site; his excavations (he worked at the site in 2010, too) are the only ones to have occurred there in nearly 50 years. He hopes the berms will help explain the "insane, utterly unsustainable" construction boom that led to Marden Henge and four other nearby Neolithic monuments, including Stonehenge. "Not nearly enough attention has been paid to the archaeology of the fertile valley in between these places," says Leary. So far he's found the remains of a 4,000-year-old teen wearing an amber necklace just outside Marden Henge, per the BBC; fancy arrowheads; and a stone building within the henge containing the bones of at least 13 pigs, suggesting a huge feast was held there. "For all the attention that has been lavished on Stonehenge over the years, we may well find out that Marden was where it was really at during the Neolithic," he says. (There's a giant "super henge" under Stonehenge.)

**BP Shareholders Rebel Against CEO Bob Dudley's Pay Package**

In a historic and unprecedented move, a majority of BP PLC's shareholders voted against the company's executive pay policy, including a 20% increase in CEO Bob Dudley's total pay for 2015. The nonbinding vote, which saw 59% of investors reject the pay decisions, is a stinging rebuke to Dudley and his board, who have been under increasing pressure to navigate the company through the low oil price environment.

**Background to the Pay Package**

Dudley's pay package was criticized by several shareholder advisory groups, including Glass Lewis, which recommended that shareholders vote against the payment plans. The company's compensation committee awards executive bonuses based on the company's performance in several strategic areas, including its safety record and internal targets for operational cash flow and underlying profits. Dudley's pay was defended by the company, which said that he had met or exceeded every one of the targets he had been set.

**Shareholder Reaction**

Shareholders who criticized the pay deal included Aberdeen Asset Management, Royal London Asset Management, and investor group Sharesoc, which branded the pay deal "simply too high". Several shareholders publicly criticized the pay increase at the company's annual general meeting in London, with one senior corporate governance investor saying that the company had "ignored warnings" and that it was "time for people to step down".

**BP's Response**

BP's Chairman Carl-Henric Svanberg promised to review the terms on which pay is awarded in consultation with shareholders before next year. The company defended the pay rise, saying that Dudley had met or exceeded every one of the targets he had been set. However, after the vote, Svanberg said that the company wouldn't adjust Dudley's pay.

**Industry-Wide Pressure**

The BP vote is part of a growing trend of institutional investors and advisers around the world taking a more aggressive stance over pay. US banks from Citigroup to Bank of America have faced pressure to toughen bonus "clawback" regimes, which put executives on the hook for future losses. The Institute of Directors said the shareholder rebellion would "determine the future of corporate governance in the UK".

**Conclusion**

The BP vote is a significant moment in the debate over executive pay, and highlights the growing pressure on companies to navigate the low oil price environment while also keeping their shareholders happy. The company's response to the vote, including Svanberg's promise to review the pay terms, suggests that the company is taking the concerns of its shareholders seriously.
details.ground_truth: Oil giant BP lost $5.2 billion last year, but the company somehow saw fit to propose maximum bonuses for 2015 for its top executives, including a 20% pay increase for CEO Bob Dudley—a proposal that 59% of shareholders roundly rejected by proxy vote at Thursday's annual meeting, MarketWatch reports. The company had indicated earlier in the day that it may also have to reduce its dividend, the Wall Street Journal reports. Dudley is set to receive the full bonus he was eligible for, which comes to $4.2 million (including $1.4 million in cash and a portion in deferred BP shares), per an earlier MarketWatch report. This amount was bumped up from the $3 million ($1 million in cash) he received in 2014. BP's CFO was also on the list to rake in his full bonus. "We think it sends the wrong message," a rep for shareholder Royal London Asset Management tells the BBC. "It shows that the board is out of touch." Not only did BP suffer straight-up monetary losses as the price of oil fell, it also announced it will be getting rid of about 7,000 jobs and taking other belt-tightening measures. And the Financial Times notes that other energy company execs saw their pay slashed in 2015. But a BP spokesman says "executives performed strongly in a difficult environment in 2015, managing the things they could control and for which they were accountable." Andy Critchlow, writing for the Reuters Breakingviews blog, agrees. "Dudley has to work harder than his predecessors," he writes, noting the CEO has helped the company recover from the 2010 Deepwater Horizon disaster and improved safety protocol. "The mild-mannered American has had possibly the toughest job in the oil industry. His rewards look in line with that task." Carl-Henric Svanberg, chairman of BP's board, says the nonbinding shareholder vote won't alter the payouts they've already decided on, but that the board will take investors' concerns into account when coming up with next year's compensation packages.

**Peru's Top Investigator Put on Leave for Misleading Public on Serial Killers**

In a shocking turn of events, Peru's top organized crime investigator, Eusebio Felix, has been put on leave from his job after he misled the country by claiming to have caught a gang of serial killers who acted out an ancient Andean legend and sold their victims' fat. The legend, known as the "Pishtacos," tells the story of mythical killers who roam the Andes mountains extracting fat from travelers.

According to Felix, the gang murdered up to 60 people and exported their fat to Italian cosmetics makers for thousands of dollars a liter. However, police in Lima, the capital, later admitted that the investigation had been botched and that there may have been only one victim. The police also displayed what they said was human fat stored in an empty bottle of Inca Kola, a popular soft drink in the Andes, and a video of police pulling body parts from a shallow grave at a house in the mountainous region of Huanuco.

The police's account has been widely criticized, with anthropologists saying that investigators foolishly believed the Pishtacos legend when searching for a motive for the murder, and then played on people's fears by turning the legend into reality. Doctors have also dismissed the theory that the fat was sold for thousands of dollars in the European black market, saying that human fat has no monetary value and injecting it from one person to another would be potentially life-threatening.

The police's actions have also been criticized for scaring away tourists, with politicians blaming the police for the negative impact on the country's tourism industry. The incident has raised questions about the credibility of the police and the need for a more thorough and transparent investigation into the case.

**Key Points:**

* Peru's top organized crime investigator, Eusebio Felix, put on leave from his job for misleading the country on serial killers
* The legend of the "Pishtacos" tells the story of mythical killers who roam the Andes mountains extracting fat from travelers
* Police in Lima admitted that the investigation had been botched and that there may have been only one victim
* The police's account has been widely criticized, with anthropologists and doctors dismissing the theory that the fat was sold for thousands of dollars
* The police's actions have been criticized for scaring away tourists and damaging the country's tourism industry
details.ground_truth: The Peruvian cop who claimed to have busted a crime ring that killed dozens of people to harvest their fat has been suspended for lying. Felix Murga, the country's top organized crime investigator, said that a gang had killed 60 people to sell their fat at $15,000 a liter. Investigators now believe there was just one victim and his murder was linked to drug trafficking, the BBC reports. Murga appears to have revived an ancient Andean legend of killers who roam the mountains extracting fat from travelers, say authorities, who blame him for damaging the police force's reputation and scaring tourists away. "This has been a ruse of bad taste," a local politician tells Reuters.

**Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) Sees Increased Interest and Crowds**

The Pacific Crest Trail, a 2,650-mile hiking route from the Mexican border to the Canadian border, has seen a significant increase in interest and crowds in recent years. The release of the book and movie "Wild" by Cheryl Strayed, which tells the story of her 1,100-mile hike on the PCT, has been credited as a major factor in the surge in popularity.

**Increased Crowds and Environmental Concerns**

The number of long-distance hiking permits issued on the PCT has increased from 300 in 2006 to over 4,000 in 2016. This has raised concerns about the environmental impact of the increased crowds, including degraded vegetation, establishment of new camp spots, and human waste. The U.S. Forest Service has warned that deserts are fragile ecosystems and that damage can last longer.

**Financial Costs and Rescues**

The increased number of hikers on the PCT has also led to a rise in financial costs, including helicopter evacuations and searches. In 2016, there were 20 rescues in the first 50 miles of the trail, and the PCTA has warned that hikers should be prepared for the challenges of the trail.

**PCTA's Permit System**

The Pacific Crest Trail Association (PCTA) introduced a permit system in 2015 to spread out the number of hikers and mitigate the environmental impact. The system allows for 50 permits per day, but some hikers have expressed concerns about the honor system system and the potential for overcrowding.

**Hiking Community's Response**

The hiking community has been discussing the issue of increased numbers and pressures on the trail for over a decade, but the current permit system was only introduced in response to the surge in popularity. Many hikers, including those who have completed the trail in the past, have expressed concerns about the impact of the increased crowds on the trail experience.

**Conclusion**

The Pacific Crest Trail has seen a significant increase in interest and crowds in recent years, but this has raised concerns about the environmental and financial impact of the increased numbers. The PCTA's permit system has been introduced to mitigate the impact, but some hikers have expressed concerns about the system. As the trail continues to grow in popularity, it is essential that hikers, the PCTA, and the U.S. Forest Service work together to ensure that the trail is protected and preserved for future generations.
details.ground_truth: Hikers on the Pacific Crest Trail can expect to encounter harsh-but-beautiful landscapes, extreme fatigue, and—these days, at least—traffic. "You can’t talk about the Pacific Crest Trail without mentioning the crowds," Pete Brook writes for Outside. He should know: Brook currently is trekking the 2,650-mile PCT, which runs from the Mexican border through California, Oregon, and Washington to the border with Canada. But don't take his word for it: The Pacific Crest Trail Association so far this year has issued more than 4,000 permits to people planning on tackling at least 500 miles of the trail. To put things in perspective: 300 people attempted to complete the entire PCT in 2006, according to the AP, with about 120 of those making it. In 2014, 1,000 people set off and about half completed the trail. More traffic means more stress on the land, more improperly disposed of poop, and more inexperienced hikers requiring rescue. So what's with the increase in traffic over the past few years? You can thank Cheryl Strayed and her enormously popular memoir-turned-major-motion-picture Wild. After the book came out in 2012, there was a small increase in interest in the trail, the PCTA's Jack Haskel told the AP in 2015. But after the movie, starring Reese Witherspoon, was released in 2014, interest in the PCT exploded. "Millions are hearing about it now and are being inspired," Haskel said. Strayed, more or less unprepared for the journey, hiked 1,100 miles of the trail as a form of therapy as she grappled with personal demons. "However for others that follow her strategy of deliberate ineptitude, things might not turn out so well," writes DontHikeLikeWild.org. "Our advice to would-be hikers: Grow a spine first. Then get out there and hike the PCT." (This female hiker is the "biggest badass you've never heard of.")

**Pete Davidson's Journey to Sobriety and Medical Marijuana Use**

Pete Davidson, a cast member of the popular late-night sketch show Saturday Night Live (SNL), has recently opened up about his struggles with addiction and his reliance on medical marijuana to manage his Crohn's disease.

In an interview with HIGH TIMES, Davidson revealed that he has been sober for the first time in eight years, crediting his newfound sobriety to the support of his loved ones and his own determination to overcome his addiction. He also expressed his gratitude for the love and support of his fans, stating that they are a "family" and that he will always be there for them.

Davidson's use of medical marijuana to manage his Crohn's disease has been well-documented. He has stated that the only thing that helps him eat and perform on SNL is marijuana, and that he wouldn't be able to do the show if he wasn't high. He has also credited Kid Cudi's music with saving his life, stating that the rapper's music helped him through a difficult time and gave him hope.

Davidson's sobriety and medical marijuana use have been met with praise from his colleagues and fans. Lorne Michaels, the creator of SNL, has stated that Davidson is "really focused" despite his love of marijuana, and that he is a "proud and productive pot person." Davidson's first hour-long stand-up special, "SMD," is set to air on Comedy Central on October 29th, and the new season of SNL premiers on October 1st.

Overall, Pete Davidson's journey to sobriety and his reliance on medical marijuana to manage his Crohn's disease are a testament to the power of determination and the importance of seeking help when needed. His openness and honesty about his struggles have inspired his fans and given them hope, and his continued success on SNL and in his stand-up career is a testament to his hard work and dedication.
details.ground_truth: Noticed Pete Davidson's absence from SNL as of late? Well, there's a good reason behind it: The 23-year-old has been focusing on his sobriety. After telling High Times last fall that he couldn't function without the medical marijuana he's prescribed for Crohn's disease, Davidson now says, "I quit drugs and am happy and sober for the first time in eight years" in an Instagram post. Rapper Kid Cudi might have inspired that sobriety, per the New York Daily News. Last fall, Davidson told a radio station, "I would have killed myself if I didn't have Kid Cudi," who'd checked himself into rehab a few weeks earlier.

details.ground_truth: Bryan Singer has already been hit with two lawsuits alleging sexual abuse of minors—and the lawyer who filed both of them, plus other suits against Hollywood bigwigs, says more are coming. "I’ve heard from victims about very recent claims of being sexually exploited in Hollywood," Jeff Herman said at a news conference yesterday, according to Variety. **Double Shooting in Portland, Texas: A Family's Fight for Recovery and Justice**

On June 23, 2012, a senseless act of violence shook the small town of Portland, Texas, when 19-year-old Mollie Judith Olgin was killed and 18-year-old Mary Kristene Chapa was left critically injured after being shot in the head. The two women, who were in a same-sex relationship, were shot in a grassy area of Violet Andrews Park while waiting to see a movie.

The investigation into the shooting is being led by the Portland Police Department, with assistance from the Texas Rangers. While there is no evidence to suggest that the attack was a hate crime, the police are not ruling it out. Mollie Olgin's father, Mario, has expressed confidence that his daughter's killer will be brought to justice and has stated that "justice will be served."

Mary Kristene Chapa, who is making an "amazing" recovery, according to her brother Hilario, has been able to communicate with her family through sign language. She has made physical progress on her right side, but her left side remains unresponsive. The family is waiting for a response from her left side, and her doctors have stated that it's too early to use the word "paralyzed."

The Chapa family, who are of Mexican-American descent and live in Sinton, Texas, have set up a donation page on WePay to help pay for Mary Kristene's medical bills, as they are without insurance. The family of Mollie Olgin has reached out to the Chapa family, but a meeting has not yet taken place due to the emotional toll of the shooting on Mary Kristene's mother.

The community has come together to support the families of the two women. A candlelight vigil will be held at 6 p.m. on Friday at Violet Andrews Park, and a memorial service for Mollie Olgin will be held at 4:30 p.m. at Limbaugh Funeral Home in Portland.

As the investigation continues and Mary Kristene Chapa fights for her recovery, the community remains united in their support and their demand for justice.
details.ground_truth: The dad of the teenage lesbian shot dead in a Texas park called for justice yesterday. He immediately had a "bad feeling" when his 19-year-old daughter, Mollie, failed to show up for work, said Mario Olgin. “It was not like Mollie," he told KIII-TV. "If she had some place to be she was going to be there.” Mollie and her 18-year-old girlfriend, Mary Kristene Chapa, had been shot in the head by an unknown assailant. Mollie died at the scene, but Chapa is making an "amazing" recovery, her brother told NBC Latino. Mollie has just finished her first semester of college and dreamed of becoming a psychiatrist. "She was happy," Olgin recalled. Now, "she's my guardian angel. I know she's looking down on us, in a better place." He's confident police will find her killer. "Justice will be served," he declared. Texas Rangers have joined Portland police in continuing to investigate the crime, but haven't yet discussed any progress. They haven't labeled the shootings a hate crime, but aren't ruling it out.

**Scientists Discover Iridescent Dinosaur with Rainbow Feathers**

A team of scientists has made a groundbreaking discovery in northeastern China, uncovering the fossilized remains of a dinosaur with iridescent feathers that shimmered and shifted in the light, similar to those of hummingbirds. The dinosaur, named Caihong juji, or "rainbow with a big crest," lived 161 million years ago during the Jurassic Period and was a small, bird-like creature with a crested head that resembled a Velociraptor.

**Unique Features of Caihong juji**

Caihong juji had several distinct features that set it apart from other dinosaurs. Its feathers were iridescent, with colors that shimmered and shifted in the light, and were likely used for display and social or sexual cues, similar to the peacock's tail. The dinosaur also had a bird-like body, including the sorts of feathers required for flight, but its crested head and sharp teeth suggest that it was a predator that hunted small mammals and lizards.

**Fossilized Feathers and Melanosomes**

The fossilized remains of Caihong juji were found in exceptional conditions, allowing the dinosaur's feathers to fossilize along with its bones. Scientists were able to sample 66 sites across the fossil and compare the melanosomes they saw with melanosomes from modern bird feathers. They found that the melanosomes on Caihong's head, chest, and parts of its tail were long, flat, and organized into sheets, similar to those in the iridescent throat feathers of hummingbirds.

**Evolutionary Significance of Caihong juji**

The discovery of Caihong juji provides new insights into the evolution of feathers and their use in dinosaurs. Scientists believe that feathers originally evolved for display and social or sexual cues, and later took on the traits needed for flight. The discovery of Caihong juji, which lived 161 million years ago, is significant because it is the oldest known animal to have asymmetrical feathers, a key adaptation for flight.

**Future Research and Implications**

The discovery of Caihong juji has many implications for our understanding of the evolution of feathers and their use in dinosaurs. Scientists hope to learn more about how Caihong juji used its iridescent feathers, and whether males or females of the species had different plumage. Further study of the new fossil and perhaps even more bones from Caihong and other feathered dinosaurs will be necessary to fully understand the
details.ground_truth: Scientists are now saying there may have been "a more colorful Jurassic World than we previously imagined," thanks to the recent discovery of a fossil in China's Hebei province. Reuters reports that a closer look at the "exquisitely preserved," almost completely intact fossil of a crow-like dinosaur that lived about 161 million years ago revealed that Caihong juji had luminous, brilliantly hued feathers that closely resembled those of hummingbirds. The study in the journal Nature Communications documenting the find explains that "Caihong" means "rainbow" in Mandarin. Scientists think the colorful plumage, which appeared to have covered the bony-crested creature's head, neck, and chest areas, may have kept the dinosaur warm, as well as attracted potential mates; National Geographic compares it to peacock feathers. So how were researchers able to tell what color feathers the creature had from preserved bones? They used high-tech microscopes able to home in on 66 sites on the fossil, detecting tiny cell structures called melanosomes, which underlie pigmentation. Which colors they led to can be found by their shapes, and the melanosomes in Caihong were long and flat like pancakes—similar to those found in hummingbirds, which boast iridescent plumage. Although they can't pick out from the Pantone wheel Caihong's exact colors, researchers say the creature likely sported feathers with a "rainbow glimmer." Study co-author Xing Zu tells National Geographic that Caihong was a predator that spent its days gliding from tree to tree. And "glide" is the operative word, as this dinosaur likely didn't fly: Its feathers were located on its tail, not on its wings like birds, Discover notes. (A study postulated that nearly all dinosaurs had feathers.)

**Middle East Protests: A Region in Turmoil**

The Middle East is witnessing a wave of protests and unrest, with several countries experiencing violent clashes between government forces and demonstrators. The uprisings, which began in Tunisia and Egypt, have spread to Libya, Yemen, Algeria, and Bahrain, with each country facing its unique set of challenges and grievances.

**Libya: A Bloody Crackdown**

In Libya, the government's response to the protests has been brutal and violent. Security forces fired on mourners in the city of Benghazi, killing at least 15 people and wounding many more. The protests, which have been ongoing for several days, are against the 42-year rule of Colonel Muammar Gaddafi. The international community has condemned the violence, with the US and European allies calling for an end to the bloodshed.

**Yemen: A 10th Day of Protests**

In Yemen, the protests against President Ali Abdullah Saleh have entered their 10th day. Riot police opened fire on thousands of protesters, killing one person and injuring five others. The protests are against the 30-year rule of Saleh, who has promised not to seek re-election in 2013. However, the protesters are demanding more concrete reforms and an end to the corruption and repression that have characterized his rule.

**Bahrain: A Symbolic Square Reclaimed**

In Bahrain, the protesters have reclaimed the symbolic Pearl Square in the capital city of Manama. The square was the heart of the anti-government demonstrations, which began with calls to weaken the Sunni monarchy's power and address claims of discrimination against the Shiite majority. The royal family, under international pressure, has withdrawn its forces from the square, but the protesters remain wary of their intentions.

**Algeria: A Thwarted Rally**

In Algeria, the police thwarted a rally by thousands of pro-democracy supporters, breaking up the crowd into isolated groups to keep them from marching. The protesters, who are demanding an end to the state of emergency and the corruption that has characterized the rule of President Abdelaziz Bouteflika, are facing a long-standing ban on protests in Algiers.

**A Region in Turmoil**

The Middle East protests are a symptom of a deeper malaise, characterized by corruption, repression, and poverty. The region's wealthier countries, such as Libya and Bahrain, are facing the same challenges as the poorer ones, with the protesters demanding an end to the autocratic rule and the establishment of democratic institutions. details.ground_truth: In direct contrast to the "delirious joy" in Bahrain, the death toll keeps climbing in Libya's protests. Moammar Gadhafi's minions killed another 20 people today, bringing the five-day total to at least 104, says Human Rights Watch. Gadhafi has effectively shut off Internet service and forbid media coverage, but witnesses told AP of attacks by police and government loyalists wielding guns, knives, and even anti-aircraft missiles. While the accounts are impossible to verify—and at least one puts the death toll closer to 200—a grim cycle has emerged, reports the New York Times: "Security forces fire on funeral marches, killing more protesters and creating more funerals." In Yemen, meanwhile, President Ali Abdullah Saleh's forces also opened fire on protesters, killing at least one, reports AP.

**Rare Nirvana Discoveries and Internet Archive Efforts**

In recent news, two distinct stories have captured the attention of music enthusiasts and the online community. The first involves the unearthing of rare photographs from Nirvana's first concert in March 1987. Maggie Poukkula, the 19-year-old daughter of Tony Poukkula, a member of the Seattle band Laytem who hosted the fledgling Nirvana in his basement, shared the photographs on Twitter. The pictures show Nirvana's original lineup, consisting of Kurt Cobain, Krist Novoselic, and Aaron Burckhard, with Poukkula joining in on guitar for Led Zeppelin covers. The discovery comes as part of a larger trend of rare Cobain and Nirvana artifacts being shared, including those featured in the acclaimed documentary Montage of Heck.

The second story involves the efforts of the Internet Archive to preserve and fix broken links on the web. The organization is using crawls to archive pages as they are created and the pages they refer to, allowing for a link to the original version to be preserved even if the referenced page is changed or removed. The goal is to create a more stable and reliable online environment, with the organization hoping that references to archived pages will be used in place of broken links.

**Key Dates:**

- August 7th: Montage of Heck returns to movie theaters
- July 16, 2015: Maggie Poukkula shares the rare Nirvana photographs on Twitter

**Related Resources:**

- Rolling Stone: "Rare Nirvana Photos from First Concert in 1987"
- Internet Archive: "No More 404" project
- Montage of Heck: documentary documentary featuring rare Cobain and Nirvana artifacts
details.ground_truth: Most teens don't find music history in their dad's stuff, but Maggie Poukkula's dad is Seattle musician Tony Poukkula, who was a friend of Kurt Cobain. Her find? Photos of Nirvana's first concert, performed by then 20-year-old Cobain and his new band in March 1987 in Tony Poukkula's basement. "My dad showed me them awhile back, but he never mentioned that's what was going on in the photos," Maggie Poukkula, 19, tells Rolling Stone. "I found out because of all the articles. I didn't realize it was such a historical thing. I thought they were just cool pictures of my dad and Kurt jamming together." She tweeted the photos last week, and the post quickly went viral. That first concert featured the early Nirvana lineup of Cobain, bassist Krist Novoselic, and drummer Aaron Burckhard playing original songs and Led Zeppelin covers. Similar early-days footage of Cobain and Co. featured in the recent documentary Montage of Heck has renewed interest in the grunge band. The Emmy-nominated project includes home movies, never-before-heard music, and intimate interviews with Cobain's family and friends, notes SPIN. After being critically acclaimed at festivals across the country, the film will return to theaters on Aug. 7, adds Rolling Stone. (Want a little more Cobain history? Here's the mix tape he made at 21.)

Clarissa Dickson Wright, the renowned British chef, food historian, and television personality, has passed away at the age of 66. Born Clarissa Theresa Philomena Aileen Mary Josephine Agnes Elsie Trilby Louise Esmerelda Dickson Wright on June 24, 1947, in the St. John's Wood section of London, she rose to fame as one half of the BBC's Two Fat Ladies partnership with Jennifer Paterson.

The duo's show, which debuted in 1996 and ran until 1999, was known for its irreverent humor, eccentricity, and indulgent recipes. Dickson Wright and Paterson traveled the UK in a motorcycle-sidecar, cooking and sharing their love of food with the nation. The show's success led to a book, "Cooking With the Two Fat Ladies," which was reviewed by The New York Times as a "cookbook that purports to be useful" but was often impossible to follow.

After Paterson's death in 1999, Dickson Wright went on to front several TV shows, including Breakfast, Lunch and Dinner in 2012. She was a champion of countryside pursuits and was prepared to go to prison to support people's right to hunt. In 2009, she was convicted of attending an illegal hare coursing event.

Dickson Wright was also an outspoken campaigner for rural life and a keen supporter of the Countryside Alliance. She accused the BBC of dropping her and the show because she went too far in praise of hunting, angering then-Prime Minister Tony Blair. She caused a storm by declaring people should eat the meat of badgers that had been culled and even suggested ways to cook the animal.

Throughout her life, Dickson Wright struggled with addiction and was declared bankrupt several times after spending her £2.8 million inheritance on "drinking and debauchery." However, she credited her AA meetings with helping her achieve 27 years of sobriety and said, "I don't mind people drinking. I keep a very good cellar for my friends in my house - it's only me who doesn't drink."

Dickson Wright's friends and fans will miss her fun, laughter, and intelligence, which made her a commanding presence on television. She was a true original, and her legacy will continue to inspire and entertain food lovers around the world.
details.ground_truth: Both of TV's "Fat Ladies" have sung. Clarissa Dickson Wright, one half of the BBC's "Two Fat Ladies" cooking duo, died in Edinburgh Saturday at age 66, the BBC reports. Wright was a former lawyer who filmed four of the "Fat Ladies" series, going on food-related road trips across the UK in a motorbike and sidecar with Jennifer Paterson, before Paterson died in 1999 from cancer. The New York Times describes Wright as a "rebel," both hosts as "irreverent and eccentric," and the recipes as "sometimes confounding." Wright's eclectic working life also included stints as a cook, an author, and a cookbook shop manager; she also ran a catering business, was a guild butcher, and once worked on a yacht in the Caribbean. In fact, she recently said, "I've had a fantastic life and I've done everything I could have wanted to do and more." It wasn't until her 40s, after she'd recovered from alcoholism, that she got into cooking seriously. As for the perhaps-controversial title of the show that brought her fame? "If you're fat you're fat," she once said. "I hate this modern-day political correctness, that you don't call things by their proper name." Her agent remembers Wright similarly in a statement: "Loved dearly by her friends and many fans all over the world, Clarissa was utterly non-PC and fought for what she believed in, always, with no thought to her own personal cost." There's no word on Wright's cause of death, but the Guardian reports that she had been undergoing treatment at a hospital since the beginning of the year.

**Mountain Walker's Fatal Fall: Inquest into Tryfan Incident Reveals Safety Concerns**

A 53-year-old experienced mountain walker, Jane Wilson, fell to her death while attempting to find a safe route off Snowdonia's Tryfan peak in March 2023. An inquest into the incident has revealed that the couple, who were both experienced walkers, relied on a smartphone app to navigate the mountain, which may have contributed to the tragedy.

According to the inquest, Jane and her husband Gary decided not to reach the summit of Tryfan due to the approaching dusk. They then used Gary's phone to find a route down, but Jane went ahead to look for a suitable path. When Gary asked if it was safe, Jane replied that they would only go if they both agreed. However, a short time later, Gary heard a cry and the sound of a rock fall, and realized that his wife had fallen.

Jane's body was found over 150 meters lower down, with a fractured skull and other severe injuries. The inquest heard that the couple had been mountain walking and scrambling for six years, and that Jane was a fit and active woman who had even completed the New York Marathon and the Great North Run.

The incident has raised safety concerns about the use of smartphone apps for navigation in the mountains. Chris Lloyd, a member of the Ogwen Rescue team, described the route the couple took as "not a straightforward path," with "tricky little rock steps" at the top end. Andy Simpson, a spokesman for Mountain Rescue England and Wales, urged walkers to use a map and compass in addition to electronic navigation aids, citing the potential difficulties of poor detail on an electronic map and the risk of batteries running out at the wrong time.

The coroner recorded a conclusion of accidental death, and the incident has served as a reminder of the importance of careful planning and navigation when venturing into the mountains.
details.ground_truth: A hiker's fatal fall in March has been blamed on using a smartphone app map instead of an actual map. The BBC reports Jane Wilson and her husband Gary were hiking on Tryfan in Wales when it started to get dark and they decided to skip the summit. While attempting to descend the mountain, Gary was looking at an Ordnance Survey app while Jane went a short way ahead to make sure the path was safe, according to the Telegraph. The Manchester Evening News reports Gary heard his wife fall, got to safety, and called for help. Rescuers found the 53-year-old librarian's body at the bottom of a 30-foot cliff with a fractured skull. At a hearing Friday, Jane's was ruled an accidental death due, in part, to using an app instead of a map. "Apart from potential difficulties caused by poor detail on an electronic map, batteries on mobile phones have a nasty habit of running out just when you need them most," the Telegraph quotes a spokesperson for Mountain Rescue England and Wales as saying. A detective says the app map "would have been small and not as detailed" and took the couple on a route that was not the safest. A director with Ordnance Survey says hikers should always use the app in conjunction with an actual map "due to the nature of mobile devices when navigating the real world." Authorities also say Jane wasn't wearing appropriate footwear for the hike. (Go inside the futile search for "Inchworm" on the trail.)

**Summary of Recent News Developments**

**Politics and Elections**

The 2012 presidential election is heating up, with several key figures weighing in on the candidates and the issues. New York Mayor Michael Bloomberg praised President Barack Obama as a "credible, formidable candidate" for reelection, while also criticizing his use of billionaire investor Warren Buffett as an example for why taxes should be increased on the wealthy. Bloomberg also praised Texas Gov. Rick Perry for his 2007 executive order requiring sixth-grade girls to receive the HPV vaccine and said Mitt Romney's health care plan for Massachusetts worked. Indiana Gov. Mitch Daniels, who considered a White House run himself, says fellow Republicans should forgive Perry's uneven debate performances.

**Republican National Committee**

Republican National Committee Chairman Reince Priebus mocked President Obama's 2008 election slogan, arguing it won't pass the smell test with voters in 2012. He also said that the Democratic National Committee's talking points have already been proved false, referring to the recent GOP victory in New York's special House election.

**Congressional Stalemate**

Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) called the specter of a government shutdown because Congress can't agree to another short-term spending bill "embarrassing" and blamed tea party-affiliated Republicans for the stalemate. Sen. Lamar Alexander (R-Tenn.) blamed Majority Leader Harry Reid (D-Nev.) for holding up the bill.
