Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) says the United States needs to leave "all options on the table" in dealing with Pakistan after U.S. military officials accused the country's intelligence officials of assisting terrorist operations. President Obama's senior adviser, David Plouffe, said the U.S. may continue reducing aid to Pakistan if it can't do more to impede anti-American terrorism being conducted by the Haqqani terror network.

**Jobs and the Economy**

President Obama's senior adviser, David Plouffe, pushed for Congress to pass Obama's American Jobs Act, saying Obama is "confident in his team, in the direction we've laid out here." He also said he doesn't expect significant changes in top White House staff or the Cabinet ahead of the 2012 reelection campaign.
details.ground_truth: Mitch Daniels has some advice for those who are writing Rick Perry's political eulogy: Hold your horses. "It's way too early to know, or to issue, to pronounce last rites over one performance," Daniels said today. "There's still many of these (debates), too many maybe. ... I'd cut him some slack and give him a little time." Politico notes that Daniels also had kind words for Democrat Sen. Mark Warner's work on the Gang of Six, saying, "It's very encouraging, by the way, to hear a guy like Sen. Warner speak to it as clearly as he just did. I wish he were president." More fun on your Sunday dial, as per Politico: Michael Bloomberg on President Obama's 2012 chances: "He would be a credible, formidable candidate. Obama is "confident in his team, in the direction we've laid out here." Warner on Congress' standoff over a short-term spending bill: "It is embarrassing. The Senate is saying, 'Why should we build schools in Iraq on the credit card but expect that rebuilding schools in Joplin, Mo.,'" will be paid for. Warner blames "a group, more centered in the House, in terms of some of these Tea Party Republicans, who say on every issue, 'We're going to make this a make-or-break.'" Seattle's Sweetened Beverage Tax: A Failure in Implementation and Policy

Seattle's sweetened beverage tax, which went into effect on January 1, 2018, has been met with widespread opposition and criticism from residents, businesses, and experts. The tax, which adds 1.75 cents per ounce to the price of sugary drinks, was touted as a way to reduce sugar consumption, raise revenue for important projects, and subsidize purchases of healthy foods by low-income families.

However, the tax has had the opposite effect, driving down sales and revenue for the city. According to a study by the Mercatus Center at George Mason University, Seattle is one of the top three states with the most paternalistic laws, and the sweetened beverage tax is a prime example of this.

Costco, a popular retail chain, has taken a creative approach to the tax by posting signs directing customers to locations outside the city limits where they can buy the product without the massive tax. Already, there is evidence that people are voting with their feet and buying drinks in the suburbs, depriving Seattle of the revenue it claims to need.

The tax has also been criticized for its regressive nature, disproportionately affecting low-income families. Additionally, the tax leaves out sugar-heavy beverages like those sold at Starbucks, which has rejected its hometown tax.

In a similar vein, Cook County's sweetened beverage tax, which was repealed in December 2018, has been met with widespread criticism and opposition. The tax, which added a penny-per-ounce tax on sugar and artificially sweetened beverages, was seen as a way to raise revenue for the county. However, it had the opposite effect, driving down sales and revenue for the county.

A bill to bring a similar tax statewide to Washington has been re-introduced, but local representatives are skeptical about its chances of passing. Some business owners, like Heather Baldwin of King's Bar & Grill, are worried about the impact of the tax on their businesses, which rely heavily on soda sales.

Experts and critics have also pointed out the flaws in the tax, including its inclusion of artificially sweetened beverages and its lack of application to food stamp purchases. Elissa Bassler, CEO of the Illinois Public Health Institute, has criticized the tax for its failure to address the root causes of chronic diseases like obesity and diabetes.

In conclusion, Seattle's sweetened beverage tax has been a failure in implementation and policy, driving down sales and revenue for the city and disproportionately affecting low-income families. The tax has also been met with widespread criticism
details.ground_truth: "That new soda tax in Seattle is working out about as well as Chicago's," proclaims a headline at Hot Air. It's not a compliment. The tax, which went into effect Jan. 1, slaps an additional 1.75 cents on each fluid ounce of sugar-sweetened drinks, a group that includes soda, sports drinks, and kombucha. That's nearly double the one-cent levy the Chicago-encompassing Cook County tried, a tax it repealed after about two months. The Chicago Tribune reported at the time that Costco's nine affected locations saw their sales of beverages impacted by the tax fall 34%, while sales jumped 38% at locations just beyond the county line. And Costco is again at the fore of what's happening: Because bulk purchases contain significantly more ounces than an individual one, the impact is starker, as KIRO discovered when it spotted an updated Costco sign for Gatorade in Seattle. The sign details Costco's price for the 35-pack of 16-ounce bottles—$15.99—and separately lists the city's $10.34 tax, for a new total of $26.33. But that's not all the sign says: It also directs consumers to nearby locations that are outside city limits and exempt from the tax. The tax is meant to fight obesity and raise funds for worthy expenditures, but the blog for Citizens Against Government Waste sees the ends quite differently: "Let’s be clear. Soda taxes don’t make people healthier. They don't raise revenue—they drive it outside city limits. They don’t help ease inequality—they make the poor poorer." But some in government seemingly remain hopeful: KXLY reports a bill was reintroduced Monday that would push the tax statewide. (Speaking of Seattle and beverages, the world's largest Starbucks is no longer located there.)

**Tragic Birthday Party Incident: Woman Fails to Stop After Hitting Man in Front of Car**

A 34-year-old man, Jonathan Carlyle Merkley, was killed in a tragic incident at a birthday party in San Diego, California. The incident occurred on a Sunday morning, just after midnight, at the Staybridge Suites hotel on Mira Mesa Boulevard.

According to police, Merkley and his friends had been drinking alcohol before the incident. The woman, who was also at the party and had been drinking, decided to leave and get behind the wheel of her BMW. Merkley, in an attempt to prevent her from leaving, walked into the path of the car and laid down in front of it.

The woman failed to stop and ran over Merkley, causing major trauma to his chest. He was taken to a hospital, where he later died. The police know the identity of the BMW driver but have not made any arrests as of late Sunday morning.

The incident has sparked outrage and calls for the woman to turn herself in. A hotel guest, Gloria Williams, said, "I think this is crazy, we don’t value each others lives." The police have released a statement stating that they have interviewed the female friend and have a vehicle in custody pending examination to determine if it is the vehicle in question.

In the following days, the police tracked down the woman and interviewed her. A car that "may have been involved" in the crash was impounded. The investigation is ongoing, and it is unclear whether the driver knew she hit the man.

The incident has left the community in shock and has raised questions about the importance of road safety and the consequences of drinking and driving.
details.ground_truth: A birthday party in California turned tragic after the guest of honor was run over and killed by his friend. Merkley wanted her to stay and reportedly walked toward her BMW and laid down in front of what the Orange County Register describes as a moving car. The woman didn’t stop, ran him over, and kept going. Police cite witnesses as saying both had been drinking, Fox 5 reports. Merkley, who suffered major chest trauma, died about 45 minutes later at a hospital. Police tracked the woman down and a car that "may have been involved" was impounded, cops tell the San Diego Union-Tribune. The woman was not arrested but police said on Tuesday that the investigation is continuing. "There's a lot we still need to find out," says San Diego Police Sgt. Tim Underwood.

**Reality TV Star Snooki's Pregnancy and Fetal Alcohol Syndrome**

Nicole "Snooki" Polizzi, a reality TV star from the show "Jersey Shore," has announced that she is pregnant with her first child. The news comes as a surprise, as Snooki had previously denied rumors of a pregnancy on a TV appearance just a few months ago. The father of the child is reportedly Snooki's boyfriend, Jionni LaValle.

The news of Snooki's pregnancy has sparked concerns about the potential risks of fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS) in the child. FAS is a condition that occurs when a fetus is exposed to alcohol in the womb, and can result in a range of physical and developmental problems. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), FAS is the most severe form of a group of conditions known as fetal alcohol spectrum disorders (FASDs).

FASDs can result in a range of problems, including growth deficiency, facial abnormalities, and central nervous system damage. The CDC estimates that FAS affects between 0.2 and 9 per 1,000 live births in the United States, while FASDs affect between 2 and 5% of the population.

Snooki's history of heavy drinking has raised concerns about the potential risks of FAS in her child. In an interview with Ellen DeGeneres, Snooki admitted that she used to drink heavily and wake up in strange places, including a garbage can. She also confessed that she didn't want to be a role model for her child.

The news of Snooki's pregnancy has also sparked a debate about the risks of drinking during pregnancy. While some experts argue that moderate drinking is safe, others say that any amount of alcohol can pose a risk to the fetus. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) recommends that women abstain from alcohol during pregnancy, while the CDC advises that women who are trying to become pregnant or are pregnant should avoid drinking.

In conclusion, the news of Snooki's pregnancy has raised concerns about the potential risks of fetal alcohol syndrome in her child. While Snooki's history of heavy drinking has raised red flags, the debate about the risks of drinking during pregnancy continues to be a contentious issue.

**Employee Tracking App Lawsuit: A Central California Woman's Fight for Privacy**

A former sales executive for Intermex, a money-transfer service company, has filed a lawsuit against her former employer for allegedly firing her after she uninstalled an app that tracked her every move, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. Myrna Arias, who worked for Intermex from February 2014 to May 2014, claims that her boss, John Stubits, admitted to monitoring her location even when she was off the clock and bragged about knowing her driving speed and routes.

The app, Xora, was required for all employees to use on their company-issued iPhones, and Arias claims that there was no way to turn it off when she was at home. She likened the app to a prisoner's ankle bracelet and informed Stubits that his actions were illegal. However, Stubits replied that she should tolerate the illegal intrusion because Intermex was paying her more than her previous employer, NetSpend.

Arias' lawsuit, filed in Kern County Superior Court, alleges invasion of privacy, retaliation, unfair business practices, and other claims, seeking damages in excess of $500,000. Her attorney, Gail Glick, stated that Arias was one of the first employees asked to put the app on her phone and that they were essentially "guinea pigs."

The lawsuit also claims that Intermex's president and CEO, Robert Lisy, interfered with Arias' contract with NetSpend, resulting in her termination from both companies. Arias has had a hard time finding a new job due to the two black marks against her and a gap in her employment record.

This case highlights the importance of employee privacy and the need for companies to balance their legitimate business interests with their employees' right to privacy. The outcome of this case will be closely watched by employment law attorneys and companies that use similar tracking apps.

The Xora app, now known as ClickSoftware Solution, was designed to enable employees to create electronic timecards on their phones to track when their shifts start, when they take breaks, and when a shift ends. However, the app also contained a global positioning system function that tracked the exact location of the person possessing the smartphone on which it was installed.

details.ground_truth: Myrna Arias didn't like the GPS app on her phone that constantly tracked her, so she uninstalled it. The problem: Arias' iPhone was issued by her employer, which required her to run the app constantly, and after she removed it, the California woman was fired. Now she's suing her former employer, money transfer service Intermex, for invasion of privacy, unfair business practices, and retaliation, among other things, Ars Technica reports. Arias started researching the app, then called Xora—its website touts the app's ability to let employers "instantly see where their employees are and where they have been"—and eventually she and some of her colleagues asked a trainer from the app company to clarify. Boss John Stubits then, per the suit, "admitted that employees would be monitored while off duty and bragged that he knew how fast she was driving at specific moments." Arias says she has no problem being monitored while at work, but she complained to Stubits about being tracked in her free time; he was unsympathetic and said she should have no problem because of how much she was being paid by Intermex. "Her manager made it clear that he was using the program to continuously monitor her, during company as well as personal time," the complaint states, noting that he told Arias she needed to keep her phone on at all times in case a customer called. Arias' attorney tells Courthouse News that there was no way to turn the app off, as it would constantly run in the background. "She found it very offensive that they were treating her like a felon," the attorney says. Arias says her boss "scolded" her for uninstalling the program last year, and fired her soon after. (Elsewhere, a woman got fired over a text mistakenly sent to the boss.)

**Recent Freeway Shooting Incidents and Updates on Suspects**

In the past few days, there have been two significant incidents related to freeway shootings in the United States. The first incident occurred in Phoenix, Arizona, where a judge reduced the bond of Leslie Allen Merritt Jr., a man charged with four freeway shootings that took place in August and September 2015. Merritt, who has pleaded not guilty to the charges, was allowed to be released from jail under electronic monitoring.

The reduction in bond was a major victory for Merritt's defense lawyers, who have cast doubt on the ballistic tests that authorities claim link him to the crimes. The judge, Warren Granville, imposed a gag order on the lawyers, preventing them from discussing the evidence that prompted the shift in bond.

In a separate incident, a driver suspected of firing a gun at motorists on an Alabama highway and exchanging gunfire with officers was killed in a crash. The incident occurred in Blount County, about 45 miles northeast of Birmingham, and began around 7 a.m. Tuesday. The suspect's name was not released, and no other details, such as the race of the suspect or the officer, were immediately available.

The two incidents highlight the ongoing concern of freeway shootings in the United States, which can cause panic and put the lives of drivers at risk. In the Phoenix case, Merritt's lawyers have pointed to road rage shootings that took place after his arrest, suggesting that the culprit may still be at large. The Alabama incident, while tragic, serves as a reminder of the dangers of gun violence and the importance of cooperation between law enforcement and the public.

**Key Dates and Events:**

- November 16, 2015: Leslie Allen Merritt Jr. is taken into custody in connection with the Phoenix freeway shootings.
- May 18: Merritt's court date is set, with the judge telling him to show up.
- July 2024: A driver suspected of firing a gun at motorists on an Alabama highway is killed in a crash.

**Key Figures:**

- Leslie Allen Merritt Jr.: The man charged with four freeway shootings in Phoenix, Arizona.
- Warren Granville: The judge who reduced Merritt's bond and imposed a gag order on the lawyers.
- Jason Lamm: Merritt's defense lawyer, who has cast doubt on the ballistic tests that link his client to the crimes.
details.ground_truth: A judge on Tuesday allowed a man charged in freeway shootings that rattled Phoenix last year to be released from jail amid questions about evidence authorities say links him to the crimes. The judge overseeing the case of Leslie Merritt Jr. reduced his bond to zero and said he can return to his home under electronic monitoring, the AP reports. He was expected to be released later in the day. The reduction of the bond—once $1 million—was a major victory as defense lawyers contend that ballistic tests cast doubt on the claim by authorities that Merritt was behind four of the freeway shootings. "With all due respect your honor, there's no evidence against him to show he's responsible for this," defense lawyer Jason Lamm said. "He is no more the I-10 shooter than, respectfully, you are." Merritt lifted up his shackled arms in celebration as he walked from the courtroom. After the hearing, family members hugged and shook the hands of defense lawyers. Merritt has pleaded not guilty to drive-by shooting, aggravated assault, and other charges. The shootings caused panic on Phoenix-area freeways, where 11 vehicles were hit in August and September. Detectives took Merritt into custody on Sept. 18; in court the next day, Merritt adamantly denied shooting any cars, telling the judge, "I'm the wrong guy." His lawyers immediately began raising questions about the evidence, citing ballistics information and phone records they say provided an alibi for their client. (Also Tuesday, a motorist suspected of shooting at other vehicles on an Alabama highway died after a shootout with officers.)

In the past 24 hours, the United States has witnessed a disturbing and tragic series of shootings, leaving a trail of death and injury in its wake. **New Study Suggests Acetaminophen May Increase Risk of ADHD in Children**

A recent study published in the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA) Pediatrics has found that pregnant women who frequently take acetaminophen, commonly found in over-the-counter pain relievers such as Tylenol, may be at a higher risk of having children diagnosed with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and similar disorders.

The study, which analyzed data from over 64,000 children in the Danish National Birth Cohort study, found that women who reported taking acetaminophen for longer periods and later in pregnancy were associated with higher risks of their children receiving ADHD medication and exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors. The study's authors concluded that prenatal exposure to acetaminophen may increase the risk of a child being diagnosed with a severe form of ADHD called hyperkinetic disorder (HKD).

While the study does not show a cause-and-effect relationship between acetaminophen and ADHD, it highlights the importance of not taking a drug's safety during pregnancy for granted. The study's authors suggest that acetaminophen may interfere with maternal hormones that are critical for fetal brain development, citing a previous study done using acetaminophen in rats and a study of acetaminophen and autism done with humans.

**Experts Weigh in on the Study's Findings**

Dr. Beate Ritz, a professor and chair of the epidemiology department at the University of California, Los Angeles Fielding School of Public Health, says that pregnant women shouldn't worry at this point, but that they should try to avoid taking painkillers as much as they can until more is known about the association. Dr. Jeffrey Chapa, head of maternal-fetal medicine at Cleveland Clinic Children's Hospital, suggests that nonpharmacological ways to deal with pain, such as massages, baths, and acupuncture, may be a better alternative to medications.

**What to Do if You're Pregnant and Need to Take Acetaminophen**

The study's findings do not mean that pregnant women should never take acetaminophen. However, they may want to avoid using it frequently until more studies have been done. If you're pregnant and need to take acetaminophen, follow these guidelines:

* Take the smallest dose possible for the shortest amount of time necessary.
* Avoid taking acetaminophen for chronic or long-term pain.
* Consider nonpharmacological ways to deal with pain, such as massages, baths, and acupuncture.
*
details.ground_truth: The bad news about acetaminophen just keeps on coming: A new study finds that use of the drug during pregnancy is linked to "ADHD-like behavioral problems" in children, CNN reports. The Danish study looked at data from more than 64,000 children and found that those who had prenatal exposure to acetaminophen had a 13% higher risk of exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors, a 29% higher risk of being prescribed ADHD medication, and a 37% higher risk of receiving a diagnosis of hyperkinetic disorder, a severe form of ADHD, USA Today reports. The risk increased the later in pregnancy the drug was taken and the longer it was taken. Women who used acetaminophen for 20 weeks or longer had a 50% higher chance of their children being prescribed ADHD medication. But the authors did not find a cause-and-effect relationship, and they note that "exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors" is not the same as actually having ADHD. Right now, doctors consider acetaminophen the "safest" pain reliever for pregnant women, NBC News reports; the study author says pregnant women "shouldn't worry at this point." What could account for the link? The authors think acetaminophen may interfere with key maternal hormones that impact fetal brain development. Another recent study also found that frequent acetaminophen use during pregnancy was linked with a 70% higher risk of behavioral problems in children.

**Bill Cosby Sentenced to Prison, Accuses Racism and Sexism in the Justice System**

On September 25, 2018, Bill Cosby, the 81-year-old actor and comedian, was sentenced to three to 10 years in state prison for drugging and sexually assaulting a woman, Andrea Constand, at his home outside Philadelphia in 2004. The conviction marked a significant milestone in the #MeToo era, which has seen numerous high-profile figures accused of sexual misconduct.

In a bizarre and fiery press conference outside the courthouse, Cosby's publicist, Andrew Wyatt, accused the judge and the justice system of racism and sexism, comparing Cosby's downfall to the accusations of sexual misconduct against Judge Brett M. Kavanaugh, the embattled Supreme Court nominee. Wyatt claimed that Cosby was a victim of a "sex war" and that his trial was the "most racist and sexist trial in the history of the United States."

Wyatt's comments sparked a heated debate, with many critics accusing him of trying to deflect attention from Cosby's crimes and manipulate the public's perception of the justice system. Cosby's team has repeatedly invoked the persecution of Jesus and Emmett Till, a black boy murdered after a white woman falsely accused him of whistling at her, to try to garner sympathy and paint Cosby as a victim of systemic racism.

**Cosby Begins Serving Sentence at SCI Phoenix**

On September 25, 2018, Cosby was taken away in handcuffs and began serving his sentence at SCI Phoenix, a new 3,830-bed state prison in suburban Philadelphia. The Department of Corrections says he will be allowed phone calls and visitation, as well as the opportunity to exercise during the classification process. Cosby is being housed in a single cell in a unit adjacent to the infirmary, and will be required to receive monthly counseling as a "sexually violent predator."

The decision to send Cosby to SCI Phoenix was made after prosecutors said correction officials would assess Cosby's needs and safety issues to determine where it would be best for the 81-year-old Cosby, who is legally blind, to complete his sentence.
details.ground_truth: Bill Cosby was labeled a "violent sexual predator" by the judge who sentenced him to three to 10 years in prison on Tuesday, but his publicist had a different description: one of the "greatest civil rights leaders" in history and one of the "greatest educators of men and boys." Outside the courtroom in Norristown, Pa., publicist Andrew Wyatt claimed the 81-year-old Cosby had faced the "most racist and sexist trial" in American history, the New York Times reports. Wyatt claimed that three psychologists who testified against Cosby in the sexual assault trial were trying to "make money off of accusing black men of being sexual predators." He also read a statement from Camille Cosby, the disgraced entertainer's wife, alleging that an audio recording played in the trial had been doctored. Wyatt, who previously called Cosby's trial a "public lynching," accused Judge Steven O'Neill of being part of a "sex war" and linked the sentencing to the treatment of Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh. Vox reports that the strangest moment came when Wyatt likened his client to Jesus. "They persecuted Jesus, and look what happened," he said. "Not saying Mr. Cosby is Jesus, but we know what this country has done to black men for centuries." Cosby, who left the Montgomery County Courthouse in handcuffs, spent his first night behind bars at SCI Phoenix, a state prison that opened two months ago in suburban Philadelphia, reports WPVI. (Cosby's mug shot was released Tuesday afternoon.)

**U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton Rules Out Presidential Run in 2012 and 2016**

In a series of interviews during her Asia-Pacific tour, U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton has made it clear that she has no plans to run for the top U.S. office in either 2012 or 2016. Clinton, who was the Democratic presidential nominee in 2008, stated that she is "very pleased to be doing what I'm doing as Secretary of State" and that she does not intend to pursue a presidential run.

Clinton's comments were made in response to speculation that she might run for president again, particularly in the wake of the Democrats' poor showing in the mid-term elections. However, Clinton emphasized that she hopes the United States is ready for a female president, adding "it should be" and that it is nice to visit countries that have already proven that they can elect women to the highest governing positions.

Clinton's comments were echoed in a separate interview with TV New Zealand, where she defended President Barack Obama and put the Democrats' losses in the midterms in perspective. Clinton acknowledged that the losses were "big" but added that they were not out of the pattern of historical political elections.

**Incident at New Zealand Parliament**

During a visit to New Zealand, Secretary of State Hillary Clinton was introduced as "President Clinton" by New Zealand Prime Minister John Phillip Key, sparking laughter and applause from the audience. Key quickly corrected himself, referring to Clinton as "Secretary Clinton" and thanking her for her time in New Zealand.

**Clinton's Visit to New Zealand**

Clinton's visit to New Zealand was part of a nearly two-week Asia-Pacific tour, which will wrap up in Australia. Clinton's visit to New Zealand was marked by a series of meetings with government officials, including New Zealand Prime Minister John Phillip Key and New Zealand Foreign Minister Murray Stuart McCully.

Overall, Clinton's comments on her presidential prospects and her visit to New Zealand have provided a glimpse into her current priorities and goals as Secretary of State.

Asked in another interview whether she could be the first female president, she answered, "Well, not me. But it will be someone ..." The significance? On another front, Clinton said the midterm losses were "big—but not out of the pattern of historical political elections." She added, "I think the president made very clear was he made decisions which were essential for the well-being of the American people."

**Amy Schumer in the News: Pregnancy, Politics, and Social Activism**

Comedian and actress Amy Schumer has been making headlines in recent weeks with a series of announcements and statements that reflect her commitment to social activism and politics. Here's a summary of the key news:

**Pregnancy Announcement**

In a clever and strategic move, Schumer announced her pregnancy on Instagram by directing her followers to check out the account of journalist Jessica Yellin, who was promoting her #NewsNotNoise campaign. The campaign aims to educate people about what they need to know versus what noise they can tune out. Schumer's pregnancy announcement was cleverly hidden at the bottom of a lengthy list of recommended candidates for the upcoming midterm elections.

**Support for Colin Kaepernick and NFL Protest**

Schumer has been a vocal supporter of Colin Kaepernick's peaceful protest against racism and police brutality in the NFL. She recently urged Maroon 5 to pull out of the Super Bowl Halftime Show, citing her support for Kaepernick's protest.

**Support for #NewsNotNoise and Midterm Elections**

Schumer's support for the #NewsNotNoise campaign reflects her commitment to educating people about politics and social issues. She has also been urging her followers to vote in the upcoming midterm elections, highlighting the importance of civic engagement and participation.

**Social Activism and Advocacy**

Schumer has a long history of social activism and advocacy, including supporting gun control, the transgender community, and other causes she cares about. Her recent arrest and detention for protesting Brett Kavanaugh's nomination to the Supreme Court reflect her willingness to take a stand and speak out on issues she believes in.

Overall, Schumer's recent announcements and statements reflect her commitment to social activism, politics, and civic engagement. Her clever and strategic approach to announcing her pregnancy has also generated widespread attention and praise.
details.ground_truth: Amy Schumer took the long way around to announce she's pregnant with husband Chris Fischer. The comedian and actress broke her baby news Monday on the Instagram stories of friend and journalist Jessica Yellin, the AP reports. Yellin, of the site NewsNotNoise.org, showed at the end of a list of Schumer's recommended congressional and gubernatorial candidates the line: "I'm pregnant-Amy Schumer." Schumer had teased the announcement on her own Instagram page, captioning a photo of hers and Fischer's heads atop Prince Harry's and Meghan Markle's bodies, "About to announce some exciting news on @jessicayellin insta page. Please follow her for up to the minute #newsnotnoise she breaks down what’s really going on. She agreed to post a lil noise today for me! Follow her and VOTE!!" "We are 15 days out from the midterms and there is a lot going on," Yellin explained before going into Schumer's "lengthy" list of recommendations, per Bustle. "I wanted to share some news from our community. Maybe it's noise, but it's happy noise," Yellin continued. "These are the recommendations of Amy Schumer, one of the most consistent and early supporters of #NewsNotNoise. Now, read all the way to the bottom. You'll see there's some news down there. Congratulations, Amy." Schumer is known for her liberal politics: She was recently arrested protesting the confirmation of Judge Brett Kavanaugh to the US Supreme Court. The 37-year-old married Fischer, a chef, in February. (Here's why Schumer won't appear in any Super Bowl ads.)

**Tyson Foods Faces Animal Abuse Allegations, Fires 10 Employees and Retrains Live Poultry Workers**

Tyson Foods, one of the world's largest meat producers, is facing new allegations of animal abuse after an undercover investigation by the animal advocacy group Compassion Over Killing. The video, released in January 2006, shows Tyson workers stomping, kicking, and suffocating breeder chickens at facilities in three Virginia counties. In response, Tyson Foods has fired 10 employees who can be seen in the video and is retraining all of its live poultry workers on the company's animal welfare policies.

This is not the first time that Tyson Foods has faced animal abuse allegations. In 2016, the company was accused of mistreating chickens at its facilities in Virginia after a video was released by Compassion Over Killing. The video showed workers throwing, punching, and kicking chickens, as well as sticking plastic rods through their beaks in a process known as "boning." Tyson Foods responded to the allegations by firing the employees involved and discontinuing the "boning" process at two of the facilities.

The latest video released by Compassion Over Killing has sparked a renewed call for action from the animal rights group. Erica Meier, the group's executive director, said that Tyson Foods needs to be held accountable for the abuse and that it is a systemic problem within the company. The group has made formal complaints about the abuse to prosecutors and animal control officers in the three affected counties.

Tyson Foods has said that it is committed to animal well-being and is taking steps to ensure that its animal welfare procedures are being followed throughout its operations. The company has begun meeting with every employee in its chicken operations that handles live birds to re-emphasize the importance of proper animal handling and the consequences of not complying with the company's animal welfare policies.
details.ground_truth: Chicken giant Tyson Foods says it will retrain all of its workers who deal with live birds after an animal rights group released hidden footage from within its facilities on Wednesday, USA Today reports. According to the Washington Post, the video from Compassion Over Killing shows workers choking, punching, and kicking chickens, plus running them over with forklifts and leaving them in piles to die. One worker in the video says it's "inhumane" to kill a chicken by stepping on its head while he kills a chicken by stepping on its head. Tyson calls the behavior "inexcusable" and says it fired 10 employees at the Virginia facilities shown in the video. Animal rights activists have infiltrated Tyson Foods to film such videos at least five times in a little over a year.

**Donald Trump's Campaign Shake-up and Michael Cohen's "Says Who?" Moment**

In a bid to regain momentum in the polls, Donald Trump's campaign has undergone a major shake-up, with the appointment of new leaders, including Stephen Bannon as campaign chief executive and Kellyanne Conway as campaign manager. However, Trump's special counsel and lawyer, Michael Cohen, has been at the center of controversy after a heated exchange with CNN's Brianna Keilar on the campaign's changes.

During the interview, Keilar pointed out that the campaign was "down" in the polls, to which Cohen responded with the now-infamous phrase, "Says who?" Keilar repeatedly pressed Cohen for an answer, to which he replied, "Polls. Most of them. All of them?" The exchange has sparked a wave of ridicule on social media, with many users responding to Cohen's tweets with the same phrase, "Says who?"

The controversy has also highlighted the tension between the Trump campaign and the media, with Cohen being known for his aggressive defense of Trump against negative stories. Interestingly, Cohen was a registered Democrat who voted for Barack Obama in 2008, before switching his allegiance to the Republican Party.

In the polls, Hillary Clinton is currently leading in most states, including Virginia and Colorado, while Trump is ahead in Indiana, according to a Monmouth University poll. However, the Iowa State Fair corn poll has also been cited as a bonus Trump-led poll.

The exchange on CNN has also sparked a wider conversation about the use of language and semantics in politics, with some users pointing out that the correct phrase should be "Says whom" rather than "Says who."
details.ground_truth: Donald Trump's lawyer has apparently decided that it's his turn to make the gaffes. In an interview with CNN Wednesday, notoriously combative Trump Organization attorney Michael Cohen interrupted host Brianna Keilar when she said that the campaign was "down," Politico reports. "Says who?" he asked. "Polls. Most of them. All of them?" she replied. "Says who?" Cohen asked again. When she repeated it was polls, he responded, "OK, which polls?” and Keilar said: “All of them." USA Today reports that Twitter users were quick to mock Cohen and are now replying "Says who?" to all his tweets. Cohen, in what Law Newz calls "typical lawyer fashion," also took issue with Trump's overhaul of campaign staff being called a "shake-up," insisting it was actually a "change-up." Cohen also raised eyebrows when he told Kielar that Trump knows about "the African-American problem in this country," the Independent reports. "What I meant to say is the problem that exists in the African-American community," he later said. (Michael Moore says he "knows for a fact" that Trump never really wanted to be president.)

**South Carolina Deputy Fired After Violent Confrontation with Student**

A South Carolina sheriff's deputy, Senior Deputy Ben Fields, has been fired and terminated from his position as a school resource officer at Spring Valley High School in Columbia, South Carolina. The decision comes after a viral video showed Fields forcefully removing a student from her seat, flipping her across the classroom, and handcuffing her.

The incident occurred on Monday, when the student, who has not been identified, became disruptive in her algebra class and refused to leave when asked by her teacher and an administrator. Fields was called to the classroom and, in a confrontation that was captured on cellphone video, he body-slammed the student to the ground, dragged her and her desk across the floor, and arrested her along with a second student, 18-year-old Niya Kenny, who witnessed the mayhem and fought back.

Richland County Sheriff Leon Lott said that the department's internal investigation found that Fields' actions were "not acceptable" and that he had violated the department's policies. Lott also acknowledged that the student's unruly behavior set off the incident, but said that it did not justify Fields' actions.

The FBI, the Justice Department, and state law enforcement have opened separate investigations into the incident, and the president of the South Carolina chapter of the NAACP has called for Fields to be charged with assault. School district officials have blasted Fields' actions as "outrageous" and "reprehensible," and the video itself as "shamefully shocking."

Fields, 34, had been a school resource officer at Spring Valley High School for seven years and had previously been accused of racial bias and excessive force. He has a history of lawsuits, including a case in which a federal jury sided with him after a black couple accused him of excessive force and battery during a noise complaint arrest in 2005.

Lott said that the department will be reviewing its policies and procedures for handling student discipline, and that school resource officers will receive more training and certification. The incident has sparked a national conversation about the use of police in schools and the need for more training and accountability for law enforcement officers.

* Deputy Ben Fields fired and terminated from his position as a school resource officer at Spring Valley High School
* FBI, Justice Department, and state law enforcement open separate investigations into the incident
* President of the South Carolina chapter of the NAACP calls for Fields to be charged with assault
* School district officials blast Fields' actions as
details.ground_truth: The school resource officer suspended after videos of him throwing a black female high-school student around a South Carolina classroom went viral Monday has been fired, CBS News reports. Richland County Sheriff Leon Lott announced the firing of Ben Fields Wednesday after an investigation found his "use of force was unacceptable" and he didn't follow procedure during the arrest. According to NBC News, the decision was made after the sheriff's department looked at videos of the incident and interviewed witnesses. "From the very beginning that's what's caused me to be upset, and continued to upset me, is that he picked the student up and threw the student across the room," Lott says. The Department of Justice is also conducting its own investigation into the incident. This isn't the first time Fields, who is white, has faced accusations of excessive force and racial bias. He's been involved in three other such cases dating back to 2005, and he's scheduled to go to trial over a 2013 racial bias incident in January.

**Headline:** Mark David Chapman's Wife Hopes for His Release After 38 Years in Prison

Mark David Chapman, the man who murdered John Lennon on his New York doorstep in 1980, is set to face his 10th parole hearing. His wife, Gloria Hiroko Chapman, has expressed her hope that he will be released from prison after nearly four decades behind bars.

Gloria, 67, revealed that her husband had told her two months before the murder that he planned to kill Lennon. However, she claimed that he never carried out the threat at the time because of his love for her and insisted that he had dumped the gun he was going to use.

The couple met in Hawaii in 1978, where Gloria was a travel agent and Chapman was a maintenance worker at a local hospital. They got married in 1979, but their relationship was marked by violence and abuse, with Gloria admitting that she was physically hurt by her husband on several occasions.

Despite this, Gloria has remained devoted to her husband and has been allowed to have sex with him during prison visits in a caravan on the grounds. The couple has spent 44 hours each year together for the past 25 years, making pizzas, watching TV, and making love.

Gloria's hope for her husband's release is based on the New York State parole board's recent willingness to place more emphasis on an inmate's behavior behind bars than the severity of the crime. Several notorious murderers who had been denied parole have been set free in recent years, and Gloria is praying for the same outcome for her husband.

**Internet Archive Effort to Fix Broken Links**

In a separate development, the Internet Archive is launching an effort to archive pages as they are created and archive the pages that they refer to. This will help preserve links to pages that are changed or taken from the web, and fix broken links on the web.

The goal of the effort is to create a comprehensive archive of the web, allowing users to access and view the original version of a page even if the link is broken. The Internet Archive hopes that references to these archived pages will be put in place of a link that would be otherwise be broken, or a companion link to allow people to see what was originally intended by a page's authors.
details.ground_truth: The man who murdered John Lennon will remain behind bars after his 10th unsuccessful parole hearing, reports the Daily News. Mark David Chapman, now 63, was turned down again this week. No reasons have yet been made public, but the newspaper reports that Yoko Ono sent the parole board a letter asking that Chapman remain in the Wende Correctional Facility in upstate New York—not only for the safety of her and her family, but for his own safety. Chapman, who shot Lennon in New York City in 1980, is up again for parole in about two years. Chapman's wife recently told the UK Mirror that he talked of killing Lennon two months before actually doing so.

**The Lovely Bones: A Film Review Summary**

"The Lovely Bones" is a film adaptation of the bestselling novel by Alice Sebold, directed by Peter Jackson and starring Saoirse Ronan, Stanley Tucci, Mark Wahlberg, and Rachel Weisz. The movie tells the story of Susie Salmon, a 14-year-old girl who is murdered by her neighbor, George Harvey, in 1973. Susie's spirit is trapped in a limbo between heaven and earth, where she watches her family and friends go on with their lives.

**Positive Aspects:**

* Saoirse Ronan's performance as Susie is praised as natural, honest, and radiant, bringing a sense of innocence and otherworldly wisdom to the character.
* Stanley Tucci's performance as George Harvey is unsettling and disturbing, making him a nightmare-inducing presence on screen.
* The film's visuals, particularly the dreamscape look of the in-between world, are praised as beautiful and special.
* The movie's themes of loving and mourning, and the importance of a father's love for his child, are well-explored.

**Negative Aspects:**

* The film's tone is inconsistent, veering from lightheartedness to heavy-handedness, which can be jarring and off-putting.
* The depiction of heaven in the film is cheesy and unoriginal, breaking no new ground in the depiction of the afterlife.
* The film's reliance on the musings of a dead, omniscient narrator comes off artificial and emotionless on-screen.
* The movie's focus on the thriller aspects of the story can overshadow the more nuanced and complex themes of the novel.

**Overall:**

"The Lovely Bones" is a film that has its moments of beauty and poignancy, but is ultimately marred by its inconsistent tone and reliance on unoriginal visuals. While Saoirse Ronan and Stanley Tucci give strong performances, the film's themes and ideas are not fully explored or developed. Overall, the film is a C+ effort, and a disappointing adaptation of a complex and nuanced novel.

**Rating:** PG-13 for mature thematic material involving disturbing violent content and images, and some language.

**Running Time:** 2 hours, 15 minutes

**Opens:** Friday nationwide nationwide
details.ground_truth: Peter Jackson's crafts a fantastic vision of the afterlife in The Lovely Bones, say critics, but some feel it came at the expense of the human side of the story of a murdered teen, adapted from the book by Alice Sebold. "By turns warmly sentimental, serial-killer sinister, and science-fiction fantastical, The Lovely Bones was an unlikely book to achieve worldwide success," Kenneth Turan writes in the Los Angeles Times. "Those mismatched elements come back to haunt" the film version, "making the final product more hit-and-miss than unblemished triumph." "Sebold's Lovely Bones is fleshed out with the perilous, irresistible power of sex—a real world of extramarital sex and sex between young lovers in addition to the heinous rape from which moviegoers are shielded." But Jackson "shies from the challenge, shortchanging a story that isn't only about the lightness of souls in heaven but also about the urges of bodies on earth," writes Lisa Schwarzbaum at Entertainment Weekly. "Through Jackson's art" and the magic of star Saoirse Rogan, Richard Corliss writes at Time, "the obscenity of child murder has been invested with immense gravity and grace." "Some books are not meant to be adapted to the big screen," and The Lovely Bones is one of them, writes Claudia Puig at USA Today. What works in the book, she writes, comes off as "artificial and emotionless on-screen."

**Mexico's Military Under Fire: Video of Soldier Executing Civilian Sparks Outrage and Calls for Investigation**

A disturbing video has emerged from Mexico, appearing to show a soldier executing a civilian lying face down in the street with a shot to the back of his head. The video, which was first published by Diario Cambio, is believed to have been taken by a security camera and shows the events of a May 3 encounter between the military and fuel pipeline thieves in the central state of Puebla.

According to the military's original account, the thieves used local residents as human shields in an initial ambush on an army patrol, resulting in the deaths of two soldiers and a third being wounded. However, it is unclear if the events depicted in the video are from this initial ambush or a subsequent one that occurred hours later, in which two more soldiers were killed and three attackers.

The video shows a silver four-door car slowly reversing into the frame on a darkened street with gunshots hitting it and marks already visible on its windshield. At least one shot appears to come from the rear door on the driver's side, which is ajar. The driver's door opens and a man steps out with his back to the camera, followed by a second man who lies down in the street. The driver is briefly illuminated as he steps through the beam of the tail light, then two soldiers rush in from the darkness and the driver quickly gets on the ground beside the other man.

A minute later, a soldier pushes a third man from around the back to the front of the car and out of the frame. Seconds later, the man who had gotten out of the back of the vehicle emerges from the darkness and is pushed by a soldier out of the frame in the same direction. Then a soldier grabs the arms of the apparent driver and another soldier takes his legs, and they drag him toward the car's front and deposit him on his back. With apparent difficulty, he rolls over onto his stomach.

Six minutes later, the driver remains face down in the street as a soldier holds a rifle in the foreground and two other soldiers stand near the car. Suddenly, a gun and helmet enter the left edge of the frame directly over the unmoving driver and there is a flash from the gun. The man's prone body bucks and the soldier in the foreground lifts up one foot as if surprised by the sound. Whatever happened next is not seen, as the remainder of the video does not include a time stamp, but a narrator says more than 1½ hours
details.ground_truth: A video released online appears to show a Mexican soldier executing a suspected oil thief at point-blank range as he lays defenseless on the ground. The heavily edited video, taken from a surveillance camera, shows a car coming under fire during what the military describes as a series of ambushes that left four soldiers and six suspects dead in Palmarito on May 3. As soldiers surround the vehicle, its occupants exit and are forced to lie down in front of the car. Soldiers are then seen dragging a suspect to the same spot. He appears injured but rolls from his back onto his front. Six minutes later, a soldier appears at the side of the frame and appears to shoot the man in the back of the head, per the AP. A dark stain spreads around him, reports Reuters. "There was already concern about the use of excessive force by the military. Now this video seems to give us the proof," says the chairwoman of Mexico's Senate Commission on Human Rights, per the Washington Post, which identifies the man shot as Raul Jimenez Martinez, 46. Mexico's defense ministry, however, says it will cooperate fully with an investigation launched by the attorney general's office on May 4. "Under no circumstances can conduct contrary to the law and human rights be justified," the ministry says. Residents of Palmarito have already taken to the streets to protest the deaths of the suspects, placing the blame on the army, though the defense ministry previously claimed the suspects used residents as human shields.

**Tragedy at the Four Seasons Hotel: 20-Year-Old Photography Enthusiast Falls to His Death**

A devastating incident occurred at the 52-story Four Seasons Hotel in Manhattan, New York, when a 20-year-old man, Connor Cummings, fell to his death while attempting to take pictures from the hotel's scaffolding. Cummings, a photography buff and a sophomore psychology major at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, was with his 18-year-old friend, Dimitri Olivares, when the incident occurred.

According to police sources, Cummings and Olivares had taken the hotel elevator to the top floor and then walked up two flights of stairs to the rooftop exit. They then climbed a ladder to access a catwalk on the roof, where Cummings lost his footing and fell 25 feet to his death. Olivares, who was carrying their photography equipment, was left traumatized and eventually returned to the hotel to report the incident to security and police.

The investigation into the incident is ongoing, and no charges have been filed against Olivares. Hotel officials have expressed their condolences and sympathy to Cummings' family, stating that the safety and security of their guests, patrons, and employees is a top priority.

Cummings' friends and family have remembered him as a loving and dedicated individual who was passionate about photography and helping others. His fraternity fraternity, Alpha Sigma Phi, has posted a message on Facebook mourning his death and describing his "fiery passion" for photography.

The incident serves as a reminder of the risks and dangers associated with climbing and accessing restricted areas, and the importance of prioritizing safety and security in all aspects of life.
details.ground_truth: Connor Cummins' family describes him to WABC-TV as a photography devotee—and that enthusiasm may have cost him his life. The New Jersey man, said to be either 20 or 24 years old, and an 18-year-old pal ascended the scaffolding outside NYC's Four Seasons hotel Wednesday night in search of the perfect panoramic picture—but Cummins may have slipped on railings made slippery by the rain and fell nine stories to his death, cops tell NJ.com. An NYPD spokesman tells the New York Daily News that the initial investigation suggests the men weren't guests of the hotel. Cummins, who originally hails from Rockaway and whose Facebook page indicates he's now a student at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, per ABC, landed on one of the hotel's lower-level rooftops around 11:15pm and was pronounced dead at the scene, reports the New York Post. His friend wasn't injured and was questioned by authorities to fill in the blanks; he was released Thursday morning without being charged. "He was a loving boy, that's all I can say," one of Cummins' relatives tells the Post. "I don't know what happened." (There's more suspicion surrounding a Muslim teen's fatal fall off a Seattle building.)

**Truckers' Protest in Washington, D.C. - A Summary of the News**

A group of truck drivers, organized by the "Truckers for the Constitution" and "Truckers Ride for the Constitution" rally, have planned to protest the federal government by backing up the Capital Beltway around Washington, D.C. The truckers are frustrated with the Obama administration's regulations, low wages, and high fuel costs. They also express their disapproval of national political leaders and want to call attention to their grievances.

The initial plan was to intentionally clog the inner loop of the Washington, D.C., beltway, with tractor-trailer drivers circling the beltway "three lanes deep." The trucks would keep the left lane open for emergency vehicles, but commuters who did not have a supporter sticker on their window would be blocked. The protest was also expected to include the arrest of members of Congress, who the truckers believe have disregarded the Constitution.

However, an update to the story has revealed that the plan to arrest members of Congress has been abandoned. Earl Conlon, a Georgia trucker handling logistics for the protest, said that the group is now seeking the arrest of politicians who have allegedly committed "treason" by funneling weapons to al-Qaida-linked rebels in Syria. The list of politicians includes House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi, D-Calif., and Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif.

The truckers' grievances include Environmental Protection Agency fuel efficiency standards, the high cost of diesel fuel, state and local anti-idling laws, and insurance companies requiring technological updates. They also express their concern about the perceived deterioration of Fourth Amendment rights protecting truckers' cabs.

The number of participants is expected to be significant, with close to 50,000 likes on a Facebook page advertising the ride. A similar three-day protest in 2007 against illegal immigration and competition from Mexican truckers did not spoil commutes as feared, the Washington Examiner reported.

The truckers' protest is expected to take place on the morning of Oct. 11, and it remains to be seen whether the group's demands will be met or if the protest will escalate into a larger confrontation.
details.ground_truth: Protesting truckers are planning a shutdown of their own—on Washington's beltway. Starting on Friday morning, they intend to fill the road "three lanes deep" with tractor-trailers, an organizer of "Truckers Ride for the Constitution" tells US News and World Report. In a demonstration against what they view as violations of the Constitution, planners like Earl Conlon are calling for "the arrest of everyone in government who has violated their oath of office." Their grievances include truckers' wages, industry regulations, and NSA spying, the Hill reports. They're also accusing President Obama of treason for allegedly putting weapons in the hands of rebels with ties to al-Qaeda. Organizers have received some 3,000 RSVPs, says one. Moving at the 55mph speed limit, they'll leave a lane open for emergency vehicles; vehicles with "T2SDA," or "Truckers to Shut Down America," displayed will also be allowed to pass. "Everybody that doesn't have a supporter sticker on their window, good luck: Nobody in, nobody out," says Conlon. A Facebook page for the event has more than 55,000 "likes."

**Secret Service Scandal Rocks the Nation: Multiple Agents and Supervisors Implicated in Prostitution Scandal**

A growing scandal within the Secret Service has left the nation reeling, with multiple agents and supervisors implicated in a prostitution scandal that took place in Cartagena, Colombia, ahead of President Barack Obama's visit to the Summit of the Americas. The scandal has sparked widespread outrage and calls for accountability, with lawmakers and the White House expressing confidence in the agency's leadership despite the growing number of firings and resignations.

**Key Players Implicated in the Scandal**

* David Chaney, a 48-year-old supervisor in the Secret Service's international programs division, was forced out of the agency after making light of his official protective work on his Facebook page, joking about a picture of himself standing watch behind Sarah Palin during the 2008 campaign.
* Greg Stokes, an assistant special agent in charge of the K-9 division, was notified by agency officials that he will be fired, although he will be given an opportunity to contest the charges.
* 11 Secret Service agents and uniformed officers were involved in the scandal, which investigators believe included heavy drinking, visits to a strip club, and payments to women working as prostitutes.
* 10 military personnel and as many as 20 Colombian women were also involved in the scandal.

**Reactions from Lawmakers and the White House**

* Rep. Peter King (R-N.Y.), chairman of the House Homeland Security Committee, said he expected more resignations and firings in the case, and that the 11 agents involved in the scandal underwent drug tests and polygraph exams.
* President Barack Obama's chief spokesman, Jay Carney, noted that some Secret Service employees involved have already lost their jobs, and asked for patience as official investigations continue.
* Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid (D-Nev.) suggested that the problem lies with the judgment of those involved, and that there is not a bill that can be passed to cause people to have common sense.
* Rep. Randy Forbes (R-Va.) called for leadership changes at the Secret Service, and said that the incident is a fundamental principle of security planning - you don't let the prostitutes in.

**Palin Weighs In on the Scandal**

* Sarah Palin responded to the Post's revelations about Chaney's comments by saying she was disgusted that a Secret Service agent would make jokes about checking out her "backside" and called his behavior "pretty embarrassing."
* Palin stressed that she viewed the scandal as emblematic of
details.ground_truth: One of the two Secret Service supervisors to lose their jobs so far over the Colombia sex scandal joked that he was checking out Sarah Palin as he guarded her during the 2008 campaign, the Washington Post finds. Senior agent David Chaney posted at least two pictures of himself with Palin on Facebook, one with the comment, "I was really checking her out, if you know what I mean." Chaney has been forced to resign, and another supervisor has been informed that he will be fired. In an interview with Fox News, Palin said the joke is on Chaney. "Well check this out, buddy—you're fired!" she said, calling the scandal "a symptom of government run amok," reports AP. "It's like, who's minding the store around here?" she added. "The president, for one, he better be wary, there, of when Secret Service is accompanying his family on vacation. They may be checking out the first lady instead of guarding her."

**Falling from the Top: 5 Notable Individuals Who Lost Their Stature**

In a culture where fame, fortune, and power are often glorified, it's not uncommon for individuals to experience a precipitous loss in stature. 24/7 Wall St. recently compiled a list of 50 well-known individuals who have fallen from the top, and we take a closer look at 5 of these notable individuals.

**1. Anthony Scaramucci: The Short-Lived White House Communications Director**

Anthony Scaramucci's tenure as White House Communications Director was the shortest in the position's nearly 50-year history, lasting just 11 days. His tumultuous time in the Trump administration was marked by protocol breaches, conflicting statements, and a profanity-laced interview with The New Yorker. His wife's divorce filing during his brief stint added to the chaos.

**2. Chris Christie: The Governor with the Lowest Approval Ratings**

Chris Christie, the outgoing Governor of New Jersey, has the lowest approval ratings of any governor in the country and in the state's history. His reputation was sullied by the Bridgegate scandal, and his decision to fawn over Donald Trump after the presidential election didn't help. His recent decision to lounge on a state-owned beach while the public was denied access due to a government shutdown further eroded his public image.

**3. James Comey: The FBI Director Who Lost His Job**

James Comey's time as FBI Director was marked by controversy, particularly during the 2016 presidential election. His decision to publicly attack Hillary Clinton's judgment and announce an investigation into her just days before the election sparked widespread criticism. Trump's abrupt firing of Comey in May 2017 added to the controversy, with the White House providing multiple conflicting justifications for the decision.

**4. Mark Fields: The CEO Who Failed to Inspire Confidence**

Mark Fields, the former CEO of Ford Motor Company, was shown the door in 2017 due to his failure to inspire confidence on Wall Street. Despite his efforts to invest in electric and autonomous vehicle technology, Ford shares fell by nearly 40% during his tenure as CEO. His attempts to woo shareholders by announcing a 10% reduction in the global workforce in May 2017 were too little, too late.

**5. Tiger Woods: The Golfer Who Lost His Way**

Tiger Woods, the golf superstar with 14 major wins to his name, has been embroiled in controversy on and off the course since reports of his infidelity came to
details.ground_truth: Power: One day you have it, the next you don't. No one likely knows that better than the world's 50 "least powerful" people, described by 24/7 Wall St. as "well-known individuals around the world who have recently experienced a precipitous loss in stature." The top 10: Anthony Scaramucci: Where to begin? Try here, here, and here. Chris Christie: The former head of Donald Trump's transition team has the lowest approval rating of any governor in New Jersey's history. James Comey: The former FBI director lasted not even five months under Trump. Mark Fields: The Ford CEO was canned after shares fell almost 40% during his tenure. Tiger Woods: Blame his DUI arrest and 899th ranking in men's pro golf. Kendall Jenner: Remember that Pepsi commercial? Montgomery Moran: After a wave of issues at Chipotle, the co-CEO stepped down last year. Tomi Lahren: The conservative media personality was suspended from the Blaze after announcing she was pro-choice. Park Geun-hye: The former president of South Korea was impeached and jailed after a corruption scandal involving Samsung. Mitch McConnell: As Senate majority leader, McConnell failed to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act. Click for the full list, which also includes Sean Spicer, R. Kelly, and Theresa May. **Government Shutdown Looms as Congress Fails to Reach a Deal**

The United States is bracing for a partial government shutdown as Congress fails to reach a deal on a spending bill by the Monday night deadline. The standoff has been fueled by a Republican-led House bill to delay the implementation of President Barack Obama's signature healthcare law, which Democrats have vowed to reject.

**House and Senate Stances**

The House of Representatives voted early Sunday morning to approve a plan to fund federal agencies through Dec. 15, which includes conditions related to the healthcare law. However, Senate Democrats have said they will reject any changes to the law, and President Obama would veto such a bill. House Speaker John Boehner (R-OH) has a powerful incentive to continue to press his party's fight against the healthcare law, as conservatives in the House Republican conference are clamoring for a confrontation.

The Senate Majority Leader, Harry Reid (D-NV), has taken a hardline stance, urging the president to call off a meeting with congressional leaders and pushing a Democratic legislative strategy to make the Republicans cave. Reid believes that giving even a few small concessions on a short-term stopgap funding measure would lead to bigger concessions on the debt ceiling.

**Economic Impact of a Shutdown**

The Washington region, home to the largest concentration of federal workers and contractors, could lose an estimated $200 million a day and see more than 700,000 jobs take a financial hit if the federal government shuts down. The region's economy is weak, and a shutdown would have real costs, according to economist Stephen Fuller. Federal benefits may continue to be processed, but with fewer workers to process them, no new applications would be accepted and "serious backlogs" would be expected.

Local governments have been setting aside pots of money in anticipation of a shutdown, with Montgomery County accounting for a possible $60 million loss in revenue in its current budget. Maryland and Virginia officials are looking at the possibility of tapping reserve funds.

**Ripple Effects of a Shutdown**

The shutdown could affect every federal agency in some way, with air traffic controllers considered essential, but planes could be delayed without "nonessential" support staff. Lawmakers passed legislation to continue paying active military members, but veterans' benefits could run out if a shutdown lasts more than a few weeks. The shutdown could also have a disproportionate effect on the Washington region, where the federal government's presence accounts for one-third of the economy.

In 1995 and 1996, a government shutdown resulted in the closure of national parks and monuments
details.ground_truth: Washington is braced for the first government shutdown in 17 years, which will happen at midnight tonight unless lawmakers manage to break the deadlock over funding and hammer out a deal. Here's what to expect from a day of drama in DC: The House has passed legislation to delay ObamaCare for a year and repeal a tax on medical devices, so the measure will return to the Senate this afternoon, just hours before the shutdown is due to kick in. Harry Reid is expected to table the anti-ObamaCare amendments with a simple Democratic majority and quickly send it back to the House—but if any opportunity arises for Senate Republicans to use delaying tactics, expect Ted Cruz to lead the way, the Wall Street Journal predicts. There's a chance of a straight up-or-down vote in the House, but if it becomes clear that it will be impossible to break the deadlock by midnight—which appears to be the likeliest scenario—the partial shutdown could still be averted if lawmakers pass a short-term funding bill to buy another week or so of debating time. So who will blink first? Neither Reid nor John Boehner appears likely to give way. Reid is determined to play hardball and not give an inch on ObamaCare, Politico reports. Reid and his allies believe that giving any concessions now will only lead to bigger concessions to avoid a debt default next month—and they think the backlash from a government shutdown will hit the GOP hardest. Boehner, meanwhile, will face the wrath of his party's conservatives if he gives way too soon—but polls show the public will blame his House Republicans if there is a shutdown. If there is a shutdown, it won't be an abrupt halt to government, but more "like a spending freeze that will gradually spread through the government like ice forming in water," Quartz explains. Federal employees, 800,000 of whom will be sent home without pay, will be the first affected, and the shutdown will also affect scientific research, trade negotiations, and the federal programs that guarantee home loans and provide capital to business. Air traffic controllers will still be at work, however, and agencies that deal with national security and law enforcement are exempt from the shutdown. A shutdown of a few days might not do too much damage, but experts warn that the national economy will take a serious hit if it is prolonged. In Washington, DC, however, even a brief shutdown will cost the district an estimated $200 million a day—and affect basic services like trash collection. "This is serious," the director of George Mason University’s Center for Regional Analysis tells the Washington Post. "The national economy may not notice a shutdown much unless it lasts three or four weeks. But for the Washington area, this is a tsunami."

**NASA's Parker Solar Probe Mission: Unveiling the Secrets of the Sun**

NASA's Parker Solar Probe, previously known as the Solar Probe Plus, is set to embark on a historic mission to explore the sun's atmosphere and unlock the secrets of solar storms that impact Earth's technology and power grid. The spacecraft, named in honor of astrophysicist Eugene Parker, who predicted the existence of the solar wind in 1958, will fly directly into the sun's corona, or outer layer, and withstand temperatures of up to 1,400 degrees Celsius.

**Mission Objectives and Science**

The Parker Solar Probe will perform 24 close flybys of the sun, with some encounters bringing it within 3.9 million miles of the solar surface, seven times closer than any previous spacecraft. The mission's objectives include:

1. Tracing the flow of energy that heats and accelerates the sun's corona and solar wind.
2. Determining the structure and dynamics of the plasma and magnetic fields at the sources of the solar wind.
3. Exploring mechanisms that accelerate and transport energetic particles.

The spacecraft will be equipped with a 4.5-inch thick carbon-composite solar shield, which will protect it from the extreme heat while keeping the science instruments at a comfortable room temperature. The probe will also make a series of Venus flybys to shrink its orbit around the sun over the course of nearly seven years.

**Impact of Space Weather on Earth's Technology and Power Grid**

Space weather, including solar storms and coronal mass ejections, can impact Earth's technology and power grid, causing widespread outages and disruptions. A study by the Metatech Corporation suggests that an unusually powerful magnetic storm could result in widespread outages and possible long-term damage to the nation's power grid. The Parker Solar Probe mission will help improve our understanding of space weather and its impact on Earth.

**A Living Tribute to Eugene Parker**

The Parker Solar Probe is a living tribute to Eugene Parker, who is now the S. Chandrasekhar Distinguished Service Professor Emeritus at the University of Chicago. Parker was awarded the NASA Distinguished Public Service Medal and a model of the probe, and he will also have a chip with photos of himself and his revolutionary paper on the spacecraft. The mission's science scientist, Nicola Fox, said that the findings will help answer questions about how stars work and improve space weather forecasting.

**Launch and Timeline**

The Parker Solar Probe is scheduled to launch on July 31, 2018,
details.ground_truth: NASA is going to the sun. More specifically, it's launching an unmanned probe next year that will travel closer to the star than any spacecraft has done previously. "It's a spacecraft loaded with technological breakthroughs that will solve many of the largest mysteries about our star," says Nicola Fox of the Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory. In less scientific terms, per the Telegraph: "We will finally touch the sun." The nuts and bolts: The mission: The 10-foot probe will launch in July or August of 2018 and eventually get to within 3.7 million miles of the sun, about seven times closer than any previous spacecraft. Eventually, it will be whizzing around the sun at a speed of 450,000 miles per hour, reports CNN. The mission ends in 2025. Corona: The probe will actually fly into the outermost part of the sun's atmosphere, called the corona, per a mission overview at NASA that touts "humanity's first visit to a star." The heat: The probe will rely on a 4.5-inch-thick carbon-composite solar shield to help it withstand temperatures up to 2,550 degrees Fahrenheit, reports Orlando's WKMG. Instruments will remain at room temperature. The name: The probe's name has been changed to the Parker Solar Probe to honor astrophysicist Eugene Parker, who's credited with discovering solar wind. Parker, who turns 90 in June, is a professor emeritus at the University of Chicago, and the Guardian recounts that the theory he put forward in 1958 about a stream of charged particles flowing from the sun was once thought to be "crazy." A first: This is the first time NASA has named a mission after a living scientist. "I'm certainly greatly honored," said the man himself, per Space.com. Two puzzles: Scientists hope to better understand two things in particular: "How is the solar wind accelerated, and why is the ... corona so much hotter than the solar surface?" (It's 3 million degrees Fahrenheit vs. 10,000 degrees Fahrenheit.) Why it matters: Generally, solar storms are relatively harmless when they reach Earth, but these particle bursts occasionally wreak havoc on satellites and here on Earth, and they have the potential to be devastating. "The more we know about how these processes work, the better we can get at predicting when they will happen," writes Loren Grush at the Verge.
