**Holiday Season Data Breaches: Target and Neiman Marcus Affected**

The holiday season has seen a significant rise in data breaches, with two major retailers, Target and Neiman Marcus, being affected. According to reports, the computer network at Neiman Marcus was penetrated by hackers as far back as July, and the breach was not fully contained until Sunday. The company disclosed the data theft of customer information late last week, saying it first learned in mid-December of suspicious activity that involved credit cards used at its stores.

The breach at Neiman Marcus appears to have gone on for significantly longer than the widespread attack on Target. In Target's case, however, the data that was stolen appears to be much more significant and ripe for fraud. Target has said card numbers from 40 million customers were stolen, along with encrypted PINs for debit cards. It also estimated that other personal information belonging to 70 million people had been stolen by the hackers.

A 17-page report from a computer firm hired to look into the massive data breach at Target stores provides new insight into the holiday hacking that endangered data for more than 100 million customers. The report, from computer firm iSight, details the type of software used in the massive hack, how it was effective, and mentions regions known to have the software coding prowess to pull off this kind of digital heist.

The report does not draw conclusions on who is ultimately responsible for the attack, citing an active U.S. government investigation. The data breach was caused by a type of malware, similar to a computer virus, placed in a store's point-of-sale systems. The report concludes that since this style of POS hacking can net big rewards for the cyber crooks with little risk, cardholders can expect more of this type of breach.

The holiday data breach at Target Corp. appeared to be part of a broad and highly sophisticated international hacking campaign against multiple retailers, according to a report prepared by federal and private investigators that was sent to financial-services companies and retailers. The report offers some of the first details to emerge about the source of the attack that compromised 40 million credit- and debit-card accounts.

**Key Takeaways:**

* Neiman Marcus's computer network was penetrated by hackers as far back as July, and the breach was not fully contained until Sunday.
* The breach at Neiman Marcus appears to have gone on for significantly longer than the widespread attack on Target.
* Target's data breach compromised 40 million credit- and debit-card accounts, along with encrypted PINs for debit cards
details.ground_truth: It looks like the hackers that hit Target had lots of other, well, targets. The Department of Homeland Security recently sent retailers and financial service companies a secret memo warning that the Target hit appeared to be part of a larger international campaign, the Wall Street Journal reports, an insight gleaned with the help of Dallas cybersecurity firm iSight Partners. Yesterday, iSight released its own report, saying that a virus it's calling KAPTOXA "has potentially infected a large number of retail information systems"—and noting that the "intrusion operators displayed innovation and a high degree of skill," particularly in terms of the "operational sophistication" of the hack. The Journal shares this feature by way of example: The virus focused on stealing data during the peak hours of 10am and 5pm; the data was housed in a Target server that the hackers later accessed. The virus attacks point-of-sale systems in a way that is "new to eCrime," subverting traditional efforts to protect consumer data, the report warns, according to NBC. Parts of the code, which is impervious to all known antivirus software, have been online since last spring. It's partly in Russian, which US officials think may indicate a link to organized crime in the former Soviet Union. The finding follows reports of similar breaches at Neiman Marcus. That breach had gone unnoticed since July, the New York Times reports today; Neiman's system was only fully secured Sunday.

**Lion Escapes and Killings in Kenya and South Africa Spark Outcry and Conservation Concerns**

In recent weeks, several incidents of lion escapes and killings in Kenya and South Africa have sparked an outcry among citizens and conservationists, highlighting the difficult balance between protecting people and conserving lions.

**Kenya: Lion Mohawk's Tragic Death**

In Kenya, the most famous lion in the country, Mohawk, was killed by wildlife rangers after he strayed from Nairobi National Park. The 13-year-old lion was surrounded by a crowd of people, including men who taunted and stoned him, before he lashed out and injured a man. Wildlife officials said they had no choice but to kill him, citing the risk of further injury to others. The incident has sparked an outcry among Kenyans, with many using the hashtag #JusticeforMohawk to ask why rangers did not tranquilize him instead.

**Kenya: Lion Escapes and Habitat Loss**

The Nairobi National Park, where Mohawk was killed, has had several lion escapes in recent weeks, with all but Mohawk being recaptured alive. Conservationists attribute the surge in escapes to habitat loss, citing the park's encroachment by lucrative development projects and the government's construction of a highway through a section of the park. The park's electric fence, which is meant to keep animals inside, has also been damaged in some areas.

**South Africa: Lion Sylvester's Escape and Potential Relocation**

In South Africa, a lion named Sylvester escaped from Karoo National Park and is currently being hunted by wildlife officials. The 3-year-old lion is wearing a satellite tracking collar and has a history of killing livestock. While officials have vowed to put him down once they capture him, some conservationists are advocating for his relocation to a different park.

**Conservation Concerns**

The African lion, a "vulnerable" species according to the International Union for Conservation of Nature, has seen a 50% decline in population over the past 20 years. Habitat loss, human-wildlife conflict, and poaching are all major contributors to the decline. The recent incidents in Kenya and South Africa highlight the need for more effective conservation efforts and the protection of lion habitats.

**Conclusion**

The lion escapes and killings in Kenya and South Africa are a tragic reminder of the difficult balance between protecting people and conserving lions. While conservation efforts are underway, more needs to be done to protect lion habitats and address the root causes of human-wildlife conflict
details.ground_truth: An escaped lion, a taunting crowd, and an ill-prepared first-response team came together in Kenya to cause what some are saying was the unnecessary, senseless death of the lion, the country's Star reports. Mohawk, a 13-year-old lion who was one of Nairobi National Park's most well-known creatures due to his trademark mane and rep as somewhat of a "ladies' lion," had escaped from an unfenced section of the park and ended up in the town of Isinya, where he was promptly surrounded by a jeering group of men, the Washington Post reports. Mohawk became upset by the noise and the growing crowd—Kenya Wildlife Service officials tell the AP about 400 had surrounded him—and lunged at a 27-year-old man, who was sent to the hospital with bruises and severe lacerations on his back. The wildlife service already had an animal management team on the scene, but those rangers had only rifles, not tranquilizers. After Mohawk pounced on the bystander, the rangers felt they couldn't wait for a second team on its way with tranqs—so they unleashed what the Star says was nine bullets, leaving the animal "roaring and writhing in pain" as he died. "If the rangers had the right equipment, this lion would not have died," a local resident tells the paper. "You can't keep a lion in the midst of people for so many hours without stressing it. We blame them for this heinous act." The shooting has prompted plenty of backlash on social media, including a #JusticeForMohawk hashtag, but the wildlife service says it had no choice once the lion started attacking humans. "This action was taken as a last resort after an escalation of the situation and a concern for public safety," the Kenya Wildlife Service says, per CNN. A bunch of lions have busted out of the park lately, driven by what conservationists say is the noise from development projects, including a highway being built through the park. (Same ending, different means for Cecil the lion.)

**Tragic Leopard Attack in Queen Elizabeth National Park, Uganda**

A devastating incident has shaken the community in Queen Elizabeth National Park, Uganda, after a leopard attacked and killed a 2.5-year-old child of a game ranger, Elisha Nabugyere, at the Mweya Safari Lodge. The incident occurred on a Friday evening at around 7:30 pm, when the child was seated at the doorway of their house with a maid. The leopard grabbed and ran with the child, and despite efforts to rescue him, the child's remains were later recovered with only a skull and some bones left.

The deceased's mother, who is also a park ranger, was at a neighbor's house at the time of the incident. The father, SSP Francis Manana Nabugyere, expressed his shock and disappointment at the incident, calling on the Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) to do more to ensure the security of their staff and children. He also stated that he expects reasonable compensation from UWA for the loss of his son's life.

In a separate incident, a 3-year-old child was snatched and eaten by a leopard at the same park on the same day. The child had been left in the care of a nanny at the unfenced staff quarters of a safari lodge, and the nanny heard the child scream for help before the leopard vanished with it into the bush. A search team later found the child's skull and some other bones.

The UWA has launched a hunt to capture the leopard responsible for the attacks, with the intention of removing it from the wild due to the high temptation to eat human flesh once it has done so. The incident has shocked many in Uganda, where leopard attacks on humans are rare.

This is the fourth unfortunate incident for the UWA in recent weeks, following the death of a pride of 11 lions, a French tourist, and another lion in the park.

**Sources:**

* Kampala Post
* Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA)
* Associated Press
* Buzz60
details.ground_truth: A horrifying story out of Uganda, where a toddler was grabbed and eaten by a leopard Friday at the Mweya Safari Lodge in Queen Elizabeth National Park. The 2-year-old boy's mother is a game ranger at the park and was working, and the boy was with his nanny in the kitchen of the family's home in the park's staff quarters, near a doorway. "He was seated with the maid when the leopard grabbed and ran with him," the boy's father tells the Kampala Post. But a spokesperson for the Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) says the boy followed the nanny outdoors: "The maid was not aware the child followed her. She heard the kid scream for help, she intervened but it was too late the leopard had vanished with it in the bush." The staff quarters are reportedly in a protected area that is popular with tourists, USA Today reports, but the family's home is said to be unfenced. A search team ultimately found some of the boy's bones, including his skull, and they were buried over the weekend. The boy's father says he expects compensation from the UWA and that the organization should do more to protect staff and their families. "UWA gave us the coffin," he says. "I have not talked to them about the incident but I would expect something reasonable to compensate me, although my son's life is gone." Leopard attacks on humans are rare in Uganda. The UWA spokesperson says efforts are being made to locate the leopard and possibly relocate it. "The hunt is on with the intention of capturing the leopard and removing it from the wild because once it has eaten human flesh, the temptations are high to eat another human being, it becomes dangerous," he says, per the Telegraph.

**Archaeological Discovery of the Century: Relics of John the Apostle Found in Bulgaria**

In a groundbreaking archaeological discovery, a team of researchers in Bulgaria has unearthed a lead vessel containing the ashes of John the Apostle, one of the twelve apostles of Jesus Christ. The relic, which dates back to the sixth century, was found in a reliquary at a medieval fortress site in Burgas, Bulgaria.

According to Milen Nikolov, the director of the Burgas Regional Museum of History, the early Christians believed that the relic had healing properties, and it was a popular pilgrimage site for those seeking to be cured. Nikolov described the discovery as "one of the most important discoveries" in the museum's history.

The relic, which is less than an inch long, is decorated with crosses and was found in a 6th century basilica. The team of researchers also discovered a 10th century Bulgarian royal seal at the fortress site.

The discovery of the relic is significant not only because of its historical importance but also because of its rarity. Only 43 lead tubes from the same time period are known in the entire world, and this relic is one of the most well-preserved and significant examples.

The team of researchers believes that the relic was brought to the fortress site by a pilgrim who had visited the grave of John the Apostle in Ephesus, Turkey. The pilgrim would have believed that the relic had healing properties and would have brought it back to the fortress site to be venerated.

This discovery is not the only significant archaeological find in the region. In 2010, a team of researchers discovered a reliquary containing the relics of St. John the Baptist, which were later confirmed to be authentic by Oxford University scholars.

The area of the Burgos fortress and the Cape of Foros is also famous for being the site of a major battle during the Second Bulgarian Empire. The victory in the Battle of Skafida helped the Second Bulgarian Empire regain most of the region of Thrace from Byzantium.

The discovery of the relic of John the Apostle is a significant reminder of the importance of archaeology in understanding our shared history and cultural heritage. It is a testament to the dedication and hard work of the researchers involved in the project and a reminder of the many secrets that still lie hidden beneath our feet, waiting to be uncovered.
details.ground_truth: Archaeologists in Bulgaria think they've found ashes belonging to one of the 12 apostles. The team found a small lead container with ashes while excavating a basilica that dates back to the sixth century AD, reports Archaeology in Bulgaria. The markings on the inch-long vessel match those at the grave of St. John the Apostle in what is now Turkey, leading researchers to surmise that a long-ago Christian from Bulgaria traveled to the grave site and returned home with the sample of ashes. Such journeys were common around that time, reports Novinite. “Probably a pilgrim from the Foros Peninsula went on a pilgrimage to Ephesus, and came back here with this relic, which was then donated to the basilica on Foros,” says the director of the Burgas Regional Museum of History in Bulgaria. (The ancient basilica is located in what is now the Black Sea city of Burgas.) Archeologists also found a Bulgarian royal seal from the 10th century at the dig site, which includes a fortress, notes the Week. (Another archaeological discovery: an ancient underground city in Turkey.)

**Netflix Introduces Groundbreaking Unlimited Maternity and Paternity Leave Policy**

In a move that is set to revolutionize the way companies approach work-life balance, Netflix, the world's leading streaming entertainment platform, has announced an unlimited maternity and paternity leave policy for all its employees. The policy, which was announced in a blog post by Tawni Cranz, Netflix's Chief Talent Officer, allows new parents to take off as much time as they want during the first year after their child's birth or adoption.

According to Cranz, the policy is designed to give employees the flexibility and confidence to balance the needs of their growing families without worrying about work or finances. Parents can return part-time, full-time, or return and then go back out as needed, and Netflix will continue to pay them normally, eliminating the hassle of switching to state or disability pay.

The policy is a significant departure from the traditional approach to maternity and paternity leave, which often requires employees to take a set amount of time off and then return to work. Netflix's policy, on the other hand, recognizes that the first year after childbirth can be challenging for new parents, and provides them with the support and flexibility they need to navigate this period.

The policy is also notable for its extension to fathers, who are often often left out of these types of policies. By including fathers, Netflix is recognizing that they too play a critical role in childcare and are often often needed to take time off to care for their newborns.

While some experts have noted that unlimited time off policies can have their pitfalls, including the potential for employees to abuse the system or strain workplace relations, Netflix's policy is designed to mitigate these risks by requiring employees to work with their managers and teams ahead of time to ensure that their absence won't jeopardize a project or deadline.

Overall, Netflix's unlimited maternity and paternity leave policy is a groundbreaking step forward in the way companies approach work-life balance. By recognizing that employees are not just workers, but also parents and caregivers, Netflix is providing them with the support and flexibility they need to thrive in their careers and in their personal lives.

**Background**

Netflix's policy is not the first of its kind. In 2013, Yahoo doubled its maternity and paternity leave to make it more competitive with Facebook and Google. However, Netflix's policy is notable for its extension to fathers and its recognition of the challenges of the first year after childbirth.

**Impact**

The impact of Netflix's policy is likely to be significant, both for the company and
details.ground_truth: Expecting parents wondering which company offers the best maternity and paternity leave in the US need look no further. Netflix announced yesterday that it is upping the ante, offering new parents unlimited, fully-paid maternity and paternity leave for the first year after having or adopting a child. The streaming video company based in Los Gatos, Calif., also offers unlimited paid vacation, and it's all toward fostering "a 'freedom and responsibility' culture that gives our employees context about our business and the freedom to make their own decisions along with the accompanying responsibility," writes Tawni Cranz, chief talent officer. The new policy "deserves high marks for extending leave to fathers," reports Fortune, "as well as understanding that the entire first year after childbirth can be challenging." It's a "landmark perk," reports TechCrunch, noting that Yahoo doubled its maternity and paternity leave in 2013 (to 16 paid weeks for moms and eight paid weeks for dads) to compete with packages offered by Facebook and Google because "the talent is growing up." A decade ago big company perks included free lunch and ping pong tables, but now the major moves are about helping employees balance work and home life because "experience shows people perform better at work when they’re not worrying about home," Cranz adds. Of course, employees will have to plan their leave with managers and coworkers in advance to make sure their absence isn't disruptive—behaving, you know, like adults. (See who beat Netflix to the punch.)

**London School of Economics (LSE) Incident**

A disturbing incident has occurred at the LSE, where a Jewish student's nose was broken during a skiing trip to Val d'Isère organized by the students' union. The incident allegedly involved a Nazi-themed drinking game, which included players saluting the Führer and arranging cards in the shape of a swastika. The LSE and the students' union are investigating the incident and have stated that they do not tolerate antisemitism or any other form of racism.

The Jewish Society at the LSE has condemned the incident, with its president, Jay Stoll, stating that Nazi glorification and antisemitism have no place in universities. A Jewish student who suffered the injury has also spoken out, saying that the comments and insults made during the game were the tipping point for her.

**Antisemitism in Universities**

This incident is not an isolated one, with similar reports of antisemitism in universities emerging in recent months. In November, the Daily Telegraph reported claims that members of the Oxford University Conservative Association regularly sang an antisemitic song with Nazi references. The LSE athletics union has also condemned the actions of a small group of individuals involved in the incident, stating that they do not uphold such behavior.

The LSE and other universities must take a strong stance against antisemitism and ensure that their students are safe and respected. This includes providing education and training on diversity and inclusion, as well as taking disciplinary action against those who engage in hate speech or behavior.

**Conclusion**

The recent news updates highlight the importance of promoting diversity, inclusion, and respect in our universities and communities. The LSE incident and other reports of antisemitism demonstrate that there is still much work to be done in this area. By working together, we can create a safer and more inclusive environment for all students and individuals.
details.ground_truth: A Nazi drinking game played by students from the London School of Economics sparked a brawl and ended with a broken nose, reports the Guardian. The row occurred during a ski trip taken by 150 members of the school's athletic union to Val D'Isere in France. The confrontation occurred after a Jewish student objected to the drinking game, according to the school's Jewish Society. The game involved playing cards arranged on a table in the shape of a swastika. Players were required to "salute the führer," according to the student newspaper. "Nazi glorification and anti-semitism have no place in our universities," said the head of the Jewish Society. A video of the drinking game was briefly posted on Facebook. The school is investigating. "These are disturbing allegations relating to events which took place on a foreign trip organized by the students' union," said a school statement, which added that officials are "prepared to take disciplinary action. Students must abide by clear standards of behavior. We do not tolerate anti-semitism or any other form of racism."

**Kansas City Couple Robbed and Shot at in Craigslist Car Sale Deal**

A shocking incident of violence occurred in Kansas City, Missouri, when a couple and their teenage son were robbed and shot at while trying to purchase a car listed on Craigslist. The 53-year-old father, who was shot four times, valiantly tried to shield his wife and son from the gunfire, but his wife's right upper arm was shattered by a single bullet.

According to police reports, the family had gone to an apartment complex in the 5500 block of East 84th Street to look at a 2007 Hyundai Sonata advertised for sale on Craigslist. They test-drove the car with a suspect who identified himself as "AJ," and after completing the test drive, they returned to the apartment complex to discuss purchasing the vehicle. However, two men approached armed with black handguns and demanded money, shooting the father when he stepped in front of his wife. The suspects then grabbed the woman's purse, which contained $3,000, and fled in the Hyundai.

The incident is believed to be connected to an armed robbery hours earlier of a man answering a Craigslist ad for an Xbox. In both cases, the victims were held up while meeting someone to conduct a sale or purchase of items advertised on Craigslist.

**Police Investigation and Reward**

The Kansas City Police Department is investigating the incident and has released four people as persons of interest in the crimes. A couple who live in the Stone Haven Apartments in southeast Kansas City came to the aid of the injured family, pressing down on their wounds until police arrived.

A reward of up to $7,000 is being offered for information leading to the arrest of the suspects. Liberty-based Ferrellgas has made a $5,000 donation to Crime Stoppers, and the injured man's employer has praised him as a hero for his actions.

**Safety Tips for Online Sales**

Police advise that when meeting someone for an online sale, use caution and trust your instincts. Do not go to unknown places, but instead go to a crowded area, a business, or a police station. Do not bring cash, but instead use money orders or cashiers checks.
details.ground_truth: A Kansas City couple who went to check out a car they saw on Craigslist were shot and robbed Wednesday night, USA Today reports. They had wanted to purchase the vehicle for their 16-year-old son, who was with them but not injured. Police say the 53-year-old dad and his son had test-driven a 2007 Hyundai Sonata and were discussing payment when the seller said he had to call his wife and momentarily walked away, according to KCTV. That’s when two other men reportedly approached the family and demanded cash, then started shooting. As the dad tried to keep his wife and son out of the barrage of bullets, he was shot four times in the chest, stomach, and legs, while his 55-year-old wife took a bullet to her upper right arm, which was “shattered,” reports the TV station. The men took the wife's purse, which had $3,000 in cash for the car purchase, say police. Authorities believe the incident may be related to an earlier armed robbery that day in which a Kansas City man says he tried to sell his Xbox and gaming accessories to a man who saw his ad on Craigslist, reports the Kansas City Star. As the potential buyer was examining the wares, two other men reportedly arrived sporting a gun; the three men then lifted the Xbox and other items and ran into nearby woods, police say.

**Data Brokers Exposed: Selling Sensitive Personal Information to Marketers**

A recent Senate Commerce Committee report and a subsequent hearing have shed light on the multibillion-dollar data industry, revealing that companies are selling deeply personal and sensitive information to marketers. The report, which queried nine major data companies and invited testimony from privacy groups, found that these companies own an astonishing number of consumer profiles and are selling information in categories such as rape victims, people with cancer, and those suffering from HIV and AIDS.

The data brokers, including Acxiom, Experian, and Equifax, are collecting and selling information on a vast scale, with some companies offering lists of addresses and names of consumers suffering from specific health conditions, credit scores, and credit history. Equifax, for example, has information on whether a consumer purchased a particular soft drink or shampoo product in the last six months, how many OB/GYN doctor visits they've had within the last 12 months, and the number of whiskey drinks they consumed in the past 30 days.

The World Privacy Forum, a nonprofit nonprofit, also uncovered these lists while investigating how data brokers collect and sell consumer information. The lists include the home addresses of police officers, a mailing list for domestic violence shelters, and a list of people with addictive behaviors towards drug and alcohol.

The existence of these lists highlights the need for increased government regulations, said World Privacy Forum executive director Pam Dixon. Currently, data brokers are required by federal law to maintain the privacy of a consumer's data only if it is used for credit, employment, insurance, or housing. Medical information that data brokers get elsewhere, such as from the purchase of over-the-counter drugs and other health care items, is fair game.

The lists could put people in harm's way, said Dixon. For example, a list of more than 30,000 police officers' home addresses could put the lives of the officers and their families in danger. A list of seniors suffering from dementia could open them up to predatory financial offers.

Linda Woolley, president of the Direct Marketing Association, said in a statement that such lists represent a "tiny minority" of marketing products. However, some data brokers offer ways to opt out, but many have unclear opt-out procedures or none at all. Most consumers have no idea they're on the lists in the first place, said Dixon.

The Senate Commerce Committee investigation into the $156 billion data brokerage industry has led to a call from the Federal Trade Commission for major data brokers to increase transparency into their data practices.
details.ground_truth: Secretive data companies are tracking almost every American's every move online—and compiling and selling disturbingly targeted lists based on that spying, a new Senate Commerce Committee report concludes. That includes lists of rape victims, people suffering from ailments including HIV, AIDS, and dementia, and people with substance abuse problems, a privacy group said in a hearing yesterday, as per CNN. Mailing addresses for police officers and domestic violence shelters—the latter of which are usually protected by law—are also for sale, as are lists based on demographics and economics, including "Ethnic Second-City Strugglers," and "Rural and Barely Making It." The World Privacy Forum urged Congress to act to "remove unsafe, unfair, and overall just deplorable lists from circulation." The Direct Marketing Association released a statement saying that while the lists are occasionally "used to disparage certain groups," they represented a "tiny minority" of marketing products. Overall the committee looked at nine companies, some of which refused to explain how they got their data or who they sold it to, Gawker reports. One company admitted it had sold social security numbers and banking information to an alleged identity theft ring.

**Astronomers Discover Evidence of a Ninth Planet in Our Solar System**

A team of researchers from the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) has announced that they have found new evidence of a giant icy planet lurking in the outer reaches of our solar system, which they are calling "Planet Nine." The planet is estimated to be five to 10 times the mass of the Earth and is thought to be orbiting billions of miles beyond the orbit of Neptune. The researchers, led by Mike Brown and Konstantin Batygin, used mathematical and computer modeling to infer the existence of the planet, which they believe is exerting a gravitational influence on the orbits of smaller, icy objects in the outer solar system.

**The "Pluto Killer" Weighs In**

Mike Brown, the astronomer who was instrumental in the discovery of more than 30 dwarf planets and asteroids at the far reaches of our solar system, has spoken out about the possibility of a ninth planet. Brown, who is known for his role in the demotion of Pluto from planet status in 2006, believes that the new evidence for a ninth planet is compelling and that it would be a significant discovery for the field of astronomy. Brown also notes that the new planet would be much larger than Pluto and would have a highly elliptical orbit, taking it as close as 20 astronomical units (AU) from the Sun and as far as 50 AU from the Sun.

**NASA and the International Astronomical Union Weigh In**

The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) has cautioned that there could be other explanations for the observed motion of the small bodies in the outer solar system, and that the Sagan Rule applies: "extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence." The International Astronomical Union (IAU) has also noted that the new evidence for a ninth planet is intriguing, but that it would need to be confirmed through direct observation before it could be considered a planet.

**The Search for Planet Nine Continues**

The researchers at Caltech are hopeful that their paper will infuse the search for Planet Nine with new energy and that it could be spotted within the next few years. They are using the most powerful telescopes on the planet to search for the object, but it is a challenging task due to its extreme distance from the Sun. The discovery of Planet Nine would be a significant one for the field of astronomy and would help us better understand the formation and evolution of our solar system.

**In Related News**

* A team of scientists
details.ground_truth: Pluto is gonna be PO'd. While the dwarf planet tries to fight its way back into the good graces of Those Who Deem What Counts as a Planet, another icy orb even further out may snatch that designation first. Astronomers at the California Institute of Technology say a giant celestial body "lurking at the end of our solar system," as the Washington Post puts it, may actually be a planet, and they're even already calling it "Planet Nine." In their research published in the Astronomical Journal, the sky-watching scientists—one of whom is known as the "Pluto killer" for his role in getting Pluto demoted—think their find is five to 10 times as massive as Earth, and per the AP, almost as big as Neptune and orbiting billions of miles past that planet's orbit. Michael "Pluto Killer" Brown and Konstantin Batygin haven't seen the supposed planet directly, but say they can infer the "massive perturber" exists by how the orbits of smaller bodies nearby are affected by its gravitational pull, the Post notes. What's interesting is that Brown and Batygin originally set out to disprove the existence of Planet Nine. "We thought their idea was crazy," Brown says of the scientists who originally floated the idea of a large, hidden planet. But as they did their own research, they soon came to their own conclusion in what Brown calls a "jaw-dropping moment" that Planet Nine could be the real deal. Now they're simply hoping more astronomers join in to actually try to spot the alleged planet—and they're not concerned it will face Pluto's fate. "That's not even a question—it's definitely a planet," Brown says. Not everyone's convinced. "I have seen many, many such claims in my career," a planetary scientist at Colorado's Southwest Research Institute tells Nature. "And all of them have been wrong." (The Washington Post caught up with Brown for a Q&A on the latest Pluto-killing endeavor.)

**Libya's Human Crisis: Thousands Missing, Mass Graves, and Brutal Prisons**

The ongoing conflict in Libya has led to a severe human crisis, with thousands of people missing and feared to be held in underground prisons or mass graves. According to rebel leaders, nearly 60,000 Libyans have disappeared since the six-month-long conflict began, with only about 10,000 prisoners accounted for. The situation is particularly dire in the notorious Abu Salim facility in Tripoli, where Abdul Rauf, a 25-year-old rebel fighter, was held for 48 days. He shared a small cell with 70 people, all sleeping on the floor, and told them about the revolution in the streets against Moammar Gaddafi.

Abdul Rauf's family was reunited with him on Sunday, and he described the brutal conditions in the prison, including being beaten and jabbed with electric prods every morning. His story is a testament to the resilience of the Libyan people and the determination of the rebels to bring an end to Gaddafi's regime.

In a separate incident, Muneer Masoud Own, a 33-year-old manual laborer, survived a harrowing ordeal in a warehouse in Salahuddin, Libya. He and his 30-year-old brother were detained by pro-Gaddafi forces and held in a warehouse with about 60 others. Over the next 18 days, the number of detainees grew to about 175, and Own endured horror and deprivation, including no food or water and the stench of human waste.

Just before sunset on the promised day of release, guards opened the warehouse doors, but instead of freedom, Own and the others were met with a grenade and gunfire. Dozens fell dead, and Own managed to escape, but he has not heard from his brother since that day and assumes that he did not survive. The discovery of charred remains and maggots in the area has left many families without answers to their fate.

The situation in Libya is dire, and the international community must come together to support the rebels and bring an end to the human crisis. The stories of Abdul Rauf and Muneer Masoud Own are a testament to the strength and resilience of the Libyan people, and it is our duty to ensure that they are protected and supported in this time of need.
details.ground_truth: Troops loyal to Moammar Gadhafi massacred some 150 detainees last week, according to volunteers who cleared charred bodies from the warehouse that had apparently served as the detainees’ prison. An escaped prisoner told his story to CNN: He and his brother were handcuffed and blindfolded for no apparent reason while out walking earlier this month. They were thrown into the warehouse with 60 others ages 17 to 70. The number inside quickly reached 175 as loyalists continued to imprison people. The detainees were given no food or water for days, said Muneer Own. Finally, the troops said they would set them free—but instead hurled a grenade at them and opened fire. About 25 people were able to escape, Own said; his brother apparently was not one of them. The mass of bodies was discovered when rebels eventually took over the area. Many remain unidentified, part of a growing number of prisoners whose whereabouts remain unknown, notes the Washington Post. Thousands—some of whom have been jailed for decades—could be in underground prisons or mass graves, rebels fear. Click through for one family’s story.

**Yo-Yo Dieting and Heart Health: A Growing Concern**

Recent studies have shed light on the alarming consequences of yo-yo dieting, particularly for individuals with pre-existing coronary heart disease (CHD). Also known as weight cycling or the yo-yo effect, this phenomenon refers to repeated cycles of weight loss and weight gain. Research suggests that this pattern of weight fluctuation may significantly increase the risk of heart attack, stroke, and death in people with CHD.

**A Study of 9,509 Participants**

A study published in the New England Journal of Medicine analyzed the data of 9,509 men and women with CHD, aged between 35 and 75 years. The participants had high cholesterol levels and a history of other heart problems, with around half undergoing intensive cholesterol-lowering therapy. Over a median follow-up period of 4.7 years, the researchers monitored changes in body weight and examined whether these changes were associated with poorer outcomes.

**The Findings**

The study found that individuals who experienced the largest changes in body weight (up to 3.9 kilograms or 8.6 pounds) had a significantly higher risk of:

* 117% more heart attacks
* 124% more deaths
* 136% more strokes
* New-onset diabetes

These findings remained after accounting for participants' average body weight and the presence of common risk factors for heart disease.

**A Growing Concern**

Dr. Sripal Bangalore, the lead study author, emphasizes that the study is observational and cannot prove cause-and-effect. However, he believes that the results warrant further investigation and highlight the need to help Americans keep weight off, rather than having it fluctuate. The study's findings raise concerns about the impact of weight fluctuation on people with CHD, a condition that kills over 370,000 people in the United States each year.

**Prevention and Treatment**

While the study's findings are alarming, there is hope for prevention and treatment. A healthy lifestyle, including a balanced diet and regular exercise, can make a significant impact in reducing the risk of CHD. Additionally, intensive cholesterol-lowering therapy and other medical treatments can help manage the condition.

In conclusion, the study's findings highlight the importance of maintaining a stable weight and adopting a healthy lifestyle to reduce the risk of heart attack, stroke, and death in people with coronary heart disease.
details.ground_truth: Losing weight is one thing, keeping it off quite another. And now researchers are finding that among those with coronary heart disease (CHD)—which the Mayo Clinic reports develops from damaged or diseased blood vessels typically caused by blockage and inflammation—yo-yo dieting may dramatically increase the risk of stroke, heart attack, and death. Reporting in the New England Journal of Medicine, researchers say they followed nearly 10,000 men and women with CHD between the ages of 35 and 75 for about five years, monitoring changes in body weight and other health outcomes. The takeaway? "It's important to lose weight, but this data says you have to keep it off," lead researcher Dr. Sripal Bangalore at the New York University School of Medicine tells the New York Times. The highest weight fluctuation was on average 8.6 pounds over five years, while the smallest body weight changes averaged closer to two pounds, reports Medical News Today. Those who were already overweight or obese at the beginning of the study and who experienced the highest level of weight fluctuation experienced 124% more deaths, 136% more strokes, and 117% more heart attacks, meaning these CHD patients were more than twice as likely to suffer these negative outcomes in just a few years than people with CHD whose weight only fluctuated two pounds. This doesn't pinpoint a cause, as pre-existing heart problems could be the culprit, but researchers hope to study this correlation further. (Yo-yo dieting is bad for women's hearts in general.)

**Odessa Funeral Home Theft: Family Seeks Justice for Stolen Wedding Ring**

A shocking incident has unfolded at the Sunset Memorial Gardens & Funeral Home in Odessa, Texas, where a woman allegedly stole a wedding ring from a deceased person's body. The theft, which occurred on April 8th, has left the family of the deceased, Lois Hicks, in a state of shock and grief.

According to the Odessa Police Department, the suspect is described as a heavy-set white female wearing a brown long sleeve sweater, black sweat pants, and black sandals. She was seen fleeing the scene in a red or maroon 4-door Saturn. The police have released higher-quality video footage of the suspect and are urging anyone with information to come forward.

Lois Hicks' daughter, Vel McKee, spoke out about the incident, saying that she and her sister, Brenda Vaughan, had only been gone for 15 minutes when they discovered that the ring was missing. McKee described the theft as "heartbreaking" and "sickening," and said that she hopes the police catch the suspect soon.

The funeral home's general manager, Bill Vallie, said that the video surveillance showed the unidentified woman parking her car, getting out of it, and entering the building. He also stated that several shots of the vehicle's license plate can be enhanced for close-ups, and that Hicks' family is going to press charges.

Jewelry theft from the dead at funeral homes is not a new phenomenon, but Vallie said that this incident was particularly brazen and disturbing. The Odessa Police Department is currently investigating the incident as a State Jail Felony and is urging anyone with information to contact them at 335-5784 or Odessa Crime Stoppers at 333-8477.

The family of Lois Hicks is seeking justice for the stolen wedding ring, which held great sentimental value to her and her husband, Gayle, who passed away three years ago. The community is coming together to support the family during this difficult time, and the police are working tirelessly to bring the suspect to justice.
details.ground_truth: A Texas woman was caught on surveillance video stealing a wedding ring off the finger of a dead woman Friday, the Odessa American reports. The body of Lois Hicks, 88, was in an open casket at Odessa's Sunset Funeral Home. The woman's family had just left the funeral home after a visitation service when a stranger parked outside, came in, and asked to use the restroom. Instead, the funeral home's manager says, she walked into the room with Hicks' coffin, grabbed the ring off her finger, and left. Hicks' daughter says some of her mother's skin was actually torn off during the theft. "I can't believe someone would be that low," she says. The surveillance video captured the alleged thief's license plate, and the family is pressing charges. The incident is being investigated as a felony, KOSA reports. The suspect is described as a heavy-set white woman who was wearing a brown long-sleeved sweater, black sweat pants, and black sandals at the time; she drove off in a red or maroon four-door Saturn.

**Tragic Death of 3-Year-Old Girl at Hawaii Dentist's Office**

A 3-year-old girl, Finley Puleo Boyle, from Kailua, Hawaii, died in January after suffering a heart attack during a dentist visit in December. According to the medical examiner's autopsy report, the likely cause of death was the sedatives and local anesthesia administered during the procedure.

Finley was undergoing a dental procedure at Island Dentistry for Children, where Dr. Lilly Geyer was the dentist. The report stated that the girl had no signs of underlying heart problems or an allergic reaction to the sedatives and anesthetic she received. Her teeth were also in good shape, with "native dentition in good repair."

The report revealed that Finley was given five different drugs, including Demerol, hydroxyzine, and chlorohydrate, as well as laughing gas and an injection of a local anesthetic, lidocaine with epinephrine. Immediately after the lidocaine injection, the girl became unresponsive and went into cardiopulmonary arrest.

The parents of Finley, Ashley and Evan Boyle, filed a negligence lawsuit against Dr. Geyer and her office while their daughter was in a coma. The lawsuit alleged that the girl was not properly monitored while she was sedated, and that CPR was not administered in a timely manner.

The medical examiner's office ultimately ruled the death an accident, but no criminal charges have been brought against Dr. Geyer. In response to the allegations, Dr. Geyer's attorney called them "unproven" but declined to comment further due to the lawsuit.

The incident has led to a tightening of oversight of dental sedation in Hawaii, with new state rules taking effect in January. The rules aim to improve the safety of dental procedures for young children and others who require sedation.

**Key Takeaways:**

* Finley Puleo Boyle, a 3-year-old girl from Kailua, Hawaii, died in January after suffering a heart attack during a dentist visit in December.
* The medical examiner's autopsy report stated that the likely cause of death was the sedatives and local anesthesia administered during the procedure.
* The report revealed that Finley was given five different drugs, including Demerol, hydroxyzine, and chlorohydrate, as well as laughing gas and an injection of a local anesthetic, lidocaine with epinephrine.
* The parents of Finley filed a negligence lawsuit against Dr. G
details.ground_truth: Drugs are likely to blame for the death of Finley Boyle, the 3-year-old Hawaii girl who died a month after going into cardiac arrest during a root canal at a Kailua dentist's office on Dec. 3, according to the official autopsy report. The report says the combination of sedatives and anesthesia Finley received during the procedure probably caused her death, the AP reports. The report noted that Finley was healthy, and ruled out any signs of an underlying heart condition or an allergic reaction. The medical examiner's office ruled the toddler's death an accident, and no charges have been brought against Dr. Lilly Geyer, KHON reports; however, Finley's family has filed a lawsuit claiming Finley was not monitored for 26 minutes while she was under sedation. Geyer's office has since shut down, but Geyer's attorney calls the Boyle family's allegations "unproven." (Meanwhile, a young mother in Hawaii is in a coma following wisdom teeth surgery.)

**O.J. Simpson's Parole and the Resurgence of a Notorious Figure**

In a recent development, former NFL star and infamous celebrity O.J. Simpson was granted parole by a Nevada parole board after serving nine years of a nine-to-33-year sentence for an armed robbery and kidnapping conviction in 2007. The parole hearing, which garnered wall-to-wall coverage from cable news shows, recalled the "trial of the century" and the many months that transfixed a nation over two decades ago.

Simpson, now 70, appeared before the board and apologized for his role in the crime, stating that he had done his time as well and as respectfully as anyone can. He also claimed that associates misled him during the robbery and then turned on him in court. However, one of those associates, Tom Riccio, disputed Simpson's account, stating that Simpson was the one who orchestrated the robbery and gathered up men with guns.

The parole board members voted unanimously to grant Simpson parole, with board member Tony Corda grading him a "low risk to reoffend." Simpson is expected to be released as early as October and plans to move to his home in Florida.

The resurgence of O.J. Simpson's story in recent years, through the award-winning documentary "O.J.: Made in America" and the FX true-crime drama "The People v. O.J. Simpson," has brought attention to the themes of criminal justice and race, trust in police, celebrity, and domestic violence that remain remarkably resonant in modern culture.

**The Rise and Fall of O.J. Simpson**

A photo gallery of O.J. Simpson's life, from his college football days to his infamous murder trial and subsequent convictions, highlights the highs and lows of his career. Simpson's legal team argued that the nine-to-33-year sentence did not match the crime and was a form of payback for his acquittal in the deaths of his ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald Goldman.

details.ground_truth: OJ Simpson's big moment has arrived. A hearing is underway at which he is trying to convince members of the Nevada Board of Parole that he should go free. "I haven't made any excuses in the nine years that I've been here and I'm not trying to make an excuse now," said the 70-year-old Simpson early in the proceedings, per the AP. Later, he added, "I am sorry that things turned out the way they did. I had no intent to commit a crime." He also appeared to stifle a sob in his appeal to the board. Simpson has been imprisoned since 2008 over an armed robbery and kidnapping case, but he and many legal analysts think that the board will vote to release him. If so, he's expected to be out on Oct. 1. The televised hearing is being livestreamed at various sites, including CNN. Per CNN, one of the first questions posed to Simpson by a board member in regard to the robbery, in which he and other men entered a Vegas hotel room to reclaim sports memorabilia, was, "What were you thinking?" Simpson called it a "big mistake" and emphasized that he wasn't armed. "I would never pull a gun on anybody." He insisted that he learned only afterward that one of the men with him, who was "behind me," brandished a gun. Another board member noted that he hadn't taken an alcohol-abuse program as he promised he would at his last hearing. Other Simpson quotes: "I'm at a point in my life where all I want to do is spend as much time with my children." "I've basically spent a conflict-free life." Daughter Arnelle Simpson said her father is "remorseful," and she wants him to come home so they can "move forward," adding, "It's been hard."

President Donald Trump held a 77-minute press conference on February 16, 2017, in the East Room of the White House. The conference was called to address a week of negative news and to defend his administration's accomplishments. Trump took on the role of media critic, excoriated CNN for its poor ratings, and mocked the BBC as "that's another beauty." He also responded to a question about anti-Semitism, saying to his questioner, "OK, sit down, I understand the rest of your question."

Trump made several claims during the conference that were later fact-checked by the Associated Press. He said he inherited a mess from his predecessor, Barack Obama, but the facts show that Obama inherited a far worse situation when he became president in 2009. Trump also said ISIS has spread like cancer, but the group has been losing ground before he took office. He claimed his Electoral College win was the biggest since Ronald Reagan, but it was not even close. Trump also said the 9th Circuit Court of Appeals has been overturned at a record number, but it is not.

The conference was marked by Trump's combative and defensive tone, as he bullied reporters, dismissed facts, and cracked a few caustic jokes. He blamed the media for his woes, saying they are "dishonest" and "out of control." Trump also made a point of complimenting a softball inquiry about the first lady as "a very nice question" and teased CNN reporter Jim Acosta for having the same last name as his new pick for labor secretary.

Trump's performance was seen as a throwback to the messy, zinger-filled news conferences he held during the early stages of his campaign. It was also seen as a attempt to get the boss his groove back and to re-energize a president whose White House in recent days has been buffeted by crisis and paralyzed by dysfunction.

The conference was widely covered by the media, with many outlets focusing on Trump's attacks on the press and his factually-challenged claims. The conference was also seen as a defining moment in Trump's presidency, as he continued to put claims of press prejudice at the center of his campaign and amplified his chief strategist Stephen Bannon's decree that the media are "the opposition party."

In the aftermath of the conference, Trump's job approval rating was seen as lower than that of past presidents at the same point in their administrations. According to a Pew Research Center survey, 39% of Americans approve of his job performance while 56% disapprove
details.ground_truth: President Trump ripped into the "dishonest" and "out of control" media during his press conference Thursday—and it didn't take long for the media to fire back. Fox News anchor Shepard Smith was among those who criticized the president, saying he "keeps repeating ridiculous throwaway lines that are not true at all and sort of avoiding this issue of Russia as if we're some kind of fools for asking the question," the Hill reports. Smith said Trump "owes it to the American people" to answer legitimate questions liked the ones about Russia that he dismissed as "fake news." But not all the reviews were so negative. A look at coverage: Yes, he was "combative," observes Mara Liasson at NPR, "but he was also funny and charming like he was with the press during all those years in New York as a fixture in the tabloids. ... I think that he will get a lot of credit for doing this. I think it will thrill his supporters." The Washington Post fact-checks what it describes as 15 "dubious claims" from the conference, including Trump's claim that he had the "biggest electoral college win since Reagan." Trump had a total of 304 electoral college votes, which ranks sixth in the eight elections since Reagan's 525-vote 1984 landslide. Only George W. Bush won with fewer than Trump. "I was given that information," Trump said when a reporter challenged him on the statement. The AP takes a close look at Trump's "I inherited a mess line" and decides the claim itself is messy. CNN contributor Jeffrey Lord, reacting to scathing reviews: “Lord, I think we saw two different press conferences," he said, per Raw Story. "From my perspective, I thought he was relaxed, he was funny, he was on point. He took the whole issue of the media, and he had a very candid conversation." Politico has an exhaustive list of the topics Trump covered during the 77-minute presser, ranging from nuclear holocaust to CNN, which he downgraded from "fake news" to "very fake news." BuzzFeed has video of one of the conference's most heavily commented upon moments: when Trump asked a black reporter if she could set up a meeting between him and the Congressional Black Caucus. Presidential historians tell the AP that no president, including Nixon during the Watergate scandal, has publicly turned on the press the way Trump did. "It was bizarre theater," says Rice University history professor Douglas Brinkley. "He turned a presidential press conference into a reality-TV show in which he can be the star and browbeat anyone who objects to him with the power of his office." GOP strategist and CNN contributor Alice Stewart also found a few things to praise in Trump's performance. "You can't say he's not responsive to the press," she tells the Las Vegas Review-Journal. "He answered all the questions from all over the press corps and put to rest the long-standing dialogue that he shuts down certain news outlets." Vox lists nine moments that it suggests back up Jake Tapper's description of the press conference as "wild" and "unhinged," including Trump's claim that "the leaks are real" but "the news is fake." Late-night hosts approached the press conference as gleefully as a kid unwrapping Christmas presents, and the Washington Post rounds up some of their best lines. "My guess is he did it because he's mad and he just wanted to blow off some steam," Jimmy Kimmel said. "The tone of the press conference was like if your dad found a pack of cigarettes under your mattress." David Graham at the Atlantic looks at the continual theme of complaint in the conference and wonders: Does Trump hate his new job?

**International Space Station Faces Cooling System Issue, Potential Spacewalk Looms**

The International Space Station (ISS) has encountered a problem with one of its external cooling loops, which may require a repair spacewalk. NASA officials confirmed that the situation does not pose a life-threatening emergency, but it has forced the crew to prioritize their operations and shut down some non-critical systems.

The issue began on Wednesday when one of the station's two external cooling loops, known as Loop-A, shut down due to a pre-set temperature limit. The crew had to reroute coolant into Loop-B, which resulted in the need to prioritize life support systems, electrical systems, and science experiments, including the freezers that preserve scientific samples. Some non-critical systems were turned off in the NASA Harmony node, Japan's Kibo lab, and Europe's Columbus lab.

Engineers believe that the problem was caused by a malfunctioning flow control valve for the station's ammonia coolant. Mission managers are trying to determine whether a software fix can resolve the issue or if a spacewalk will be required. The decision may take a couple of days or as much as a couple of weeks.

The ISS has experienced similar issues in the past, with spacewalks being performed to replace a leaky coolant pump control box in May and to deal with coolant system maintenance issues in 2012, 2011, 2010, 2009, and 2007. U.S. spacewalks were temporarily suspended after an incident in July, but Russian spacewalks have proceeded without problems.

The current six-person crew on the ISS consists of NASA astronauts Mike Hopkins and Rick Mastracchio, Russians Mikhail Tyurin, Sergey Ryazanskiy, and Oleg Kotov, and Koichi Wakata of Japan. The crew has been living on the ISS since December 2000, and the station is expected to be maintained through 2020, with plans to extend its operating life.

The external cooling loops are systems that circulate ammonia outside the station to keep equipment cool. Officials discovered the malfunctioning loop was producing too much ammonia on Wednesday morning, which led to the shutdown of some non-critical systems. The life support system on the ISS is still up and running, and the current mission of the space station, officially called Expedition 38, is scheduled to go until March 2014.
details.ground_truth: NASA is reporting a glitch with one of the two cooling pumps on the International Space Station, but it says none of the six crew members are in danger, reports CNN. The crew shut down some non-essential electrical systems as a precaution while they figure what went wrong and how to fix it. Best case, it's a software glitch and a relatively easy repair. Otherwise, a spacewalk will be necessary, reports NBC News. It will likely take days or perhaps weeks to sort it all out. "They're fine for the near future," says a NASA spokesman.

**A Decade After Hurricane Katrina: Remembering the Warning that Changed History**

Ten years have passed since the devastating landfall of Hurricane Katrina in Louisiana, but the memory of the storm's destruction and the warning that preceded it remain etched in the minds of those who lived through it. National Weather Service meteorologist Robert Ricks, who was the forecast coordinator for the Slidell, Louisiana, office at the time, played a crucial role in issuing a warning that would go down in history as one of the most dire and effective ever issued by the National Weather Service.

Ricks' warning, which was titled "URGENT... SPECIAL MESSAGE," predicted that "MOST OF THE AREA WILL BE UNINHABITABLE FOR WEEKS. PERHAPS LONGER. AT LEAST ONE HALF OF WELL-CONSTRUCTED HOMES WILL HAVE ROOF AND WALL FAILURE. ALL GABLED ROOFS WILL FAIL. THE VAST MAJORITY OF NATIVE TREES WILL BE SNAPPED OR UPROOTED. OTHERS WILL BE TOTALLY DEFOLIATED." The warning also predicted that "WATER SHORTAGES WILL MAKE HUMAN SUFFERING INCREDIBLE BY MODERN STANDARDS."

Ricks' warning was not just a prediction, but a personal plea to the people of the region to take action and evacuate. He had a deep understanding of the storm's potential, having grown up in the hard-hit Lower Ninth Ward section of New Orleans and having experienced the devastation of previous storms, such as Hurricane Betsy and Camille.

The warning was issued on August 27, 2005, and it was a last-ditch effort to get people to evacuate before the storm made landfall the next day. Unfortunately, many people did not take the warning seriously, and the storm's impact was catastrophic, with widespread flooding, destruction of homes and infrastructure, and a loss of life.

Ricks' warning has been recognized as a model for how to issue effective and urgent warnings during severe weather events. The National Weather Service has since encouraged the use of bold and direct language in its warnings, and meteorologists like Gary Szatkowski, who issued a personal plea during Hurricane Sandy's landfall in 2012, have been hailed as heroes for their efforts.

Today, Ricks is back at work alongside his colleagues, who are still recovering from the storm's impact. He reflects on the warning he issued a decade ago, saying, "I would much rather have been wrong in this one. I would
details.ground_truth: It's one of the most famous weather forecasts of the modern era, and it came out exactly 13 years ago to the day. That would be Aug. 28, 2005, one day before Hurricane Katrina devastated New Orleans. National Weather Service meteorologist Robert Ricks issued a forecast so dire that some news agencies thought it was a hoax, writes meteorologist James Spann in a tweet commemorating the Ricks forecast. As it turns out, Ricks was spot on. "Most of the area will be uninhabitable for weeks…perhaps longer," he wrote. "At least one half of well constructed homes will have roof and wall failure. All gabled roofs will fail…leaving those homes severely damaged or destroyed." And this: "Water shortages will make human suffering incredible by modern standards." (Spann's tweet includes the full text.) The forecast has been credited with saving lives, and it is now in the Smithsonian Museum of American History, as noted by Slate on the 10-year anniversary. Ricks told Brian Williams of NBC News on Sept. 15, 2005, that he went through the warning line by line to verify each doomsday-sounding statement before making it public. He ended up removing nothing. "I would much rather have been wrong in this one," he told Williams. "I would much rather be talking to you and taking the heat and crying wolf. But our local expertise said otherwise. You know, 'Hey, let's gear up for the big one, this is going to be the big one.'" (Read about what JJ Watt's crowdsourced fundraiser accomplished in the wake of Hurricane Harvey.)

**Twitter's Struggle with Harassment and Abuse: A Call for Change**

Twitter, a popular social media platform, has been facing increasing criticism over its handling of harassment and abuse on the site. The latest incident involves Robin Williams' daughter, Zelda Williams, who was bullied off the platform after her father's death. The abuse included messages that blamed her for her father's suicide and pictures of him with bruises around his neck.

Twitter's vice president of trust and safety, Del Harvey, stated that the company will update its policies to better handle tragic situations like this one. However, many users and experts argue that this is not enough. Imani Gandy, a senior legal analyst, shared her own experience of facing constant abuse on the platform due to her race, gender, and medical condition. She has blocked over a thousand accounts and reported them using Twitter's "Report Abuse" form, but to no avail.

Twitter's current system of reporting abuse and blocking accounts is not effective in stopping persistent attackers, who often create new accounts to continue their actions. Soraya Chemaly, a media critic and feminist activist, argues that Twitter's commitment to free expression is clouding its judgment on this issue. She recommends that Twitter take a leaf from a third-party software called "Block Together," which allows users to automatically block new accounts that send them direct "@" replies.

The Internet Archive, a non-profit organization, has been collecting and preserving the internet's history since 1996. They have been receiving daily crawl data from Alexa Internet, which are added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. This data provides a valuable resource for researchers and historians, allowing them to study the evolution of the internet and its impact on society.

In conclusion, Twitter's struggle with harassment and abuse is a pressing issue that requires a more comprehensive solution. While the company's efforts to update its policies are a step in the right direction, they are not enough to address the scale and severity of the problem. It is time for Twitter to take a more proactive approach to protecting its users, including those who are most vulnerable to harassment and abuse.
details.ground_truth: Twitter has promised to take a look at its policies after some sickening abuse over her father's death forced Zelda Williams off the site. After she tweeted a touching tribute to her father, the grieving daughter received messages on Twitter and Instagram blaming her for her father's death, as well as images of her father Photoshopped to show bruises around his neck, Forbes reports. "We will not tolerate abuse of this nature on Twitter," a Twitter exec said in a statement. "We have suspended a number of accounts related to this issue for violating our rules and we are in the process of evaluating how we can further improve our policies to better handle tragic situations like this one." Twitter says the changes will include "expanding our policies regarding self-harm and private information, and improving support for family members of deceased users." An activist who has worked with Facebook to help protect people facing online abuse says the site needs to do more to tackle persistent abusers. "While I am truly sorry for what the Williams family is experiencing during this time, I am concerned that it takes an event like this to bring heightened attention to a problem that so many face every day," she tells the Washington Post. "As is often the case, it is Williams, the target of abuse, and not her abusers, who's left Twitter," she adds.

**Australian Radio DJs Apologize for Prank Call that Led to Nurse's Death**

Two Australian radio DJs, Mel Greig and Michael Christian, have apologized for their prank call to a London hospital where the Duchess of Cambridge, Kate Middleton, was being treated. The call, which was broadcast on their radio show, led to the death of nurse Jacintha Saldanha, who had answered the call and transferred it to a colleague.

The DJs, who were impersonating Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Charles, had called the hospital on December 4 and gained some information about the Duchess's condition, which they subsequently played on air. The hospital's chairman, Simon Glenarthur, slammed the decision to broadcast the call as "truly appalling" and called on the radio station to take steps to ensure that such an incident could never be repeated.

The Australian radio network, Southern Cross Austereo, which owns the 2Day FM station, has suspended all prank calls, pulled advertising, and ordered a comprehensive review of its policies and procedures. The network's CEO, Rhys Holleran, expressed his deep and sincere condolences to the Saldanha family and said that the company would fully cooperate with any investigations into the incident.

The Saldanha family has released a statement asking for privacy and directing questions to the police. She is survived by her husband and two children. The Duke and Duchess of Cambridge have also expressed their sadness at her death and offered their support to the hospital staff and colleagues.

**Background and Investigation**

The police are treating the death as "unexplained" and a postmortem examination will take place on Tuesday. The Metropolitan Police in London have contacted the Australian authorities in relation to the call, but are not discussing what or with whom they are talking.

The Australian Communications and Media Authority (ACMA) has not yet commented on the case, but will be engaging with the licensee, 2Day FM, around the facts and issues surrounding the prank call.

**Public Reaction**

The public reaction to the prank call has been overwhelmingly negative, with many accusing the DJs of having "blood on their hands." The Coles supermarket chain has removed all its advertising from 2Day FM, and the outcry has grown exponentially since the hospital confirmed Saldanha's death.

The incident has sparked a wider debate about the ethics of prank calls and the responsibility of media outlets to protect their sources and the public.
details.ground_truth: Australian DJs Michael Christian and Mel Greig have broken their silence on their royal prank call gone horribly wrong, reports the Guardian, tearfully telling an interviewer that they're "shattered, gutted, heartbroken" over the death of nurse Jacintha Saldanha. "No one could've imagined this to happen," Christian said. "Naturally, we're shattered. We're people, too." "There's not a minute that goes by that I don't think about what (Saldanha's) family is going through, and the thought that we may have contributed to it is gut-wrenching," says Greig. The DJs meant no harm, adds Christian: "At every single point it was innocent on our behalf." Innocent or not, Greig and Christian "will not return to the airwaves until further notice," says parent company Southern Cross Austereo, which has also axed their show, halted all prank calls, and nixed ads amid an internal review, CNN reports. But the station is doubling down on its decision to air the prank, saying that it had tried to contact King Edward VII's Hospital no fewer than five times to talk about the recording before airing it. "It is absolutely true to say that we actually did attempt to contact those people on multiple occasions," says Southern Cross' CEO. It's not clear if the hospital responded, notes the AFP, but it has mounted a withering assault against the station in the aftermath of Saldanha's death.

**Tragic Accident at Fleming Athletic Complex Leaves 12-Year-Old Boy Dead and Two Others Injured**

A devastating incident occurred at the Fleming Athletic Complex in Augusta, Georgia, on Monday, resulting in the death of a 12-year-old boy and leaving two others injured. Melquan Kwame Robinson, a seventh-grader at Morgan Road Middle School, was electrocuted when he touched a fence that had been electrified by a live wire. The incident report states that Melquan climbed over the fence to retrieve a football and was electrocuted when he attempted to come back over.

**Investigations and Condolences**

The Richmond County Coroner's Office, the Richmond County Sheriff's Office, and the city of Augusta are conducting separate investigations into the incident. Georgia Power released a statement stating that they do not control or maintain the voltage that energized the fences at the complex. Mayor Hardie Davis offered his condolences and prayers to the family of Melquan, stating that the city and community should come together to support them during this difficult time.

**Community Reaction and Support**

The community has been left reeling by the tragic loss of a young life. Melquan's former football coach, Don Clark, described him as a future leader, athlete, and amazing child. Clark praised the courage of the two other boys who tried to help Melquan, stating that they are an inspiration to everyone. The Trinity Elite Titans football team issued a statement on Facebook, expressing their sadness and sending their prayers to Melquan's family.

**Vigil and Support for the Family**

A vigil will be held at the Bernie Ward Community Center on Thursday at 6:00 PM to honor Melquan's memory and support his family. The community is encouraged to attend and show their support during this difficult time. The Richmond County school system has provided additional support to Morgan Road Middle School for students and staff.

**Update on the Injured**

The condition of the two boys who were injured in the incident is not available, but one of them, David Sette, is recovering in the hospital and has been moved from the ICU to a regular room. His mother stated that doctors have concerns with his blood levels involving muscle breakdown, which can lead to future problems.

The tragic loss of Melquan Kwame Robinson has left the community in shock, and it is essential that we come together to support his family and honor his memory.
details.ground_truth: A 12-year-old was killed and two other boys were injured after a chain link fence electrified by a live underground wire electrocuted them at a city park in Georgia. During football practice at Fleming park in Augusta on Monday, Melquan Robinson hopped a fence to retrieve a ball, WRDW reports, citing the incident report that says when he "went to come back over the fence, he was electrocuted." The seventh-grader was pronounced dead later at Children's Hospital of Georgia, according to the Augusta Chronicle. Two other boys who tried to help their friend were injured and hospitalized, according to reports. The mother of one of them, David Sette, tells WRDW that her son is improving. Per the Chronicle, an adult also was injured in the incident. "We lost a future leader. We lost a future athlete. We lost a child in our city," Melquan's former coach, acting as a spokesman for the bereaved family, tells the Chronicle. The oldest of three children, Melquan loved his family and was very protective of his siblings, he says. Multiple investigations into the incident are reportedly underway, with city officials looking into how the fence became electrified. Georgia Power, offering "condolences and sympathy," said in a statement that the utility "does not control or maintain the voltage" that caused the electrocution. Melquan's family will hold a vigil Thursday at the Bernie Ward Community Center. "This young man was where he needed to be," his former coach says, per WRDW. "He was doing what he loved." (A medieval re-enactor was impaled in a freak accident.)

In a recent string of incidents, a woman has been accused of impersonating a Target employee and stealing thousands of dollars' worth of iPhones in Fairfax County, Virginia. The suspect, who is not affiliated with the store, allegedly gained access to the stockroom by posing as an employee and used her knowledge of store procedures and the location of the iPhones to carry out the theft.

Things get pretty vague beyond that: He didn't know the method of transport or when the movement was noticed. But Reuters does pick up some alleged details on those fronts, via an unnamed source quoted by South Korea’s Asia Business Daily. Meanwhile, Vladimir Putin on Tuesday gave his take on the situation: North Korea "will eat grass but will not give up the (nuclear) program if they don't feel safe."
