**Tragic Accident: 6-Year-Old Boy Killed in Deputy-Involved Shooting**

A devastating incident occurred in Schertz, Texas, when a 6-year-old boy, Kameron Prescott, was fatally shot in a deputy-involved shooting. The Bexar County Sheriff, Javier Salazar, described the incident as a "tragic accident."

According to the investigation, the incident began when a deputy responded to a report of a stolen vehicle and spotted a suspect, Amanda Lene Jones, 30, who he believed was armed with a weapon. Jones physically threatened the deputy and verbally stated her intention to shoot him. She managed to get away, and deputies did not see her again until a couple of hours later when she turned up at the mobile home park where Kameron lived.

Jones forced her way into Kameron's home, threatened to shoot members of his family, and said she was taking their vehicle. When Jones left the home, the deputies were outside, waiting. Witnesses heard the woman threaten to shoot the law enforcement officers, and the deputies believed she was holding an object in her hand that they thought was a weapon.

Four deputies, identified as John Aguillon, George Herrera, Jesse Arias, and Johnny Longoria, a reservist, fired shots, killing Jones and Kameron. A fifth officer, a K9 officer, did not fire a weapon. The deputies have been placed on administrative leave, and the local district attorney is investigating.

The investigation found a dark-colored pipe with Jones' blood on it near her body, but no weapon was found. The body camera on one of the deputies captured good audio of the incident but was obstructed when the deputy raised his rifle to fire.

Kameron was described as a kind soul, a ball of energy, happy, smart, and could strike up a conversation with anyone. He loved to make everyone laugh and will be missed by all of his classmates and everyone at Wiederstein Elementary School.

The school district's website reported Kameron's death, and the district extended its deepest condolences to Kameron's family, friends, teachers, and classmates. A fund has been established to assist Kameron's family with expenses, and monetary donations can be made at the two Schertz Bank & Trust branches in Schertz or by mail to the Kameron Prescott Memorial Fund.

This tragic incident has left the community stunned, and it is a reminder of the complexities and challenges of law enforcement and the importance of de-escalation techniques and community policing.
details.ground_truth: Sheriff's deputies shot and killed a 6-year-old boy Thursday in Texas, WOAI reports. Bexar County Sheriff Javier Salazar says four deputies opened fire on 30-year-old Amanda Jones, a suspected car thief, after they "cornered" her outside a mobile home occupied by Kameron Prescott and his family in the town of Schertz. One of the bullets went through the mobile home and hit Kameron in the abdomen. According to CNN, Salazar says deputies performed first aid on the boy, but he was pronounced dead at the hospital. Jones was also killed. "The deputies are, of course, understandably shaken up," Salazar says. He says the shooting is under investigation, but in his opinion the boy's death is "a tragic accident." Salazar says one deputy is "adamant" he saw Jones with a gun and that she threatened to shoot him. He says Jones threatened both police and Kameron's family, who didn't know her, just before deputies opened fire. But investigators haven't been able to locate a gun despite the use of a helicopter and dive team. Salazar says they have found a pipe with Jones' blood on it near the scene of the shooting. One deputy at the scene was wearing a body camera, but he blocked it with his rifle during the shooting. Meanwhile, Kameron is being remembered by those who knew him. "Kameron was a ball of energy, happy, smart, and could strike up a conversation with anyone,” school counselor Maria Morales tells KSAT. “He also had a great sense of humor and caring heart."

In June 2023, a Seminole County, Florida, mother, Sarah Markham, was arrested and charged with child neglect after she refused to take her newborn son, Caleb, to the hospital due to her strict vegan diet. Markham wanted to try supplementing breast milk with vegan formula instead of following the doctor's orders.

details.ground_truth: A judge in Florida has allowed a vegan mom who defied a doctor's orders to regain custody of her infant son, reports WFTV. The strange case began in June when police in Casselberry charged Sarah Markham with neglect and took away 2-week-old Caleb. The charges came after a pediatrician diagnosed Caleb as dehydrated and underweight, and told Markham to take him to the hospital immediately, recounts WKMG. Instead, Markham bought soy formula to supplement her breast milk. When she didn't show up at the hospital, police came to her house, and she told them she wanted a second doctor's opinion and stressed that she did not want her son getting formula from animal products. Today, a Seminole County judge granted her custody again with the provision that she provide a nutritionist's report in 10 days. "There's no case, there's no abuse, there's no neglect—there's simply a doctor who has been challenged by a mother, and he didn't like it," says Caleb's grandfather, Bo Markham. The child has been with his grandparents since he was taken out of his mother's care, and now weighs about 17 pounds. "He's still on a soy formula," says Bo Markham. The criminal neglect charges are still pending, though Sarah Markham's attorney—Mark O'Mara, who defended George Zimmerman—says he expects they will be dismissed now. (PETA wants an Indiana sheriff to feed an alleged cannibal a vegan diet.)

**Rising Colon Cancer Rates Among Younger Adults: A Wake-Up Call for the Medical Community**

A recent analysis of data from the United States has revealed a disturbing trend: colon cancer rates are rising among men and women under the age of 50, the age at which guidelines recommend screenings start. According to the study, published in the journal Cancer, one in seven colon cancer patients is under 50, and these younger patients are more likely to have advanced stage cancer, but they live slightly longer without a cancer recurrence because they are treated aggressively.

The study, led by Dr. Samantha Hendren of the University of Michigan, analyzed data from nearly 260,000 patients diagnosed with colon cancer between 1998 and 2011. The researchers found that younger patients were more likely to be diagnosed with advanced cancer and more likely to have surgery than older patients (72% versus 63%). Radiation therapy was also used more often in younger patients than in older patients (53% versus 48%).

Despite the challenges of treating colon cancer in younger patients, the researchers found that they lived a little longer without a cancer recurrence. For patients under 50, about 68% survived five years, while about 67% of the patients 50 and older survived five years.

The findings of this study raise important questions about whether screening for colon cancer should begin at an earlier age. Dr. Hendren noted that "the cancer community needs to prepare for the increasing number of very young colorectal cancer survivors who will need long-term support to cope with the physical and psychological consequences of their disease and treatments."

Experts agree that the reasons for the increase in colon cancer among younger adults are not yet known, but may be related to environmental factors such as smoking, obesity, and physical inactivity. Dr. Andrew Chan of Harvard Medical School noted that "it's not simply a change in diagnoses, it's a very real increase. There may be an effect of our environment that could be contributing to the increase."

In light of these findings, the medical community is being urged to be more vigilant in detecting the warning signs of colon cancer, such as anemia, bowel bleeding, or a blockage in the colon. Dr. Hendren emphasized that "doctors should be on the lookout for these warning signs, and people with a family history of colon cancer and others who are at higher risk should begin screening earlier than the age of 50."

Overall, the rising colon cancer rates among younger adults are a wake-up call for the medical community to re-examine its approach
details.ground_truth: A new study says one in seven US colon cancer patients is younger than 50—raising questions about why more young people seem to be getting the disease and what can be done about it, reports HealthDay via the Philadelphia Inquirer. "Colon cancer has traditionally been thought of as a disease of the elderly," says lead author Samantha Hendren. "This study is really a wake-up call to the medical community that a relatively large number of colon cancers are occurring in people under 50." Using government data on almost 260,000 colon-cancer patients from 1998 to 2011, the study also says younger patients more often have advanced cancer and undergo surgery (72% compared to 63% of patients over 50). The young are also more likely to use radiation therapy (53% to 48%) and have a slight advantage in surviving for five years (68% to 67%). Younger people are more likely to have advanced colon cancer partly because they get tested after noticing symptoms (like colon blockage, bleeding, and anemia), while people over 50 are advised to begin screenings, says Hendren. "Unfortunately, these symptoms are often ignored by the patient or doctor or ascribed to something like hemorrhoids," a scientist adds, per Reuters. Hendren's team conducted the study after noticing a rise in colon cancer among young people, Medical Daily reports, but that spike remains unexplained. Physical inactivity, obesity, smoking, and "an effect in our environment" are all possible causes, a Harvard professor says. More screenings for young people might help, but the yield would be low because under-50s are still less likely to get colon cancer. "This would be a big and costly change," says Hendren, who recommends "a lot of research" be done first. (A new killer is expected to top cancer by 2050.)

**Ancient Human Settlements in Australia Uncovered**

A groundbreaking discovery in southern Australia has revealed a human settlement dating back 49,000 years, challenging the previous understanding of human migration and settlement in the country. The site, known as Warratyi, is located in the Flinders Ranges and contains a treasure trove of artefacts and bones, including the earliest known evidence of human interaction with megafauna.

**The Discovery**

The discovery was made by chance when archaeologist Giles Hamm and local Adnyamathanha elder Clifford Coulthard were surveying the territory. Hamm noticed a rock shelter with a blackened roof, indicating human activity, and the team began excavations. Over the past nine years, they recovered 4,300 artefacts and 200 bone fragments from 16 mammals and one reptile.

**Dating and Significance**

The artefacts and fossil finds have been dated to between 49,000 and 46,000 years ago, making it the oldest known human settlement in the arid interior of Australia. The site pushes back the earliest-known dates on the development of key bone and stone axe technologies and the use of ochre in Australia. It also provides reliably dated evidence of hafted axe technology about 38,000 years ago.

**Megafauna Interaction**

The site contains bones from the extinct giant wombat-like Diprotodon optatum and eggs from an ancient giant bird, suggesting that early humans were interacting with Australia's legendary megafauna. The team believes that the only way these bones and shells got there is because people brought them back to the cave, indicating that they were living in close enough quarters with the megafauna.

**Impact on the Debate**

The discovery has significant implications for the debate over the extinction of megafauna, with some arguing that humans were a major contributor to their extinction. The site also challenges the Eurocentric view that Indigenous Australians were not as innovative as similar populations elsewhere in the world, with the team believing that the development of these fine stone tools and bone technology happened as a local innovation due to a local cultural evolution.

**Controversy and Future Debate**

The discovery has sparked controversy, with some arguing that the dating estimates are rough and up for interpretation. However, the team's methodology is considered to be as good as it gets, and the site is considered a very important and significant find. The debate will likely rage among archaeologists for some time, but the most important thing is that we're finally seeing real evidence of just how widespread
details.ground_truth: "A man getting out of the car to go to the toilet led to the discovery of one of the most important sites in Australian pre-history," archaeologist Giles Hamm tells ABC News. Hamm was surveying a section of Australia's Flinders Ranges when his partner, aboriginal elder Clifford Coulthard, had to go to the bathroom. "Nature called and Cliff walked up this creek bed into this gorge and found this amazing spring surrounded by rock art," Hamm says. At the time, Hamm thought the newly discovered human settlement, a site called Warratyi, was only 5,000 years old. It turns out it's closer to 49,000 years old, and that has huge ramifications for our knowledge of ancient Australian civilization, Sky News reports. Hamm and his team published a study on Warratyi this week in Nature. The discovery of Warratyi in the arid interior of the continent means humans either arrived in Australia up to 10,000 years earlier than previously believed—or they expanded across the continent at a much quicker rate, Science Alert reports. Researchers at the site found 4,300 objects, including tools, and 200 bone fragments from multiple animals, including a 5,500-pound marsupial. The bones help explain how early Australians interacted with megafauna, including hunting them. The tools show Australians were using bone and stone axes earlier than believed and are evidence that early Australians developed some technologies on their own rather than adopting them from other peoples as previously believed. (Ninety rocks in Australia could rank up there with Stonehenge.)

This week, several high-profile celebrities made headlines in the entertainment industry.

Spears' reps argue that the information provided was not a mandate, but rather an aid to make the radio spots move along smoothly.

details.ground_truth: Britney Spears' father, Jamie, has been in charge of her since she was placed under conservatorship in 2008, and he thinks it's about time for a raise, Radar reports. **Gaza Conflict Escalates as Israel Vows to Destroy Hamas Tunnel Network**

The Gaza conflict has entered its 24th day, with both sides suffering heavy losses. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has vowed to destroy the Hamas tunnel network in the Gaza Strip "with or without a cease-fire," as the military called up another 16,000 reservists to pursue its campaign in the densely-populated territory.

**Death Toll Mounts**

The death toll in the conflict has surpassed 1,300 Palestinians, mostly civilians, and 50 Israelis, almost all of them soldiers. The Gaza Health Ministry has reported that 315 children and 166 women have been killed, while 2,307 children and 1,529 women have been wounded.

**International Efforts to End the Conflict Stall**

International efforts to end the conflict have stalled, despite concern over the mounting death toll. The United States has agreed to an Israeli request for several types of ammunition, including 120mm mortar rounds and 40mm ammunition for grenade launchers, from a stockpile worth over $1 billion.

**UN School Shelling Criticized**

The shelling of a UN school in the Jebaliya refugee camp, which killed at least 17 people, has been criticized by the UN Secretary-General and the White House. The Israeli military said it was responding to mortar fire coming from the area of the school.

**Heavy Air and Artillery Attacks**

Thursday marked a third day of particularly heavy Israeli air and artillery attacks, at a time when Egyptian cease-fire efforts appeared to have stalled. Israeli media said late Wednesday that Israel's Security Cabinet decided to press forward with the operation.

**Hamas Conditions for a Cease-Fire**

Hamas has said it will only halt fire once it receives guarantees that a Gaza border blockade by Israel and Egypt will be lifted. Israel says it wants to decimate Hamas' rocket-launching capability, diminish its weapons arsenal, and demolish the tunnels.

**UN Aid Agency Calls for Greater Protection of Civilians**

Pierre Kraehenbuehl, chief of the UN aid agency for Palestinian refugees, has called for greater protection of civilians, especially in Gaza, where 1.7 million people are squeezed into a small coastal territory. His agency has opened 80 of its schools to more than 200,000 Palestinians fleeing the violence.

**Pro-Israel and Pro-Palestinian Rallies**

Pro-Israel and pro-Palestinian rallies have taken place in various cities, including Paris and Tel Aviv. The French Interior Ministry
details.ground_truth: Benjamin Netanyahu today vowed to destroy Hamas' tunnels at all costs, even as Israel called up an additional 16,000 reservists—bringing the total so far to 86,000 troops. "We are determined to complete this mission with or without a ceasefire," he said of the tunnels' destruction, as per Haaretz. "Therefore I will not agree to any offer that does not allow the military to complete this important mission for the security of the people of Israel." He also issued a get-in-line message to two ministers who have been critical of his actions against Hamas, saying "beware of what you're doing." Netanyahu's statement vis a vis the tunnels comes as ceasefire efforts are sputtering anyway, notes the AP, and on the heels of the worst day of casualties thus far in Gaza.

**Snap's Spectacles Now Available for Purchase Online**

Snap, the parent company of the popular social media platform Snapchat, has made its camera-enabled sunglasses, Spectacles, available for purchase online in the United States. The glasses, which allow wearers to take circular videos and post them to their Snapchat accounts, were previously only available through vending machines that popped up in major metro areas or a store in Manhattan.

The online purchase option is a significant shift for the company, which has been trying to prove to investors that Spectacles can earn money for its business, not just be a brand stunt. The company has admitted in its IPO filing that the launch of Spectacles has not generated significant revenue for it, and expects to experience production and operating costs that will exceed the related revenue in the near future.

The online purchase option is expected to make it easier for people to get their hands on a pair of Spectacles. The glasses come in three colors - teal, coral, and black - and retail for $130. The company is currently only shipping in the United States, but the online purchase option is expected to reduce the long lines and high demand that were associated with the vending machine release.

The online purchase option is also expected to reduce the markup that people were willing to pay to get their hands on a pair of Spectacles. Following the vending machine release, plenty of pairs popped up for resale online on eBay, with some pairs selling for as much as $5,000.

The company has also announced that pop-up vending machines around the country will remain a part of its distribution strategy, though they'll be taking a short break. The company has also announced that it will no longer be selling its charging cases and charging cords on Amazon, and will instead be selling them directly on its website.

Overall, the online purchase option for Spectacles is expected to make it easier for people to get their hands on a pair of the camera-enabled sunglasses, and reduce the markup that people were willing to pay to get them.
details.ground_truth: Snapchat found itself with a marketing sensation on its hands late last year when it began selling funky-looking sunglasses called Spectacles. The trick is they allow users to record quick videos and photos and send them directly to their Snapchat accounts, but the catch was they were available only through vending machines that would turn up in random locales or through a now-shuttered pop-up store in Manhattan, notes New York. Now, however, parent company Snap is selling them online at Spectacles.com for $129. Just how crazy did demand get? CNBC notes that the glasses were selling for up to $5,000 on secondary markets, adding that its own reporters stood in line for 18 hours to get them. (There's a limit of six per household for the new online purchases.) Snap has said the glasses haven't been big money-makers, but TechCrunch thinks the new move is designed to show prospective IPO investors that they could be. The vending machines will go on hiatus for awhile, but they're expected to resurface eventually.

**Seven Men Charged in 2014 Hazing Death, Fraternity Sues for Unauthorized Use of Name**

In a recent development, seven men have been charged in connection with the 2014 hazing death of University at Albany student Trevor Duffy. The men are accused of forcing Duffy to drink a 60-ounce bottle of vodka, leading to his death.

The fraternity, Zeta Beta Tau (ZBT), has released a statement denouncing the men and claiming that they were not part of the sanctioned fraternity chapter at the University of Albany. The statement states that the group using the ZBT name is a "rogue group" not authorized to use the name, and that ZBT is retaining counsel to file a lawsuit against the individuals for falsely representing themselves and damaging the fraternity's reputation.

ZBT has also stated that it will take legal action against anyone who uses its intellectual property, including its name, symbols, and materials, without permission. The fraternity, founded in 1898 in New York City as the world's first Jewish fraternity, has chapters on over 90 campuses across North America and has over 130,000 undergraduate and alumni brothers worldwide.

In response to the lawsuit, Attorney Paul DerOhannesian, representing Joseph Angilletta, one of the students accused in connection with Duffy's death, has stated that the lawsuit is "novel" and "different" and that he is not sure why ZBT would take this step. DerOhannesian has also stated that his client is not ready to comment on the new allegations.

The charges against the seven men are a significant development in the case, and the lawsuit by ZBT highlights the ongoing issue of hazing and the unauthorized use of fraternity names and symbols.
details.ground_truth: The seven men accused of the 2014 hazing of a SUNY Albany student that caused his death are already in trouble with the school and the police. Now they've got a new problem on their hands: a lawsuit set to be filed by the fraternity whose name they apparently appropriated. Zeta Beta Tau's national office says the "rogue" group of SUNY Albany students charged in the death of 19-year-old Trevor Duffy used the frat's name without authorization and isn't linked to the actual recognized chapter on campus, NBC News reports. "Zeta Beta Tau is retaining counsel for the purposes of filing suit against these individuals who falsely represented themselves, deceiving the public and damaging the otherwise exceptional reputation of our Fraternity," a fraternity statement reads, per News10. Duffy died after allegedly being made to consume nearly half a gallon of vodka in a case that made headlines; his BAC was seven times the legal limit, notes NBC. The national fraternity says its intellectual property—including its name, the ZBT initials, and other materials—are protected under copyright rules, WRGB reports. That means anyone who falsely claims to be affiliated with the frat is fair game for legal action, a move the attorney for one of the accused says he's baffled by. "It's certainly novel, and I'm not sure why they'd take that step," the lawyer for Joseph Angilletta, one of two men arrested just last week in the case, tells the station. "Clearly one aspect here is they seem to want to distance themselves from this tragic event." (Five Baruch College frat members may face up to 20 years behind bars for allegedly taking part in the "glass ceiling.")

Kathryn Bigelow's latest film, "Detroit," is a powerful and unflinching reenactment of a 1967 police brutality incident in which three unarmed black men were killed by white police officers in Detroit. The film, which premiered in July 1967, is set against the backdrop of the Detroit riots, which left 43 people dead, 1,200 injured, and 7,200 arrested.

The film follows the story of a group of young black men, including Larry Reed (Algee Smith), a rising R&B singer, and his friend Fred Simple (Jacob Latimore), who take refuge in the Algiers Motel, a seedy establishment, after the riots break out. However, the police, led by the maniacal and hotheaded racist, Krauss (Will Poulter), storm the motel, seeking to find the sniper they believe is hiding inside. The officers' brutal and inhuman torture tactics lead to the deaths of two more men, and the film's central antagonist, Krauss, is allowed to return to the streets despite being severely reprimanded for fatally shooting a fleeing looter.

The film's script, written by Mark Boal, commingles real and fictional people, including real-life witnesses Melvin Dismukes, Julie Hysell, and Larry Reed, who are represented on screen by John Boyega, Hannah Murray, and Algee Smith, respectively. The brutal cops in the movie have different names than the officers who were eventually acquitted of the Algiers Motel killings.

The film's director, Kathryn Bigelow, has a reputation for making immersive and unflinching films, and "Detroit" is no exception. The film's early scenes, which depict a police raid on an unlicensed nightclub that sparks a riot, include a few left turns that serve to underscore the absence of trust between the police and the citizens they're sworn to protect and serve. The film's centerpiece, which depicts the police occupation of the Algiers, is harrowing and nauseating, and by the time Anthony Mackie's character agrees to never speak of what he's witnessed, Bigelow and Boal have said all they have to say.

The film's reception has been mixed, with some critics praising its unflinching portrayal of police brutality and others criticizing its lack of nuance and character development. The film has a 74% Tomatometer score, based on the opinions of hundreds of film and television critics.

Overall, "Detroit" is a
details.ground_truth: Kathryn Bigelow's Detroit tells the story of a black singer who takes refuge in a dingy motel to escape the 1967 Motor City riots. Based on real events, the film reunites Bigelow with screenwriter Mark Boal, both of the Oscar-winning film The Hurt Locker. It's got a strong 89% "fresh" rating at Rotten Tomatoes. Here's what critics are saying: "Bigelow drills down into one of American history's most egregious cases of abuse of police power, bringing it to life with visceral detail and slowed-down meticulousness" in what "feels like her timeliest movie yet," writes Ann Hornaday at the Washington Post. It's "daring, sophisticated and unforgettably disturbing." And "in scale, scope and the space it offers for a long-awaited moral reckoning, it's nothing less than monumental." "It's hard to overstate just how visceral and harrowing an experience it is," writes Lindsey Bahr at the AP, calling Detroit "a well-made and evocative film" jam-packed with "stomach-churning horror." She argues there could be more nuance and perspective. But "maybe anger is all you're supposed to feel when you step outside the theater. Maybe not feeling satisfied with Detroit is the point." Chris Klimek says Detroit is "maddeningly imperfect but still honorable," citing "lightly fictionalized" aspects of the otherwise true story that set it up "for maximum outrage." This is "messy work, even when it’s done in good faith," he writes at NPR. He applauds John Boyega, previously of The Force Awakens. Radiating a "Denzel-like calm," he shows "he's a bonafide movie star," Klimek writes. Peter Howell's main gripe is that the film "tries to tell us everything about the circumstances without telling us much about the people." But Detroit still makes for "urgent viewing," he writes at the Toronto Star. Why? "The injustice and anger behind it all feels like current reality, even a half-century on," when "a person's skin can still determine everything from employment opportunities to treatment by police."

**Tesla's Board Confirms Consideration of Elon Musk's Proposal to Take the Company Private**

In a shocking move, Tesla's CEO Elon Musk announced on Twitter that he had secured funding to take the electric carmaker private, valuing the company at over $70 billion. The proposal, which would be the largest buyout in history, has sparked a flurry of questions and concerns from investors, regulators, and experts.

**Tesla's Board Response**

In a statement, several independent directors of Tesla's board confirmed that they had met several times over the past week to discuss Musk's proposal. The statement said that the talks included how being a private company could "better serve Tesla's long-term interests" and addressed the funding for the deal. However, the statement left open the crucial question of whether the board was satisfied that Musk had enough backing to assert "funding secured."

**Funding and Valuation**

Musk's proposal would value the company at $420 per share, which is a 20% premium to the current market price. The deal would be the largest in history, surpassing the purchase of utility TXU Corp in 2007 for $44 billion. However, experts have questioned whether Musk can raise the necessary finance, given that the company is still cash-flow negative and has not made a profit.

**Regulatory Concerns**

The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has inquired about the issue, and experts have raised concerns about the irregularity of Musk's announcement, which was made via Twitter without a simultaneous regulatory filing. The SEC has the power to investigate and potentially take action against Musk for any potential securities law violations.

**Impact on Shareholders**

Musk's proposal would give existing investors the option to retain their shares, but it is unclear what evidence exists to support his claim that funding was "secured." Regulators and experts have questioned whether the deal would still be subject to the disclosure rules of a public company if more than 2,000 investors opt to retain their shares.

**Conclusion**

Tesla's board has confirmed that it will consider Musk's proposal to take the company private, but the deal is shrouded in uncertainty and controversy. The SEC, regulators, and experts have raised concerns about the funding, valuation, and regulatory implications of the deal. As the situation unfolds, investors and shareholders will be watching closely to see whether Musk's proposal becomes a reality.
details.ground_truth: Elon Musk wasn't blowing smoke. The BBC reports on a three-sentence statement issued by six of Tesla's nine board members saying the board had "met several times over the last week" to discuss Musk's proposal to take the company private, which he announced via an unexpected tweet Tuesday. The talks "included discussion as to how being private could better serve Tesla's long-term interests" and the statement ends by saying the board "is taking the appropriate next steps to evaluate this." The three board members who did not sign the statement are Musk, brother Kimbal Musk, and a board member who is on leave, reports the Wall Street Journal. At MarketWatch, Steve Goldstein flags some unresolved points: The statement didn't elaborate on Musk's claim of "funding secured" and raised but didn't answer a question of timing. "'Last week' predates Tesla's filing on Monday of its 10-Q with the Securities and Exchange Commission, which didn't mention such discussions. News of a takeover would certainly be considered material," he writes. Shares of Tesla are currently down about 1%.

In the world of tennis, several top players are facing injuries and setbacks that may impact their participation in upcoming tournaments. Serena Williams, a 13-time Grand Slam champion and current world No. 10, has returned to training after a pulmonary embolism in February and is taking it one day at a time. Her agent, Jill Smoller, stated that Williams will come back to the WTA Tour when she feels strong and healthy, in consultation with her doctors. Meanwhile, world No. 2 Kim Clijsters sustained a severe ankle injury in a freak foot accident and is expected to miss the French Open, which begins on May 22.

Sweeting moved up from No. 93 to No. 71 with the victory and celebrated by jumping into the River Oakes Club pool, a tournament tradition.

In the U.S. Fed Cup team, 18-year-old Christina McHale and 19-year-old Melanie Oudin will be relied upon this weekend in Stuttgart, Germany, in a world group playoff. The Germans are not taking the teenagers lightly, with Andrea Petkovic stating that they are "hungry, they want to prove themselves and they are surely very patriotic." German captain Barbara Rittner added that they are "still very dangerous" and that Oudin and McHale have "nothing to lose here."

**Internet Archive News**

In a separate development, Alexa Internet has been donating their crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996. The data, which flow in every day, are added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. This partnership has helped to preserve a vast amount of internet content and make it available for research and historical purposes.

Overall, these news stories highlight the challenges and triumphs in the world of tennis, as well as the importance of preserving internet content for future generations.
details.ground_truth: Guess who's back? Serena Williams. And she made sure her return to the tennis court Tuesday was a stylish one, the Miami Herald notes, tweeting a picture of herself in a hot pink body suit. "Look who I spotted on the court. Her first day back," Williams wrote. She hadn't played since July thanks to health problems; click here to catch up on what's been going on. Her agent says Williams is "progressing well" and has the OK from doctors to do light cardio, but still has not set an exact date for her return to the WTA Tour.

**The Search for Advanced Alien Civilizations: Harnessing Energy from the Universe**

A recent paper by Dan Hooper, a senior scientist at the Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory, has revived the Kardashev scale, a model proposed by astrophysicist Nikolai Kardashev in 1962 to measure the energy consumption of technologically advanced civilizations. According to Hooper, harvesting energy from distant stars is not only the best way to increase a civilization's available resources but also the only way to prevent the ever-expanding universe from leaving it alone in the vastness of space.

The Kardashev scale consists of three phases: Level I, where a civilization harnesses all the resources of a planet; Level II, where it harnesses all the radiation of a star; and Level III, where it harnesses all the resources of a galaxy. Humans, currently at a 0.7 on the scale, might reach Level II in a few thousand years and Level III in a few hundred thousand to a million years.

To harness energy from stars, advanced civilizations would need to capture and drag them back to their home galaxy. This could be done using a swarm of millions of solar-powered satellites, known as "Dyson spheres." These structures, first proposed by physicist Freeman Dyson in 1960, would collect the energy of a star and beam it back to a nearby planet.

Dyson spheres would likely be made from millions of individual solar-collecting satellites, called "statites," which would hover in space and balance against the pressure of light from the star. Rings of statites would form a cloud around the star, collecting its energy and beaming it back to the home planet.

The search for advanced alien civilizations is an active area of research, with scientists and science fiction authors alike pondering the question of how to lasso a star. While humans may not have the precise instruments yet to detect the unusual light signatures beaming from the depths of the universe, astronomers hope to develop them before our sun becomes another flaming marble in some distant civilization's collection.

**References:**

* Hooper, D. (2023). The inevitability of the encroaching horizon. arXiv.org.
* Stapledon, O. (1937). Star Maker.
* Dyson, F. J. (1960). A method for the determination of the density of a planet. The Astronomical Journal, 65(11), 537-542.
* Kardashev
details.ground_truth: Watch out for stars moving across galaxies or losing power—it could be aliens gathering energy before the lights go out. Dan Hooper, an astronomy and astrophysics professor at the University of Chicago, posted a paper in the preprint journal arXiv.org arguing that aliens in far-off galaxies may be sucking energy from stars to offset risks posed by an expanding universe, LiveScience reports. "The presence of dark energy in our universe is causing space to expand at an accelerating rate," writes Hooper. "As a result, over the next approximately 100 billion years, all stars residing beyond the Local Group [of galaxies including the Milky Way] will fall beyond the cosmic horizon and become not only unobservable, but entirely inaccessible, thus limiting how much energy could one day be extracted from them." Therefore, he argues, an advanced civilization may use something like "Dyson spheres"—an imagined solar-powered satellite dating back to 1930s science-fiction, notes LiveScience—to harvest a star's energy or use that energy to move it closer to home. Hooper hinges his case on the work of Russian astronomer Nikolai Kardashev, who argued in 1962 that technologically advanced civilizations would expand via three steps: harvesting resources of their planet, then the nearest star, then all stars in the galaxy and nearby galaxies. So how to spot energy-grabbing aliens? Massive stars emanate certain light wavelengths, which will appear in galaxies' light signatures if stars are drained energy—that is, when humans have powerful enough instruments to detect it.

**Disneyland Dry Ice Bomb Incident: Employee Arrested and Investigation Ongoing**

A 22-year-old Disneyland employee, Christian Barnes, has been arrested and charged with possession of a destructive device after a dry ice bomb exploded in the Mickey's Toontown section of the theme park on Tuesday evening. The incident forced a brief evacuation of the area, but no one was injured in the blast.

According to Anaheim police, Barnes, who worked as an outdoor vendor selling sodas and water, placed dry ice in a plastic bottle and left it in a trash can, which caused the explosion. Detectives found fragments of a water bottle in the trash can and believe Barnes was responsible for the incident.

Barnes was held on $1 million bail and is cooperating with investigators, who have stated that the incident appears to be isolated and unanticipated. Disneyland officials have also released a statement, saying they are working closely with authorities to investigate the incident.

The dry ice bomb explosion was a rare and unexpected event at a family-friendly theme park like Disneyland, and it has left many visitors shaken and concerned about their safety. However, Anaheim police have stated that it does not appear to be connected to similar dry-ice explosions in Anaheim in recent months.

The incident has also sparked a wider conversation about the importance of safety and vigilance in public spaces, with one visitor, Jonathan Rosario, saying that it reminds us all to do our part to be safe.

The investigation into the incident is ongoing, and Barnes' motive for the action is still unknown.
details.ground_truth: Police have arrested one of Disneyland's own employees after yesterday's dry-ice explosion in the Toontown section of the park, reports the Los Angeles Times. Christian Barnes, a 22-year-old vendor who sold soda and water from a cart, is being held on $1 million bond. Police say he filled a bottle with dry ice and dropped it in a trash can. The ensuing blast caused a lot of noise and chaos, but no injuries or serious damage, reports AP. No word on motive, but police don't think it's related to previous dry-ice bombs in the Anaheim area.

**Former Winnipeg Broadcaster and Journalism Instructor Charged with Bank Robberies**

Steve Vogelsang, a 53-year-old former Winnipeg broadcaster and journalism instructor, has been charged with two counts of robbery in connection with two bank robberies in Medicine Hat, Alberta. Vogelsang, who worked as a news director and sports anchor at CKY-TV (now CTV Winnipeg) from the early 1990s to 2002, and later taught journalism at Red River College from 2002 to 2011, was arrested on Saturday at a hotel in Medicine Hat.

**The Bank Robberies**

According to police, a man walked into a Royal Bank of Canada on October 19 and a Bank of Montreal on October 20, in both cases passing a note to a teller and receiving an undisclosed amount of cash. There were no reports of any weapons being seen or brandished by the accused.

**Financial and Legal Difficulties**

Court documents obtained by CTV News reveal that Vogelsang is facing financial difficulties, including missing several mortgage payments on a home in Winnipeg and borrowing money. He was also charged with sexual assault in 2016, which was stayed in 2017, and a protection order was filed and granted in March 2017. Additionally, he faces three charges of disobeying a court order, laid in August 2017.

**Reaction from Former Students**

Former students of Vogelsang have expressed their shock and disappointment at the allegations. "I'm shocked and upset, in disbelief, as I know many of Steve's former students are," said Matt Preprost in an emailed statement. "Steve was a tough, but fair teacher—one who built his lessons and rapport with us through a mix of fun and humour, always challenging us to step outside our comfort zones and to be better than our last assignment."

**Vogelsang's Personal Life**

Vogelsang and his wife separated in 2015 after 24 years together, and he moved back to Winnipeg. He filed for divorce last summer, but court documents reveal that he allegedly forged his ex-wife's signature and renewed the mortgage on their home in both their names. The home went into foreclosure in April.

Vogelsang is expected to appear in court tomorrow to face the robbery charges.
details.ground_truth: A former well-known Canadian TV sports anchor is in jail after police say he robbed two banks in as many days in the western province of Alberta last week. Police in the small city of Medicine Hat say Steve Vogelsang robbed a Royal Bank of Canada on Thursday, Oct. 19, and then turned around the next day and robbed a Bank of Montreal, CBC reports. He entered both banks without a disguise and after demanding money, left with an undisclosed sum. Police finally arrested Vogelsang at a nearby hotel at around 3am on Saturday. Per CTV Winnipeg News, Vogelsang, 53, was a longtime news director and sports broadcaster at CKY, now CTV Winnipeg. He also taught journalism at Red River College in Winnipeg from 2002 to 2011. Conor Lloyd, a spokesman for the college, says, “I can confirm he is no longer an employee here.” Vogelsang is scheduled to appear in provincial court on Tuesday.

**Oakleigh Nance's Health Battle: A Summary of the News**

Oakleigh Nance, an 11-month-old infant from Oklahoma, is currently fighting for her life in a metro hospital after contracting a "super strain" of E. coli. This bacterial infection is particularly concerning due to its potential to cause cardiac arrest and its resistance to many antibiotics, which Oakleigh has already been exposed to in her previous health issues.

Oakleigh was born premature, weighing only three pounds, and spent a significant amount of time in the NICU. She seemed to be in perfect health for six months before coming down with a bacterial infection. Her family is now hoping she can fight off this disease before an upcoming kidney surgery that could save her life.

The surgery, scheduled for the second week in August, aims to fix Oakleigh's stage 3 kidney reflux, which has led to recurring infections and a 22% functionality of her left kidney. The procedure is expected to be about 4 ½ hours long and will involve cutting back the ureters to prevent reflux and removing a pocket on the outside of the kidney.

In addition to her kidney issues, Oakleigh has also been diagnosed with moderate ASD and pulmonary stenosis, which are concerning when considering her infection and kidney involvement. After the surgery, she will meet with a cardiologist to discuss additional testing for her heart condition.

Oakleigh's family is relying on their faith in God to get them through this challenging time. They are also trying to raise money through a GoFundMe account to cover their medical expenses.

In a separate news article, a pseudomonas infection is explained as a type of bacterial infection caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa. This bacteria is common and can cause minor problems like swimmer's ear and hot tub rash, but for people who are weak or ill, it can cause serious and even deadly infections. The infections are hard to treat because the bacteria can resist many types of antibiotics.

Overall, Oakleigh Nance's health battle is a heart-wrenching and concerning situation, highlighting the importance of proper medical care and the challenges of treating antibiotic-resistant infections.
details.ground_truth: An Oklahoma girl diagnosed with "super" E. coli is facing a life-or-death battle—and worse, she's only 11 months old. "With this strain of E. coli, it can get in her brain, it can get in her spinal cord, basically cause her to go into cardiac arrest," her grandfather, Chris Curtis, tells KFOR. "And we heard that it was a very, very scary situation." Her family says it's not clear how Oakleigh Nance got the infection, which appears resistant to antibiotics, but the CDC is investigating. It all started when Oakleigh was born premature at only 3 pounds and "started off on 4 liters of oxygen," according to a GoFundMe page to help pay her medical bills. "In the NICU, they used to call her Miss Sassy because of her ability to express her displeasure with her circumstances," the page says. Then she went home and was fine for months until a 104-degree temperature and severe urinary tract infection sent her back to the hospital. Since then, she's been diagnosed with moderate ASD and pulmonary stenosis of the heart, stage 3 kidney reflux, and "a dangerous, antibiotic resistant strain of Pseudomonas," says the GoFundMe page. Now doctors are planning a 4 1/2-hour procedure on her kidney, which is functioning at 22%, to possibly prevent other infections. "I know that God is in control of all else, and when I think about what’s going on in her life, I think about my faith in God," says Curtis. "That’s what sustains me and gets me through it." (Read about a brain-eating bacteria that has returned to Louisiana.)

**Portland's Mt. Tabor Reservoir Urine Incident: A Summary of the News**

A recent incident at the Mt. Tabor Reservoir in Portland, Oregon, has sparked a heated debate over the city's decision to dump 38 million gallons of drinking water after a man allegedly urinated in the reservoir. The incident has also raised questions about the city's management of its water resources and the effectiveness of its public health policies.

**The Incident**

According to surveillance video footage, an 18-year-old man, later identified as Dallas Swonger, was seen urinating in the reservoir early on Wednesday morning. However, Swonger has since denied that he urinated in the water, claiming that he instead urinated on the wall next to the reservoir. He and his two friends were given written citations for trespassing and barred from Mt. Tabor for 30 days.

**The City's Response**

The Portland Water Bureau has insisted that Swonger urinated in the water, citing the video footage as evidence. Despite this, the city has decided to dump 38 million gallons of drinking water as a precautionary measure to ensure public health and safety. The decision has been met with criticism from some, who argue that the risk of contamination is low and that the city is wasting a valuable resource.

**Expert Opinions**

Anna Harding, co-director of the School of Biological and Population Health Sciences at Oregon State University, has stated that the risk of contamination from urine is low and that the city's decision to dump the water is unnecessary. She noted that the urine would be highly diluted and that there are no microorganisms present to cause a health risk.

**National and International Attention**

The incident has drawn national and international attention, with many news outlets and social media users mocking the situation and calling it a "joke." However, the city's decision to dump the water has also raised questions about the effectiveness of its public health policies and the management of its water resources.

**Conclusion**

The Mt. Tabor Reservoir urine incident has highlighted the complexities of public health policy and the management of water resources. While the city's decision to dump 38 million gallons of drinking water may have been well-intentioned, it has also raised questions about the effectiveness of its policies and the value of its resources. As the city moves forward, it will be important to consider the long-term implications of its decisions and to prioritize a more sustainable and effective approach to public health and water management.
details.ground_truth: The Portland teen accused of peeing in a city reservoir on Wednesday insists he didn't do it—in a relatively NSFW defense. "Yeah, it's f---ing retarded, dude," 18-year-old Dallas Swonger tells Vocativ in what the site calls an "expletive-filled interview" given as Swonger smoked a Newport cigarette. "I didn't piss in the f---ing water." He explains that he did urinate, after a night of skateboarding at Mount Tabor Park, but he did so against the wall of the reservoir, not into the reservoir. "I was like, 'Dudes I have to piss so bad.' So I just went over to the wall. I leaned up against the wall and pissed on it. Right there on the wall, dude. I don’t know else how to describe it." Officials beg to differ (a Water Bureau official got very specific about it, saying that surveillance video showed Swonger making sure "to get his little wee wee right up to the iron bars"), and thus decided to dump 38 million gallons of water, but Swonger notes that even if his urine had made it into the water, he doesn't see what the big deal is. "Dude, I’ve seen dead birds in there. During the summer time I've see hella dead animals in there. Like dead squirrels and s---. I mean, really, dude?" Vocativ also got amusing quotes from Swonger's mom ("I’m sorry, he just graduated from high school. He’s trying to get his stuff together") and one of the friends skateboarding with him that night ("He just doesn’t make the best decisions. Honestly, he has the potential to do really good"). Possible criminal charges are still pending, the Oregonian notes. The paper also reports that the water tested clean of urine-related toxins, but all 38 million gallons will still be dumped.

**North and South Korea Unite at the Winter Olympics**

In a historic breakthrough, North and South Korea have agreed to march together under a unified flag at the Winter Olympics opening ceremony in Pyeongchang, South Korea. The two countries have also formed a joint North and South Korean women's ice hockey team for the Games, which begin early next month.

The unification ministry announced a range of joint activities between the countries for the Games, including joint training for skiers at a resort in North Korea and a cultural event at Mount Kumgang. North Korea will also send around 230 supporters to cheer on its athletes, and a smaller delegation of North Korean athletes and supporters will attend the Paralympics.

The Korean Unification Flag features a blue silhouette of the peninsula and outlying islands, and has been used in rare shows of unity before, including at the 1991 World Table Tennis Championships and the 2006 Winter Games in Turin, Italy.

The International Olympics Committee (IOC) will need to approve the countries' agreements, and those that affect competition, such as the joint hockey team, could be more complicated than the ceremonial proposals. The IOC has received a number of "interesting proposals" and will discuss them with delegates from both countries in Switzerland on Saturday.

The diplomatic thaw between the two Koreas comes after years of broken communications, and while the two sides have earned praise for ratcheting down military tensions in recent weeks, some of Seoul's allies have voiced concern that Pyongyang may be using the talks to buy time to pursue its weapons program.

The US military has moved more firepower to the region, and Secretary of State Rex Tillerson warned of complacency at a Tuesday summit in Vancouver, where the top diplomats from the United States, Japan, Canada, and the United Kingdom were in town to discuss North Korea. Japanese Foreign Minister Taro Kono called on the international community to be clear-eyed about North Korea's motivations for participating in the talks.

China has denounced the Vancouver talks, and the United Nations Command, under whose framework they are being held, as demonstrating "Cold War mentality." North Korea has refused to give up development of nuclear missiles capable of hitting the United States, despite increasingly severe U.N. sanctions.

The joint ice hockey team has been met with resistance from some South Korean players, who are concerned about competing with their neighbors. More than 100 petitions have been sent to South Korea's presidential Blue House website opposing a joint Olympics team, with the most popular one garnering over 11,000 signatures.

details.ground_truth: North and South Korea will compete together under one unified flag at next month's Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang, in what will be the first such arrangement since 2006. South Korea previously announced North Korea would send officials, athletes, and a cheer squad to the Olympics following talks between the two countries. It has now revealed North Korea's intention to send a 550-member delegation. Among those arriving in South Korea beginning on Jan. 25 will be female ice hockey players joining a team of South Korean athletes (they'll compete under a flag showing the Korean Peninsula in blue on a white background), as well as 230 cheerleaders, 140 orchestra members, and 30 people who will take part in a taekwondo demonstration, report Sky News and CNN. Ahead of the Olympics, skiers from both countries will also train together in North Korea, per CNN. The thawing of relations between the North and South has drawn praise from some. But others, like Japanese Foreign Minister Taro Kono, fear Kim Jong Un only hopes to ease international pressure while continuing to develop a nuclear weapons arsenal. "It is not the time to ease pressure, or to reward North Korea," Kono said Tuesday in Vancouver, Canada, where 20 countries met and agreed to consider tougher sanctions on the North, per Reuters. Warning countries not to be fooled by its "charm offensive," Kono added, "The fact that North Korea is engaging in dialogue could be interpreted as proof that the sanctions are working." China avoided the meeting, saying it showed a "Cold War mentality."

**Breaking News: Man Jumps White House Fence, Apprehended by Secret Service**

On Tuesday, a 41-year-old homeless man, identified as James Dirk Crudup, jumped the White House fence and was apprehended by uniformed Secret Service officers. The incident was broadcast live on CNN's "John King USA" program, which was produced from the North Lawn of the White House.

According to the Secret Service, Crudup will be charged with unlawful entry and contempt of court. The contempt charge is in connection with an order requiring him to stay away from the White House due to past incidents. This is not the first time someone has attempted to breach the White House security. In an unusual twist, a 6-year-old girl reached the lawn on Sunday night by slipping between the black metal pickets, but was escorted out by the Secret Service.

The incident ended shortly after 9 p.m. ET when authorities issued an all-clear directive at the White House. There was no immediate information on whether the intruder had represented a security threat. A backpack that apparently belonged to Crudup was being examined by security officers as a precautionary measure.

The president's whereabouts at the time of the incident were not immediately known, but the last event on his official schedule was set for 4:30 p.m. The Secret Service officers who apprehended Crudup tackled him immediately, and he was in the custody of two uniformed officers.
details.ground_truth: A homeless man was arrested after hopping the White House fence Tuesday night in an incident captured by CNN's John King USA program, which was filming from the North Lawn. The man was quickly taken into custody by Secret Service agents who approached him with guns drawn. Security officers locked the area down after finding a backpack that had been thrown over the fence nearby. The 41-year-old intruder was charged with unlawful entry and contempt of court for breaking a judicial order requiring him to stay away from the White House. It's not uncommon for the White House fence to be breached, notes the Washington Post: A 6-year-old girl made it onto the White House lawn over the weekend by slipping through the fence instead of going over it. Secret Service agents returned her to her parents.

**The Evolution of The Daily Show: An Oral History**

The Daily Show, a satirical news program, has undergone a significant transformation since its inception in 1996. The show's early years, under the hosting of Craig Kilborn, focused on creating humorous, often absurd, news headlines. However, when Jon Stewart took over as host in 1999, the show's tone and direction shifted.

Stewart's arrival was met with resistance from the existing writing staff, who felt that their style and approach were being threatened. Stewart, however, saw an opportunity to create a more pointed, purposeful, and intentional show that tackled the issues of the day. He brought in new talent, including Ben Karlin as head writer and David Javerbaum as a writer, to help shape the show's direction.

The field department, led by Stewart Bailey, played a crucial role in shaping the show's tone and point of view. The correspondents, including Steve Carell, Mo Rocca, and Nancy Walls Carell, developed their characters and began to tackle more serious issues, such as politics and the media.

A breakthrough moment came in December 1999, when Steve Carell asked a question to Senator John McCain on his bus, the Straight Talk Express. The question, which was pulled from a Time article, was a clever and insightful one that threw McCain off guard. The moment was a testament to the show's ability to add a level of insight to the process that doesn't exist.

The show's focus on deconstructing the process, rather than just making jokes, came to the fore in the 2000 campaign. Stewart realized that the meeting before a public event was often the most interesting part, and the show began to focus on this aspect.

Throughout its evolution, The Daily Show has remained true to its core mission of satirizing the news and the media. The show's ability to tackle serious issues and add a level of insight to the process has made it a beloved and respected part of the media landscape.

**Key Players:**

* Jon Stewart: Host and driving force behind the show's transformation
* Ben Karlin: Head writer and brought in new talent to shape the show's direction
* David Javerbaum: Writer and brought a unique perspective to the show
* Steve Carell: Correspondent and developed his character, tackling more serious issues
* Mo Rocca: Correspondent and developed his character, tackling more serious issues
* Nancy Walls Carell: Correspondent and developed her character, tackling
details.ground_truth: Jon Stewart took over as host of the Daily Show in January 1999, but when did the program truly start becoming the Daily Show that legions of fans would come to love? Try December of that year, when Steve Carell boarded John McCain's campaign bus "and changed the entire trajectory" of the show, writes Chris Smith at Vanity Fair. The article is a fascinating oral history of the show's beginning that includes interviews with Stewart, Carell, Stephen Colbert, and pretty much everyone involved. The first year was rough for Stewart as he fought with the holdover writing staff from previous host Craig Kilborn and sought to put his stamp on the show. Things came together in that Carell interview, in which he peppers McCain with softball questions, then springs a policy zinger. After a few seconds of awkward silence, Carell says, "I was just kidding! I don’t even know what that means!" And the tense moment subsides. (See the clip.) Carell: "It was making fun of a gotcha moment. And I think that a lot of what we do on The Daily Show is making fun of journalistic tropes, and I think that was one of them." Head writer Ben Karlin: "I remember seeing it in the editing room. I remember Jon called me down, and seeing it and thinking, Yeah, this is what we should be doing. This is the goal. It was one of Carell’s most incredible moments. He asks McCain a question in a way that no journalists were talking to the candidates. And it was like, Oh s---, we are able, in this weird, unintentional way, to add a level of insight to the process that doesn’t exist. That was really, really exciting." Click for the full piece, in which Stewart talks about how that 2000 campaign provided his "aha moment" of how to present the show—by "deconstructing the process."

**Quick-Thinking 911 Dispatcher Saves Woman Trapped in Trunk of a Car**

In a remarkable display of quick thinking and resourcefulness, a 911 dispatcher in Johnston County, North Carolina, helped save a woman who was trapped in the trunk of a car. The incident occurred on January 14, 2017, when a 29-year-old woman called 911, saying she had been kidnapped by her jealous boyfriend and locked in the trunk of a car.

The 911 dispatcher, Tim Medlin, responded to the call and tried to communicate with the woman, but the call was dropped, and multiple attempts to call her back were unsuccessful. Medlin then had the idea to use the office's emergency cellphone to text the woman, which she responded to, providing Medlin with the make and color of the vehicle and her location.

With the help of a cellphone provider, Medlin was able to locate the phone, and the woman's location. Fayetteville police spotted the car and rescued the woman, arresting her boyfriend, Nicholas Mattevi, on charges of false imprisonment.

The incident highlights the importance of technology in emergency situations and the need for 911 centers to be able to initiate texts from their systems. Jason Barbour, Johnston County's 911 director, said that all 911 centers can receive text messages but cannot initiate them, and that this needs to change.

Medlin's quick thinking and resourcefulness saved the woman's life, and his actions have been praised by his colleagues and the public. The incident has also led to a change in the way that 911 centers communicate with the public, with Medlin's boss, Jason Barbour, saying that he will buy a smartphone for the team so that they can have a keyboard and communicate more effectively in the future.

**Key Facts:**

* Date: January 14, 2017
* Location: Johnston County, North Carolina
* Victim: 29-year-old woman
* Perpetrator: Nicholas Mattevi, boyfriend of the victim
* Charges: False imprisonment
* Outcome: Woman rescued, perpetrator arrested
* Technology: 911 dispatcher used emergency cellphone to text the woman, who provided her location and the make and color of the vehicle.
details.ground_truth: A North Carolina woman allegedly locked in the trunk of a moving car by her boyfriend was saved by a quick-thinking 911 dispatcher and a lowly flip phone, ABC News reports. The 29-year-old woman called 911 around 4am Jan. 14. The woman had gotten in a fight with her boyfriend, who was "jealous," according to WRAL. Before dispatcher Tim Medlin could get much information from the woman, the call was dropped. He was unable to get her to answer when he called back. Then Medlin remembered a flip phone the 911 communications center keeps around in case the phone lines go down, WTVD reports. He says texting was the only way he could think to contact her without getting "her in trouble." The woman texted Medlin back, letting the dispatcher know she was in a red Mustang headed toward Fayetteville. Verizon provided the location of the woman's phone, and police stopped her and her boyfriend, Nicholas Mattevi, in Fayetteville. Mattevi, 32, was arrested and charged with false imprisonment. "I can go to bed," Medlin recalls feeling upon hearing of the arrest. "We've done our job." He says he's planning to buy a smartphone for the office to replace the old flip phone and make it easier to text if there's a next time. Medlin's boss tells ABC the dispatcher "really stood by our motto: Failure is not an option." (This 911 dispatcher was accused of hanging up on callers.)

details.ground_truth: Police in Stanislaus County, Calif., say a 20-year veteran of the force was "executed" early Sunday after investigating a stolen van and a suspicious person outside Modesto. Sheriff's Deputy Dennis Wallace was shot twice in the head. Police say a suspect was arrested more than 150 miles away later in the day after allegedly carrying out a carjacking, an armed robbery at a convenience store, and an attempted purse-snatching, the Los Angeles Times reports. Lauren Bacall, the legendary movie star, left behind an estimated $26.6 million estate after her passing at the age of 89. The actress, known for her iconic roles in films like "To Have and Have Not" and "The Big Sleep," made sure to provide for her beloved dog, Sophie, a Papillon, by bequeathing $10,000 to her son, Sam Robards, to cover the canine's costs.

Bacall's will, signed last fall, also remembered two of her employees, Ilsa Hernandez and Maria Santos, with $15,000 and $20,000 respectively. The rest of her sizable estate, including her apartment in the Dakota building on the Upper West Side and its contents, is to be split evenly among her three children: Leslie Bogart, Stephen Humphrey Bogart, and Sam Robards.

The actress, who won two Tony Awards, a Golden Globe, and an honorary Oscar over her eight-decade career, also directed that money from a trust left to her by her late husband, Humphrey Bogart, be divvied up among the three children. She also passed on the rights to her likeness and her movie and book royalties to her kids, and named them the executors of her estate.

Bacall's will also included a personal request: she asked her children to respect her wish to keep private certain personal letters, writings, diaries, and other papers or memorabilia. The family plans to auction off her artwork this fall, and the estate documents were filed in a rush order at Manhattan Surrogate's Court.

In a touching gesture, Bacall also left gifts of $250,000 to each of her grandsons, Calvin Robards and Sebastian Robards, to be used for college, with access to the rest of the cash when they turn 30. Hernandez, a Honduran immigrant who worked as the actress's maid for 14 years, was shocked and grateful by the news of her $15,000 bequest.

Bacall's life was marked by her iconic love affair with Humphrey Bogart, as well as her relationships with other legends, including Frank Sinatra and Jason Robards. She wanted to be remembered not only for her first famous husband, but also for her second, lesser-known spouse.
details.ground_truth: Papillion Sophie has lived a pampered life, but that doesn't have to end now that her famous owner—Hollywood legend Lauren Bacall—has died. According to estate papers filed last week, Bacall left $10,000 to her son Sam Robards for Sophie's care and upkeep, Page Six reports. This bequeath isn't a surprise, considering Bacall called herself a "dog yearner" in a 2008 interview, Us reports. "I didn't have a dog growing up in the city with a working mother. As an only child, I yearned for someone to talk to," she said. She gave Sophie quite a glitzy life attending book signings and film festivals. Bacall didn't ignore her human family: The New York Daily News reports her $26.6 million estate was mostly split among her three children; her grandkids reportedly got $250,000 each. And two of her employees also received $20,000 and $15,000, respectively. Considering other famous four-legged heirs, Sophie's 10 grand isn't shocking: Leona Helmsley left $12 million to her Maltese and Alexander McQueen left $82,000 to his three dogs. But the oddest may be Dusty Springfield, who gave orders that her cat be serenaded with her own music and "marry" another cat.

The Republican presidential primary season continues to heat up, with several key developments emerging in the past few days. In the CPAC presidential straw poll, Mitt Romney emerged victorious with 38% of the vote, followed by Rick Santorum with 31%. However, Santorum has questioned the legitimacy of the poll, suggesting that Romney's campaign may have "bought" tickets for supporters to attend the event. Romney's campaign has rejected these claims, with spokeswoman Andrea Saul accusing Santorum of making "statements that aren’t grounded in the truth."

Meanwhile, Ron Paul has expressed his skepticism about Romney's self-description as a "severe conservative," suggesting that he needs to provide a clear definition of what this means.

**The White House and Birth Control Controversy**

In a bid to address the controversy over birth control and Catholic institutions, the White House has announced a new "accommodation" that would require insurance companies to cover the cost of birth control for church-run schools and hospitals. However, this plan has been met with skepticism by some Catholic leaders, who argue that it does not go far enough in addressing their concerns.

White House chief of staff Jack Lew has defended the plan, suggesting that it is a "consistent" position for the administration and that it is as far as they will go in addressing the issue. Lew has also suggested that health insurers will actually benefit from covering birth control costs, as it reduces the number of pregnancies and associated healthcare costs.

**Key Takeaways**

* Mitt Romney emerges victorious in the CPAC presidential straw poll, but Rick Santorum questions the legitimacy of the poll.
* Ron Paul expresses his skepticism about Romney's self-description as a "severe conservative."
* The White House announces a new "accommodation" on birth control and Catholic institutions, but it is met with skepticism by some Catholic leaders.
* White House chief of staff Jack Lew defends the plan, suggesting that it is a "consistent" position for the administration.
details.ground_truth: Fresh off his loss to Mitt Romney at CPAC, Rick Santorum hit the talk shows this morning, and he's downplaying the victory, reports Politico: "You have to talk to the Romney campaign and how many tickets they bought—we've heard all sorts of things," Santorum told CNN. "Ron Paul has won those (straw polls) because he trucks in a lot of people, pays for their ticket, and they come in and vote. I don't try to rig straw polls." Elsewhere on the Sunday dial, as per Politico: Santorum on his chances: "I think we can do well (in Michigan). I think we can do reasonably well in Arizona, and really make this a two-person race." Sarah Palin on Romney: He's "a great candidate," but "his idea of conservatism is evolving, and I base this on a pretty moderate past that he has had, even in some cases a liberal past. Now that’s a problem. He’s still in the 30 percentile mark ... because we are not convinced." Obama chief of staff Jack Lew on the birth control ruckus: The Friday "accommodation" is as far as the White House will go. "There are others who don't have the same objective, they will have to speak for themselves, (but) this is our plan." Ron Paul on 'severe conservative' Romney: "That was the first time I’ve heard that definition, so I guess Mitt will have to tell us exactly what it means. Obviously, he means he’s a serious conservative, and he was trying to defend himself, or portray himself as such, but I don’t know exactly what he was meaning by that."

**EPA Fines Magnolia Homes $40,000 for Lead-Based Paint Violations**

Chip and Joanna Gaines, the popular HGTV couple behind the show "Fixer Upper," have agreed to pay a $40,000 fine to the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) for allegedly violating lead-based paint regulations during their home renovations.

The EPA's investigation, which was sparked by the couple's popular TV show, found that Magnolia Homes, the company behind the Gaineses, failed to take adequate precautions to protect against lead-based paint in 33 different homes. The agency said that the company did not obtain the necessary certification, assign a certified renovator, provide homeowners with an approved pamphlet, and post signs to warn people of the work area.

As part of the settlement, the Gaineses have agreed to take steps to ensure compliance with lead-based paint regulations in future renovation projects. They will also produce and release an educational video highlighting the dangers of lead-based paint within 90 days. Additionally, the couple has agreed to spend $160,000 on a lead-abatement project in their hometown of Waco, Texas, which will reduce the risk of exposure to lead-based paint in at-risk residences.

The EPA's enforcement of the lead-based paint regulations is aimed at protecting children and others who are vulnerable to exposure to lead dust that can cause lead poisoning. According to the EPA, an estimated 37.1 million homes in the United States have lead-based paint, and an estimated 34.4 million of these homes were built before 1978.

The fine of $40,000 is a fraction of the maximum possible fine of $556,000, but it still serves as a reminder of the importance of following safety regulations and taking precautions to protect against lead-based paint.

details.ground_truth: Fixer Upper stars Chip and Joanna Gaines are having to pay up for EPA violations seen on their hit HGTV show. The couple's business, Magnolia Homes, has agreed to pay a $40,000 fine after the EPA determined 33 of its home renovations were "conducted without adequate lead paint protections," reports Deadline, which notes the potential fine the company faced was as high as $556,000. The EPA notes Fixer Upper footage "reviewed by EPA did not depict the lead-safe work practices normally required" though "Magnolia took immediate steps to ensure compliance" when it was notified. Chip Gaines, who in March tweeted advice about engaging a professional to check for lead paint when buying or remodeling a home, will also star in a Magnolia-paid video on lead paint safety as part of the settlement, per Vulture. (The couple are expecting their fifth child.)
